plan.reasoning: Round 1 should not jump directly to a speculative global polar. The hypothesis is so restrictive that we first need a clean reduction to simple modular matroids and an honest account of loops/parallel classes. In parallel, we should construct the codimension-one polarity explicitly, because any full anti-involution must restrict to this point-hyperplane layer; if incidence symmetry holds, this likely gives a complete proof in rank at most 3 and gives the blueprint for higher rank.
plan.strategic_note: Verified the key external facts: opposite flat lattice forces modularity after simplification; modular simple matroids decompose into rank-one/free components and finite projective geometries; and for modular M, Dr1_s(M) is canonically modeled by Trop(M) via elementary quotients/linear subclasses. This round assigns one solver to make the structural reduction precise, and one solver to build/test the codimension-one polarity and rank-3 case.

new_proven_results: Literature fact to use: Fu--Tang--Wang, Adjoints of Matroids, Proposition 4.1: for simple M, M is modular iff it has an adjoint adM with L(adM) isomorphic to L(M)^op.
Literature fact to use: Fu--Tang--Wang, Theorem 4.3 and Proposition 4.4: modular simple matroids have unique adjoints and every connected component is either U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.

Literature fact to use: Wang, Lorentzian polynomials and tropical incidence, Proposition 4.14(3): if M is modular and W is its adjoint, then L(M) ≅ Qt^c_1(M) ≅ L(W)^op and in particular Dr^s_1(M) ≅ Trop(M).
Literature fact to use: Wang, Theorem 4.7: Dr^s_1(M)^o is the order-complex fan of chains of linear subclasses of M.
new_bottlenecks: Need to know whether Dr(M) is genuinely unchanged by simplification, including loops and parallel classes, or only after imposing coordinate equalities.
Need incidence symmetry for the codimension-one polarity: p lies in alpha(q) iff q lies in alpha(p). Without this, even rank 3 may fail.
kb_updates.frontier: Prove the modular/simplification reduction and construct the explicit codimension-one polarity on cones; then decide whether rank <= 3 is solved and what extra lemma is needed for higher-rank polar duality.

task_assignments.description: Structural reduction to modular matroids and simplification issues
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver A. Work on the following problem: Let M be a finite matroid and Trop(M) its tropical linear space. Dr(M) is the poset of tropical linear subspaces of Trop(M), ordered by inclusion. Suppose the flat lattice L(M) admits an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp. There is a natural inclusion L(M)^op ⊂ Dr(M) by F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}). We want to know whether there is an order-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) extending F ↦ F^perp.

Your task is the mandatory structural reduction. You may use the following literature facts as black boxes: (A1) For a simple matroid S, S is modular iff S has an adjoint adS such that L(adS) ≅ L(S)^op. (A2) A modular simple matroid has a unique adjoint up to isomorphism and L(adS) ≅ L(S)^op. (A3) A simple matroid is modular iff every connected component is either U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.

Please prove or carefully analyze the following, with full details:
1. Let si(M) be the simplification of M after removing loops and identifying parallel classes. Show that an order-reversing involution of L(M) induces an order-reversing involution of L(si(M)), and hence si(M) is modular by (A1). Be precise about the bottom flat when M has loops.
2. Deduce the component decomposition of si(M), and describe how the given involution permutes or dualizes components. In particular, track atoms and coatoms and explain what constraints exist on paired components.
3. Analyze the relationship between Dr(M) and Dr(si(M)). There is an obvious coordinate map: loops force coordinates to infinity and parallel elements force equal coordinates in Trop(M). Try to prove that tropical linear subspaces of Trop(M) are equivalent to tropical linear subspaces of Trop(si(M)) under this coordinate diagonal embedding. If this is not literally true, give the precise correction or the smallest counterexample. You should discuss valuated matroid quotients: if Trop(theta) ⊂ Trop(M), do loops/parallel classes of M remain loops/parallel with zero offset in theta?
4. Verify that under whatever simplification equivalence you establish, the embedded flat subposet F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) corresponds to the same construction for si(M), and the prescribed flat-lattice involution is preserved.
5. State the cleanest reduced version of the original question that remains after your work. Important: do not claim that direct sums make Dr factor componentwise unless you prove it; quotients of a direct sum can mix components, so just record the structural constraints.

Suppose the flat lattice L(M) admits an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp. There is a natural inclusion L(M)^op ⊂ Dr(M) by F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}). Your task is the mandatory structural reduction. You may use the following literature facts as black boxes: (A1) For a simple matroid S, S is modular iff S has an adjoint adS such that L(adS) ≅ L(S)^op. (A2) A modular simple matroid has a unique adjoint up to isomorphism and L(adS) ≅ L(S)^op. (A3) A simple matroid is modular iff every connected component is either U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.

Please prove or carefully analyze the following, with full details:
1. Let si(M) be the simplification of M after removing loops and identifying parallel classes. Show that an order-reversing involution of L(M) induces an order-reversing involution of L(si(M)), and hence si(M) is modular by (A1). Be precise about the bottom flat when M has loops.
2. Deduce the component decomposition of si(M), and describe how the given involution permutes or dualizes components. In particular, track atoms and coatoms and explain what constraints exist on paired components.
3. Analyze the relationship between Dr(M) and Dr(si(M)). There is an obvious coordinate map: loops force coordinates to infinity and parallel elements force equal coordinates in Trop(M). Try to prove that tropical linear subspaces of Trop(M) are equivalent to tropical linear subspaces of Trop(si(M)) under this coordinate diagonal embedding. If this is not literally true, give the precise correction or the smallest counterexample. You should discuss valuated matroid quotients: if Trop(theta) ⊂ Trop(M), do loops/parallel classes of M remain loops/parallel with zero offset in theta?
4. Verify that under whatever simplification equivalence you establish, the embedded flat subposet F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) corresponds to the same construction for si(M), and the prescribed flat-lattice involution is preserved.
5. State the cleanest reduced version of the original question that remains after your work. Important: do not claim that direct sums make Dr factor componentwise unless you prove it; quotients of a direct sum can mix components, so just record the structural constraints.

task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver B. Work on the same problem: Let M be a finite matroid, Trop(M) its tropical linear space, Dr(M) the poset of tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M), and suppose L(M) has an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp. We seek an order-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) extending this flat-lattice involution.

For this task, assume M is simple and modular, and use the given order-reversing involution on L(M). You may use these facts: (B1) For modular M, the simplified codimension-one relative Dressian Dr^s_1(M) is isomorphic to Trop(M). More precisely, Dr^s_1(M)^o is the fan whose cones are indexed by chains of linear subclasses, and Qt^c_1(M) ≅ L(M). (B2) The principal elementary quotient associated to a flat G corresponds to the linear subclass H_G = {hyperplanes H of M : G ⊂ H}. (B3) The Bergman fan Trop(M)^o has cones indexed by chains of nonempty proper flats, with ray e_F for a flat F.

Your tasks:
1. Construct an explicit piecewise-linear map alpha: Trop(M) -> Dr^s_1(M) using the flat polarity. The expected formula on Bergman rays is e_F ↦ e_{H_{F^perp}}, where H_{F^perp} is the linear subclass of hyperplanes containing F^perp. Check carefully that if F_1 ⊂ ... ⊂ F_k then these linear subclasses form a chain in the correct order, so alpha is well-defined on cones. Also describe the inverse map.
2. Verify that alpha sends the flat point corresponding to a hyperplane H (i.e. Trop(M/H ⊕ U_{0,H}), a rank-one element of Dr(M)) to the codimension-one flat subspace corresponding to H^perp. More generally, identify what alpha does to boundary strata/rays indexed by arbitrary flats.
3. The crucial test: prove or disprove incidence symmetry for alpha. For p,q ∈ Trop(M), is it true that p ∈ alpha(q) if and only if q ∈ alpha(p)? Try to prove this using valuated quotient incidence equations or the three-term/concurrent-triple equations defining Dr^s_1(M). If you cannot prove it in full generality, test and explain the cases M = U_{3,n}, the Fano plane, and an arbitrary rank-3 projective plane with a polarity.
4. If incidence symmetry holds, give a complete construction of an order-reversing involution on Dr(M) for rank(M) ≤ 3: send bottom/top to top/bottom, send a tropical point p to alpha(p), and send a codimension-one tropical subspace L to alpha^{-1}(L). Prove the order-reversing property, involutivity, and extension of F ↦ F^perp.
5. Finally, state exactly what additional statement would be needed to extend this point-hyperplane polarity to all ranks via a polar formula Phi(X) = {p in Trop(M) : X ⊂ alpha(p)}. Be explicit about where tropical stable intersection or flag-completion issues enter.

plan.reasoning: Round 1 established the main structural reduction: the flat-lattice anti-involution forces the simplification to be modular, and the problem reduces to a simple modular matroid with a polarity. It also established the codimension-one polarity alpha and solved rank at most 3. The next best attack is to lift alpha to all ranks by working with valuated quotients of M. I also found a key elementary lemma: if p is a rank-k valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid M, then the incidence relations with the constant valuated matroid M force p to be constant on k-subsets spanning the same flat of M. Thus every relative Dressian point has flat coordinates. The remaining hard step is to prove that relabelling rank-k flat coordinates by F -> F^perp gives another valuated quotient and reverses quotient inclusions. This looks local: the Plücker relations for the polar reduce to a dual Plücker relation for four hyperplanes in a rank k+2 interval of the modular lattice.
plan.strategic_note: The promising route is no longer the naive intersection of polar hyperplanes, but a Plücker-coordinate construction on flats. We now have a likely all-rank formula: for a rank-k quotient p, define p^perp(J) = p(cl_M(J)^perp) for independent J of size r-k. Prove local dual Plücker and flag-incidence relations in modular intervals; if this works, it gives the desired order-reversing involution.

new_proven_results.statement: A tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to a global additive scalar, so Dr(M) may be treated as a projectivized set of valuated matroid quotients when constructing maps.
new_proven_results.sketch: Fink and Moci state that L(p)=L(q) iff p and q differ by a global additive, tropically multiplicative, constant. This removes ambiguity in defining Phi by Plücker coordinates. ([cris.unibo.it](https://cris.unibo.it/retrieve/handle/11585/676967/e1dcb339-cf40-7715-e053-1705fe0a6cc9/adv2moci-fink.pdf?utm_source=openai))

new_proven_results.statement: Flat-constancy lemma for relative quotients: If p is a rank-k valuated matroid quotient of an ordinary matroid M, then p_I depends only on the flat cl_M(I), for all finite p-bases I.
new_proven_results.sketch: The flag incidence relation between p and the constant valuated matroid M implies: for every circuit C of M and every subset A of size k-1, the minimum of p_{A∪{c}} over c in C minus A is attained at least twice, with invalid terms read as infinity. If two M-bases A∪{a} and A∪{b} span the same rank-k flat, then there is an M-circuit C contained in A∪{a,b} with C minus A = {a,b}; hence the two-term minimum forces p_{A∪{a}}=p_{A∪{b}}. The basis graph of the restriction of M to a flat is connected, so all p-bases spanning the same flat have the same value, and either every M-basis of that flat is a p-basis or none is.

new_bottlenecks: Need the local dual Plücker lemma: for a flat-valued rank-k quotient p and four hyperplanes H1,H2,H3,H4 in a rank k+2 flat Y of a modular lattice, the three opposite-pair sums p(H1∩H2)+p(H3∩H4), p(H1∩H3)+p(H2∩H4), p(H1∩H4)+p(H2∩H3) must satisfy the tropical minimum-attained-twice relation.
Need the dual incidence lemma for the polar p^perp to remain a quotient of M: circuit equations of M for p^perp should translate to codimension-one quotient equations for p on rank k+1 intervals.
Need to prove flag/order reversal: if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q), equivalently p is a quotient of q, then the polar construction should give Trop(q^perp) contained in Trop(p^perp).
kb_updates.frontier: Prove the all-rank flat-coordinate polarity theorem p -> p^perp and its flag/order-reversing property for simple modular M.

task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are solving the original problem in the reduced case from t1 and t2. Assume M is loopless, simple, modular of rank r, with ground set E equal to the atoms of L(M), and with a fixed order-reversing involution F -> F^perp of L(M). Let p be a rank-k valuated matroid on E with Trop(p) contained in Trop(M), equivalently p is a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid M. We want the all-rank coordinate polarity theorem.

Candidate construction: first show p_I is constant on all M-independent k-subsets I having the same closure in L(M). Write p_bar(F)=p_I for a rank-k flat F=cl_M(I), with p_bar(F)=infinity if no such p-basis spans F. Then define a rank r-k candidate p^perp by p^perp_J = p_bar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J of size r-k, and p^perp_J=infinity if J is M-dependent. Prove that p^perp is a valuated quotient of M, that the operation is involutive up to global additive scalar, and that if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q), then Trop(q^perp) is contained in Trop(p^perp).

Suggested route and deliverables: (1) Verify carefully the flat-constancy lemma, deriving the circuit-incidence relation for p from the flag incidence relations for p and M: for every M-circuit C and every A of size k-1, min over c in C minus A of p_{A union {c}} is attained at least twice. Use this to prove flat-constancy. (2) Prove the Plücker relations for p^perp. A useful local translation is: for a Plücker relation of p^perp involving S,a,b,c,d, set X=cl_M(S), Y=X^perp, and H_i=Y meet i^perp for i in {a,b,c,d}; the desired relation is a dual Plücker relation for the six rank-k flats H_i meet H_j in the rank k+2 interval [0,Y]. In the generic case this should be an ordinary Plücker relation for p after taking the common meet of the four H_i and the four triple meets; degenerate cases may need circuit-incidence equalities. (3) Prove the quotient/incidence relations for p^perp with M, perhaps by restricting p to rank k+1 intervals where it is a codimension-one quotient and using the codimension-one polarity from t2. (4) Extend from individual p to flags p quotient q quotient M, proving order reversal. If you cannot complete the proof, state the exact missing lemma in a form that could be separately attacked, and say whether you see any counterexample. Do not assume Dr(M) factors over connected components. Return a SOLVER_REPORT with proven_results, failed_attempts, and progress_notes.

# Referenced Prior Solver Outputs
Below are the full outputs of the prior tasks the orchestrator asked you to read. Each section starts with a metadata line that tells you whether the artifact has been verified by an independent verifier+refiner: a `verified ✓` block is the canonical, refined text and its claim/relaxation flags are authoritative; an `unverified self-report` block is a raw draft whose claim flags are the agent's own self-declaration and have NOT been checked. Examine the actual arguments — do not rely solely on summaries elsewhere in this prompt.

## Task t1 — Structural reduction to modular matroids and simplification issues
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Conventions

Let \(E\) be the ground set of \(M\). Write  
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
for the set of loops. This is the **bottom flat** of \(L(M)\); when \(M\) has loops, the bottom flat is not \(\varnothing\).

Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of parallel classes.

---

# 1. Simplification inherits the order-reversing involution

Every flat \(F\in L(M)\) contains \(L_0\), and if \(e\in F\) then the whole parallel class of \(e\) lies in \(F\). Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly sets of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\longrightarrow L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
In particular,
\[
\psi(L_0)=\varnothing,
\qquad
\psi(E)=\bar E.
\]

Let
\[
\alpha:L(M)\to L(M),
\qquad
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
be the given order-reversing involution. Since \(L_0\) is the bottom flat and \(E\) is the top flat,
\[
\alpha(L_0)=E,
\qquad
\alpha(E)=L_0.
\]
Define
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
Then \(\bar\alpha\) is again an order-reversing involution, and
\[
\bar\alpha(\varnothing)=\bar E,
\qquad
\bar\alpha(\bar E)=\varnothing.
\]

Because \(S\) is simple and \(L(S)\cong L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\), the matroid \(S\) is its own adjoint in the sense of (A1). Therefore, by (A1),

\[
\boxed{\operatorname{si}(M)\text{ is modular}.}
\]

The degenerate case \(S=\varnothing\) is harmless: the one-element lattice is self-dual and the empty matroid is modular.

---

# 2. Component decomposition and action of the involution

By (A3), since \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is simple and modular, its connected components are of the following types:
\[
S=\bigoplus_{i\in I} S_i,
\]
where each \(S_i\) is either

\[
U_{1,1}
\]

or a finite projective geometry
\[
\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i).
\]

The flat lattice decomposes as a product:
\[
L(S)\cong \prod_{i\in I} L(S_i).
\]

Write a flat \(F\in L(S)\) as a tuple
\[
F=(F_i)_{i\in I}.
\]

## Atoms and coatoms

An atom of \(L(S)\) is a flat supported in one component:
\[
(0,\dots,0,p,0,\dots,0),
\]
where \(p\) is a point of some \(S_i\).

A coatom of \(L(S)\) is a flat of the form
\[
(1,\dots,1,H,1,\dots,1),
\]
where \(H\) is a hyperplane of some \(S_j\).

Because \(\bar\alpha\) is order-reversing, it maps atoms bijectively to coatoms.

For points \(p,q\) in the same projective-geometry component \(S_i\), the interval
\[
[0,p\vee q]
\]
is a projective line and has \(q_i+1\ge 3\) atoms. If \(p,q\) lie in different components, then \([0,p\vee q]\) is Boolean of rank \(2\), hence has exactly \(2\) atoms.

Applying \(\bar\alpha\), the interval \([0,p\vee q]\) is anti-isomorphic to
\[
[\bar\alpha(p)\wedge \bar\alpha(q),1].
\]
Thus two atoms in the same projective component must map to coatoms supported in the same component; otherwise the upper interval would have only two coatoms. Conversely, atoms in distinct components cannot all map into the same projective-geometry component, because then the corresponding upper interval would have at least three coatoms.

Therefore \(\bar\alpha\) permutes the connected components.

More precisely, there is an involutive permutation
\[
\sigma:I\to I,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\mathrm{id},
\]
and for each \(i\in I\) an order-reversing lattice isomorphism
\[
\delta_i:L(S_i)\longrightarrow L(S_{\sigma(i)})
\]
such that
\[
\bar\alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\bar\alpha((F_i)_{i\in I})
=
\bigl(\delta_{\sigma^{-1}(j)}(F_{\sigma^{-1}(j)})\bigr)_{j\in I}.
\]

Since \(\bar\alpha^2=\mathrm{id}\), we also have
\[
\delta_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i^{-1}.
\]

## Constraints on paired components

Because \(\delta_i\) reverses ranks, \(S_i\) and \(S_{\sigma(i)}\) must have the same rank. Moreover:

- A \(U_{1,1}\)-component can only be paired with another \(U_{1,1}\)-component.
- A component \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\) can only be paired with another \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a polarity/order-reversing involution of \(L(S_i)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=j\neq i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a projective duality from \(S_i\) to \(S_j\), and \(\delta_j=\delta_i^{-1}\).

In atom/coatom language:

- A point \(p\in S_i\) maps to a hyperplane \(\delta_i(p)\subseteq S_{\sigma(i)}\).
- A hyperplane \(H\subseteq S_i\) maps to a point \(\delta_i(H)\in S_{\sigma(i)}\).

---

# 3. Relationship between \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) and \(\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\)

Work in extended tropical coordinates \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Define the diagonal embedding
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]

Indeed, loops of \(M\) force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and a parallel pair \(e,f\) gives a two-element circuit, forcing \(x_e=x_f\). After imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

Now let \(\theta\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\theta\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The following facts are useful.

### Loop/parallel detection in a valuated matroid

For a valuated matroid \(\theta\):

1. An element \(e\) is a loop of \(\theta\) iff every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\) has \(x_e=\infty\).

2. If \(e,f\) are nonloops and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \{x_e=x_f\}\), then \(e,f\) are parallel in the underlying matroid of \(\theta\), and their valuated parallel offset is zero.

The reason is that valuated cocircuits are points of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\). If \(e,f\) were not parallel, some cocircuit would contain exactly one of them, giving a point with one coordinate finite and the other \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel with nonzero offset, their valuated two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\) to equal that nonzero offset, contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

Applying this to the inclusion
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
we get:

- Every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(\theta\).
- Each parallel class of \(M\) is not split by \(\theta\): it becomes either an all-loop class, or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offsets.

Thus \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\bar E\), obtained by identifying each original parallel class and deleting the original loops. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Conversely, if \(\bar\theta\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), then its zero parallel extension to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops, satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar\theta)
=
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]

Hence the diagonal map induces an isomorphism of posets
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
}
\]
given by
\[
X\longmapsto \Delta(X).
\]
The inverse sends a tropical linear subspace \(Y\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) to \(\Delta^{-1}(Y)\).

A correction to a tempting stronger statement: a quotient may create new parallelisms between different simplified elements. For example,
\[
U_{1,1}\oplus U_{1,1}=U_{2,2}
\]
has quotient \(U_{1,2}\), whose tropical space is the diagonal \(x_1=x_2\). Thus \(\operatorname{Dr}\) does not factor componentwise in any naive way.

---

# 4. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on the ground set \(E\). Similarly define
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on the ground set \(\bar E\).

The matroid \(N_F\) is exactly the zero parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops added back as loops. One way to see this is through ranks. For \(A\subseteq E\setminus F\),
\[
r_{M/F}(A)
=
r_M(A\cup F)-r_M(F).
\]
Since \(M\) is a parallel extension of \(S\),
\[
r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup \bar F),
\qquad
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F),
\]
so
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_{S/\bar F}(\pi(A)).
\]
Adding \(U_{0,F}\) and \(U_{0,\bar F}\) gives the claimed compatibility on the full ground sets.

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
}
\]

Thus the diagram
\[
L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
corresponds under simplification to
\[
L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Moreover, the involution is preserved: if \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) and \(\bar\alpha=\psi\alpha\psi^{-1}\), then
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]

Consequently, existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending \(\alpha\) is equivalent to existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\).

---

# 5. Clean reduced version of the original question

After the reduction, one may assume:

1. \(M\) is loopless and simple.
2. \(M\) is modular.
3. Therefore
   \[
   M=\bigoplus_{i\in I} M_i,
   \]
   where each \(M_i\) is either \(U_{1,1}\) or \(\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i)\).
4. The prescribed flat-lattice involution has the form
   \[
   \alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i),
   \]
   where \(\sigma:I\to I\) is an involution and each
   \[
   \delta_i:L(M_i)\to L(M_{\sigma(i)})
   \]
   is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism.

The remaining question is therefore:

> For a direct sum of singleton components and finite projective geometries, equipped with such a componentwise projective duality on its flat lattice, does there exist an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded flat subposet?

One should not assume \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) factors over connected components: quotients of a direct sum can mix components, as already shown by \(U_{2,2}\twoheadrightarrow U_{1,2}\).

## Task t2 — Codimension-one polarity and rank-three test
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Setup and notation

Let \(r=\operatorname{rk} M\). Since \(M\) is simple, I identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathcal H(M)\) denote the set of hyperplanes. For a flat \(G\), write

\[
\mathcal H_G:=\{H\in \mathcal H(M):G\subseteq H\}.
\]

The polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats, so it bijects hyperplanes with atoms.

---

## 1. The map \(\alpha:\operatorname{Trop}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\)

In hyperplane-weight coordinates on \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\), define

\[
\boxed{(\alpha(q))_H := q_{H^\perp}\qquad H\in \mathcal H(M).}
\]

Equivalently, on Bergman rays \(e_F\),

\[
(\alpha(e_F))_H=1
\iff H^\perp\subseteq F
\iff F^\perp\subseteq H
\iff H\in \mathcal H_{F^\perp}.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

Now check cones. If

\[
F_1\subsetneq F_2\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq F_k,
\]

then polarity reverses the order:

\[
F_1^\perp\supsetneq F_2^\perp\supsetneq\cdots\supsetneq F_k^\perp.
\]

But \(G\mapsto \mathcal H_G\) is also order-reversing, since larger flats are contained in fewer hyperplanes. Hence

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_2^\perp}\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

So chains of flats map to chains of linear subclasses in the correct inclusion order. Therefore \(\alpha\) is well-defined on every Bergman cone. In fact, in the ambient quotient coordinates it is just the linear coordinate relabeling \(H\mapsto H^\perp\).

The inverse is

\[
\boxed{(\alpha^{-1}(w))_e := w_{e^\perp}\qquad e\in E.}
\]

On rays of \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\),

\[
\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G})=e_{G^\perp}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
(\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G}))_e
=1
\iff e^\perp\in\mathcal H_G
\iff G\subseteq e^\perp
\iff e\subseteq G^\perp.
\]

So \(\alpha\) is a PL fan isomorphism, with inverse given by \(e_{\mathcal H_G}\mapsto e_{G^\perp}\).

---

## 2. Effect on flat points and boundary rays

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(M\). The flat point corresponding to \(H\) is the rank-one quotient

\[
P_H=\operatorname{Trop}(M/H\oplus U_{0,H}),
\]

represented in the Bergman fan by the ray \(e_H\). Then

\[
\alpha(e_H)=e_{\mathcal H_{H^\perp}}.
\]

By fact (B2), \(\mathcal H_{H^\perp}\) is the linear subclass of the principal elementary quotient associated to the flat \(H^\perp\). Since \(H^\perp\) is an atom, this is exactly the codimension-one flat tropical subspace corresponding to \(H^\perp\). Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(P_H)=L_{H^\perp}.}
\]

More generally, for any nonempty proper flat \(F\),

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

So a Bergman boundary ray indexed by \(F\) is sent to the codimension-one Dressian boundary ray indexed, under \(Qt^c_1(M)\cong L(M)\), by the polar flat \(F^\perp\). For a flag \(F_1\subset\cdots\subset F_k\), the corresponding cone maps to the cone indexed by

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subset\cdots\subset\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

---

## 3. Incidence symmetry

I will prove the point-hyperplane incidence symmetry under the present assumptions:

\[
\boxed{p\in \alpha(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad q\in \alpha(p)}
\]

for all \(p,q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

For \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), let \(N_q=\alpha(q)\) be the corresponding rank \(r-1\) valuated quotient. Its Plücker coordinates are

\[
\nu_q(I)=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & I\subseteq E,\ |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & I\text{ dependent in }M.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(p\in \operatorname{Trop}(N_q)\) iff for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\),

\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+\nu_q(S\setminus i)\bigr)
\]

is attained at least twice.

If \(S\) is a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), then

\[
\nu_q(B\setminus b_i)=q_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}.
\]

Define

\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]

The incidence equation for \(B\) becomes

\[
\boxed{
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ is attained at least twice.}
}
\tag{\(*\)}
\]

If \(S\) has rank \(r-1\), then it has a unique circuit \(C\), and the finite terms in the incidence equation are all of the form \(p_i+q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\) with \(i\in C\). Since \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), the minimum of \(p_i\) on \(C\) is attained at least twice, so this equation is automatic. If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\). Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the basis equations \((*)\) for all bases \(B\).

Now modularity gives the key symmetry. For a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the set

\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]

is again a basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\). Indeed, for \(F_S=\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}\), modularity implies

\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]

Hence intersections of the coordinate hyperplanes \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)\) behave Booleanly, and polarity sends them to joins of the \(b_i^*\). In particular,

\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus b_i^*)=b_i^\perp,
\]

so the star operation applied again gives back \(b_i\).

Thus the basis equation for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) on \(B\),

\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*})
\]

is exactly the basis equation for \(q\in\alpha(p)\) on the dual basis \(B^*\),

\[
\min_i(q_{b_i^*}+p_{b_i}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on bases, all incidence equations are symmetric. Therefore

\[
\boxed{p\in\alpha(q)\iff q\in\alpha(p).}
\]

### Rank-three tests

- **\(U_{3,n}\).** For \(n>3\), \(U_{3,n}\) does not satisfy the assumptions: it is not modular, and its flat lattice cannot have an order-reversing involution because it has \(n\) atoms but \(\binom n2\) hyperplanes. The only admissible rank-three uniform case is \(U_{3,3}\), with Boolean polarity \(F^\perp=E\setminus F\). Then incidence is simply
  \[
  \min_i(p_i+q_i)\text{ attained twice},
  \]
  which is visibly symmetric.

- **Fano plane.** For a polarity of the Fano plane, \(\alpha(q)\) has line weights
  \[
  w_\ell=q_{\ell^\perp}.
  \]
  The concurrent-triple equations for \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\) become the circuit equations for \(q\) on the polar line. Incidence for a noncollinear triangle \(a,b,c\) is
  \[
  \min\bigl(p_a+q_{(bc)^\perp},\ p_b+q_{(ac)^\perp},\ p_c+q_{(ab)^\perp}\bigr),
  \]
  and the polar triangle has vertices \((bc)^\perp,(ac)^\perp,(ab)^\perp\), so the same equation appears with \(p,q\) interchanged.

- **Arbitrary rank-three projective plane with polarity.** The same proof works. Concurrent lines through a point \(x\) polarize to collinear points on \(x^\perp\), and noncollinear triangles polarize to noncollinear triangles. Thus the three-term incidence equations are paired by triangle polarity.

---

## 4. Rank \(\le 3\): order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Assume \(r=\operatorname{rk}M\le 3\). Then every nontrivial proper tropical subspace is either a point or a codimension-one subspace. Define \(\Phi\) by

\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\qquad
\Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0,
\]

\[
\boxed{\Phi(p)=\alpha(p)\quad\text{for points }p,}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L)\quad\text{for codimension-one subspaces }L.}
\]

This is involutive because \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\) and \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\).

It is order-reversing. The only nontrivial incidence in rank \(3\) is a point \(p\) lying in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then

\[
p\subseteq L
\iff p\in\alpha(q)
\iff q\in\alpha(p)
\iff \Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]

using incidence symmetry. The bottom/top cases are automatic.

Finally, \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), let

\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If \(F=H\) is a hyperplane, then \(X_H\) is a point and

\[
\Phi(X_H)=\alpha(e_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

If \(F=a\) is an atom, then \(X_a\) is codimension-one and

\[
\Phi(X_a)=\alpha^{-1}(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

The cases \(F=\varnothing,E\) are precisely top and bottom. Hence

\[
\boxed{\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}}
\]

for all flats \(F\) when \(r\le 3\).

---

## 5. What is still needed for all ranks

The point-hyperplane incidence symmetry is not by itself enough to define \(\Phi\) on all of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The needed extra theorem is:

> **Required all-rank polar theorem.**  
> For every rank \(k\) tropical linear subspace \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\), the set
> \[
> X^{\perp_\alpha}:=\{p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
> \]
> is a tropical linear subspace in \(\operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M)\), and biduality holds:
> \[
> (X^{\perp_\alpha})^{\perp_\alpha}=X.
> \]

Equivalently, for every valuated quotient \(V\) of \(M\) of rank \(k\), one needs a canonical complementary valuated quotient \(V^{\perp_\alpha}\) of rank \(r-k\) such that

\[
p\in \operatorname{Trop}(V^{\perp_\alpha})
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(V)\subseteq \alpha(p).
\]

The difficulty is that

\[
X^{\perp_\alpha}
=
\bigcap_{x\in X}\alpha(x)
\]

is an ordinary intersection of tropical hyperplanes. Ordinary intersections of tropical linear spaces need not have the expected dimension, need not be balanced, and need not themselves be tropical linear spaces. One would need a theorem identifying this incidence intersection with an appropriate stable intersection, or proving that it is independent of a choice of tropical spanning flag.

Thus the missing all-rank input is a stable-intersection/flag-completion theorem: every \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\) should admit compatible complete flags, reversing such a flag using the point-hyperplane polarity should give a complementary flag, and the resulting rank \(r-k\) member should be independent of the chosen flag. With that theorem, the formula

\[
\Phi(X)=\{p:X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
\]

would give the desired order-reversing involution in all ranks.

You are solving the original problem in the reduced case from t1 and t2. Assume M is loopless, simple, modular of rank r, with ground set E equal to the atoms of L(M), and with a fixed order-reversing involution F -> F^perp of L(M). Let p be a rank-k valuated matroid on E with Trop(p) contained in Trop(M), equivalently p is a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid M. We want the all-rank coordinate polarity theorem.

Candidate construction: first show p_I is constant on all M-independent k-subsets I having the same closure in L(M). Write p_bar(F)=p_I for a rank-k flat F=cl_M(I), with p_bar(F)=infinity if no such p-basis spans F. Then define a rank r-k candidate p^perp by p^perp_J = p_bar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J of size r-k, and p^perp_J=infinity if J is M-dependent. Prove that p^perp is a valuated quotient of M, that the operation is involutive up to global additive scalar, and that if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q), then Trop(q^perp) is contained in Trop(p^perp).

Suggested route and deliverables: (1) Verify carefully the flat-constancy lemma, deriving the circuit-incidence relation for p from the flag incidence relations for p and M: for every M-circuit C and every A of size k-1, min over c in C minus A of p_{A union {c}} is attained at least twice. Use this to prove flat-constancy. (2) Prove the Plücker relations for p^perp. A useful local translation is: for a Plücker relation of p^perp involving S,a,b,c,d, set X=cl_M(S), Y=X^perp, and H_i=Y meet i^perp for i in {a,b,c,d}; the desired relation is a dual Plücker relation for the six rank-k flats H_i meet H_j in the rank k+2 interval [0,Y]. In the generic case this should be an ordinary Plücker relation for p after taking the common meet of the four H_i and the four triple meets; degenerate cases may need circuit-incidence equalities. (3) Prove the quotient/incidence relations for p^perp with M, perhaps by restricting p to rank k+1 intervals where it is a codimension-one quotient and using the codimension-one polarity from t2. (4) Extend from individual p to flags p quotient q quotient M, proving order reversal. If you cannot complete the proof, state the exact missing lemma in a form that could be separately attacked, and say whether you see any counterexample. Do not assume Dr(M) factors over connected components. Return a SOLVER_REPORT with proven_results, failed_attempts, and progress_notes.

task_assignments.description: Local dual Plücker lemma and rank-four stress test
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
Focus on the key local lemma needed for all-rank polarity, and stress-test it in rank 4. Work in a loopless simple modular matroid M with flat-lattice polarity. Suppose p is a rank-k valuated quotient of M and is already known to be constant on rank-k flats.

Main local lemma to prove or refute: Let Y be a rank k+2 flat of M, and let H1,H2,H3,H4 be hyperplanes of the interval [0,Y]. Then the tropical minimum of the three sums p(H1 meet H2)+p(H3 meet H4), p(H1 meet H3)+p(H2 meet H4), and p(H1 meet H4)+p(H2 meet H3) is attained at least twice, with infinity allowed. This is exactly the Plücker relation needed for the polar p^perp.

Please attack this lemma directly. In the generic Boolean-position case, the meet Z=H1 meet H2 meet H3 meet H4 should have rank k-2, the triple meets should be four rank k-1 flats over Z, and the displayed relation should become an ordinary Plücker relation for p. The hard part is to handle degeneracies: repeated hyperplanes, total meet of rank greater than k-2, triple meets not covering the total meet, or projective dependencies among the four hyperplanes. Use the circuit-incidence relations coming from p being a quotient of M and modularity of the lattice.

Also specialize to rank r=4 and k=2. Then the lemma says: for four hyperplanes/planes in a projective 3-space or in a direct sum of modular components, the six intersection lines satisfy the rank-2 Plücker four-point relation for p. In PG(3,q), interpret this as the opposite-edge relation of a possibly degenerate tetrahedron; prove it or find an explicit valuated or ordinary counterexample, especially in PG(3,2) or in mixed direct sums such as a projective plane plus a point. If the lemma holds in rank 4, explain how it verifies the middle-rank part of the desired involution there. Return a SOLVER_REPORT with clear proven_results, any counterexamples or failed approaches, and the exact remaining gap.

## Task t1 — Structural reduction to modular matroids and simplification issues
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Conventions

Let \(E\) be the ground set of \(M\). Write  
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
for the set of loops. This is the **bottom flat** of \(L(M)\); when \(M\) has loops, the bottom flat is not \(\varnothing\).

Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of parallel classes.

---

# 1. Simplification inherits the order-reversing involution

Every flat \(F\in L(M)\) contains \(L_0\), and if \(e\in F\) then the whole parallel class of \(e\) lies in \(F\). Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly sets of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\longrightarrow L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
In particular,
\[
\psi(L_0)=\varnothing,
\qquad
\psi(E)=\bar E.
\]

Let
\[
\alpha:L(M)\to L(M),
\qquad
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
be the given order-reversing involution. Since \(L_0\) is the bottom flat and \(E\) is the top flat,
\[
\alpha(L_0)=E,
\qquad
\alpha(E)=L_0.
\]
Define
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
Then \(\bar\alpha\) is again an order-reversing involution, and
\[
\bar\alpha(\varnothing)=\bar E,
\qquad
\bar\alpha(\bar E)=\varnothing.
\]

Because \(S\) is simple and \(L(S)\cong L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\), the matroid \(S\) is its own adjoint in the sense of (A1). Therefore, by (A1),

\[
\boxed{\operatorname{si}(M)\text{ is modular}.}
\]

The degenerate case \(S=\varnothing\) is harmless: the one-element lattice is self-dual and the empty matroid is modular.

---

# 2. Component decomposition and action of the involution

By (A3), since \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is simple and modular, its connected components are of the following types:
\[
S=\bigoplus_{i\in I} S_i,
\]
where each \(S_i\) is either

\[
U_{1,1}
\]

or a finite projective geometry
\[
\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i).
\]

The flat lattice decomposes as a product:
\[
L(S)\cong \prod_{i\in I} L(S_i).
\]

Write a flat \(F\in L(S)\) as a tuple
\[
F=(F_i)_{i\in I}.
\]

## Atoms and coatoms

An atom of \(L(S)\) is a flat supported in one component:
\[
(0,\dots,0,p,0,\dots,0),
\]
where \(p\) is a point of some \(S_i\).

A coatom of \(L(S)\) is a flat of the form
\[
(1,\dots,1,H,1,\dots,1),
\]
where \(H\) is a hyperplane of some \(S_j\).

Because \(\bar\alpha\) is order-reversing, it maps atoms bijectively to coatoms.

For points \(p,q\) in the same projective-geometry component \(S_i\), the interval
\[
[0,p\vee q]
\]
is a projective line and has \(q_i+1\ge 3\) atoms. If \(p,q\) lie in different components, then \([0,p\vee q]\) is Boolean of rank \(2\), hence has exactly \(2\) atoms.

Applying \(\bar\alpha\), the interval \([0,p\vee q]\) is anti-isomorphic to
\[
[\bar\alpha(p)\wedge \bar\alpha(q),1].
\]
Thus two atoms in the same projective component must map to coatoms supported in the same component; otherwise the upper interval would have only two coatoms. Conversely, atoms in distinct components cannot all map into the same projective-geometry component, because then the corresponding upper interval would have at least three coatoms.

Therefore \(\bar\alpha\) permutes the connected components.

More precisely, there is an involutive permutation
\[
\sigma:I\to I,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\mathrm{id},
\]
and for each \(i\in I\) an order-reversing lattice isomorphism
\[
\delta_i:L(S_i)\longrightarrow L(S_{\sigma(i)})
\]
such that
\[
\bar\alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\bar\alpha((F_i)_{i\in I})
=
\bigl(\delta_{\sigma^{-1}(j)}(F_{\sigma^{-1}(j)})\bigr)_{j\in I}.
\]

Since \(\bar\alpha^2=\mathrm{id}\), we also have
\[
\delta_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i^{-1}.
\]

## Constraints on paired components

Because \(\delta_i\) reverses ranks, \(S_i\) and \(S_{\sigma(i)}\) must have the same rank. Moreover:

- A \(U_{1,1}\)-component can only be paired with another \(U_{1,1}\)-component.
- A component \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\) can only be paired with another \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a polarity/order-reversing involution of \(L(S_i)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=j\neq i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a projective duality from \(S_i\) to \(S_j\), and \(\delta_j=\delta_i^{-1}\).

In atom/coatom language:

- A point \(p\in S_i\) maps to a hyperplane \(\delta_i(p)\subseteq S_{\sigma(i)}\).
- A hyperplane \(H\subseteq S_i\) maps to a point \(\delta_i(H)\in S_{\sigma(i)}\).

---

# 3. Relationship between \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) and \(\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\)

Work in extended tropical coordinates \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Define the diagonal embedding
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]

Indeed, loops of \(M\) force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and a parallel pair \(e,f\) gives a two-element circuit, forcing \(x_e=x_f\). After imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

Now let \(\theta\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\theta\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The following facts are useful.

### Loop/parallel detection in a valuated matroid

For a valuated matroid \(\theta\):

1. An element \(e\) is a loop of \(\theta\) iff every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\) has \(x_e=\infty\).

2. If \(e,f\) are nonloops and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \{x_e=x_f\}\), then \(e,f\) are parallel in the underlying matroid of \(\theta\), and their valuated parallel offset is zero.

The reason is that valuated cocircuits are points of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\). If \(e,f\) were not parallel, some cocircuit would contain exactly one of them, giving a point with one coordinate finite and the other \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel with nonzero offset, their valuated two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\) to equal that nonzero offset, contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

Applying this to the inclusion
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
we get:

- Every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(\theta\).
- Each parallel class of \(M\) is not split by \(\theta\): it becomes either an all-loop class, or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offsets.

Thus \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\bar E\), obtained by identifying each original parallel class and deleting the original loops. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Conversely, if \(\bar\theta\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), then its zero parallel extension to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops, satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar\theta)
=
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]

Hence the diagonal map induces an isomorphism of posets
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
}
\]
given by
\[
X\longmapsto \Delta(X).
\]
The inverse sends a tropical linear subspace \(Y\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) to \(\Delta^{-1}(Y)\).

A correction to a tempting stronger statement: a quotient may create new parallelisms between different simplified elements. For example,
\[
U_{1,1}\oplus U_{1,1}=U_{2,2}
\]
has quotient \(U_{1,2}\), whose tropical space is the diagonal \(x_1=x_2\). Thus \(\operatorname{Dr}\) does not factor componentwise in any naive way.

---

# 4. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on the ground set \(E\). Similarly define
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on the ground set \(\bar E\).

The matroid \(N_F\) is exactly the zero parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops added back as loops. One way to see this is through ranks. For \(A\subseteq E\setminus F\),
\[
r_{M/F}(A)
=
r_M(A\cup F)-r_M(F).
\]
Since \(M\) is a parallel extension of \(S\),
\[
r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup \bar F),
\qquad
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F),
\]
so
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_{S/\bar F}(\pi(A)).
\]
Adding \(U_{0,F}\) and \(U_{0,\bar F}\) gives the claimed compatibility on the full ground sets.

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
}
\]

Thus the diagram
\[
L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
corresponds under simplification to
\[
L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Moreover, the involution is preserved: if \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) and \(\bar\alpha=\psi\alpha\psi^{-1}\), then
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]

Consequently, existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending \(\alpha\) is equivalent to existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\).

---

# 5. Clean reduced version of the original question

After the reduction, one may assume:

1. \(M\) is loopless and simple.
2. \(M\) is modular.
3. Therefore
   \[
   M=\bigoplus_{i\in I} M_i,
   \]
   where each \(M_i\) is either \(U_{1,1}\) or \(\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i)\).
4. The prescribed flat-lattice involution has the form
   \[
   \alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i),
   \]
   where \(\sigma:I\to I\) is an involution and each
   \[
   \delta_i:L(M_i)\to L(M_{\sigma(i)})
   \]
   is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism.

The remaining question is therefore:

> For a direct sum of singleton components and finite projective geometries, equipped with such a componentwise projective duality on its flat lattice, does there exist an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded flat subposet?

One should not assume \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) factors over connected components: quotients of a direct sum can mix components, as already shown by \(U_{2,2}\twoheadrightarrow U_{1,2}\).

## Task t2 — Codimension-one polarity and rank-three test
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Setup and notation

Let \(r=\operatorname{rk} M\). Since \(M\) is simple, I identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathcal H(M)\) denote the set of hyperplanes. For a flat \(G\), write

\[
\mathcal H_G:=\{H\in \mathcal H(M):G\subseteq H\}.
\]

The polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats, so it bijects hyperplanes with atoms.

---

## 1. The map \(\alpha:\operatorname{Trop}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\)

In hyperplane-weight coordinates on \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\), define

\[
\boxed{(\alpha(q))_H := q_{H^\perp}\qquad H\in \mathcal H(M).}
\]

Equivalently, on Bergman rays \(e_F\),

\[
(\alpha(e_F))_H=1
\iff H^\perp\subseteq F
\iff F^\perp\subseteq H
\iff H\in \mathcal H_{F^\perp}.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

Now check cones. If

\[
F_1\subsetneq F_2\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq F_k,
\]

then polarity reverses the order:

\[
F_1^\perp\supsetneq F_2^\perp\supsetneq\cdots\supsetneq F_k^\perp.
\]

But \(G\mapsto \mathcal H_G\) is also order-reversing, since larger flats are contained in fewer hyperplanes. Hence

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_2^\perp}\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

So chains of flats map to chains of linear subclasses in the correct inclusion order. Therefore \(\alpha\) is well-defined on every Bergman cone. In fact, in the ambient quotient coordinates it is just the linear coordinate relabeling \(H\mapsto H^\perp\).

The inverse is

\[
\boxed{(\alpha^{-1}(w))_e := w_{e^\perp}\qquad e\in E.}
\]

On rays of \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\),

\[
\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G})=e_{G^\perp}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
(\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G}))_e
=1
\iff e^\perp\in\mathcal H_G
\iff G\subseteq e^\perp
\iff e\subseteq G^\perp.
\]

So \(\alpha\) is a PL fan isomorphism, with inverse given by \(e_{\mathcal H_G}\mapsto e_{G^\perp}\).

---

## 2. Effect on flat points and boundary rays

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(M\). The flat point corresponding to \(H\) is the rank-one quotient

\[
P_H=\operatorname{Trop}(M/H\oplus U_{0,H}),
\]

represented in the Bergman fan by the ray \(e_H\). Then

\[
\alpha(e_H)=e_{\mathcal H_{H^\perp}}.
\]

By fact (B2), \(\mathcal H_{H^\perp}\) is the linear subclass of the principal elementary quotient associated to the flat \(H^\perp\). Since \(H^\perp\) is an atom, this is exactly the codimension-one flat tropical subspace corresponding to \(H^\perp\). Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(P_H)=L_{H^\perp}.}
\]

More generally, for any nonempty proper flat \(F\),

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

So a Bergman boundary ray indexed by \(F\) is sent to the codimension-one Dressian boundary ray indexed, under \(Qt^c_1(M)\cong L(M)\), by the polar flat \(F^\perp\). For a flag \(F_1\subset\cdots\subset F_k\), the corresponding cone maps to the cone indexed by

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subset\cdots\subset\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

---

## 3. Incidence symmetry

I will prove the point-hyperplane incidence symmetry under the present assumptions:

\[
\boxed{p\in \alpha(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad q\in \alpha(p)}
\]

for all \(p,q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

For \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), let \(N_q=\alpha(q)\) be the corresponding rank \(r-1\) valuated quotient. Its Plücker coordinates are

\[
\nu_q(I)=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & I\subseteq E,\ |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & I\text{ dependent in }M.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(p\in \operatorname{Trop}(N_q)\) iff for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\),

\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+\nu_q(S\setminus i)\bigr)
\]

is attained at least twice.

If \(S\) is a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), then

\[
\nu_q(B\setminus b_i)=q_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}.
\]

Define

\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]

The incidence equation for \(B\) becomes

\[
\boxed{
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ is attained at least twice.}
}
\tag{\(*\)}
\]

If \(S\) has rank \(r-1\), then it has a unique circuit \(C\), and the finite terms in the incidence equation are all of the form \(p_i+q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\) with \(i\in C\). Since \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), the minimum of \(p_i\) on \(C\) is attained at least twice, so this equation is automatic. If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\). Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the basis equations \((*)\) for all bases \(B\).

Now modularity gives the key symmetry. For a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the set

\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]

is again a basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\). Indeed, for \(F_S=\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}\), modularity implies

\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]

Hence intersections of the coordinate hyperplanes \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)\) behave Booleanly, and polarity sends them to joins of the \(b_i^*\). In particular,

\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus b_i^*)=b_i^\perp,
\]

so the star operation applied again gives back \(b_i\).

Thus the basis equation for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) on \(B\),

\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*})
\]

is exactly the basis equation for \(q\in\alpha(p)\) on the dual basis \(B^*\),

\[
\min_i(q_{b_i^*}+p_{b_i}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on bases, all incidence equations are symmetric. Therefore

\[
\boxed{p\in\alpha(q)\iff q\in\alpha(p).}
\]

### Rank-three tests

- **\(U_{3,n}\).** For \(n>3\), \(U_{3,n}\) does not satisfy the assumptions: it is not modular, and its flat lattice cannot have an order-reversing involution because it has \(n\) atoms but \(\binom n2\) hyperplanes. The only admissible rank-three uniform case is \(U_{3,3}\), with Boolean polarity \(F^\perp=E\setminus F\). Then incidence is simply
  \[
  \min_i(p_i+q_i)\text{ attained twice},
  \]
  which is visibly symmetric.

- **Fano plane.** For a polarity of the Fano plane, \(\alpha(q)\) has line weights
  \[
  w_\ell=q_{\ell^\perp}.
  \]
  The concurrent-triple equations for \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\) become the circuit equations for \(q\) on the polar line. Incidence for a noncollinear triangle \(a,b,c\) is
  \[
  \min\bigl(p_a+q_{(bc)^\perp},\ p_b+q_{(ac)^\perp},\ p_c+q_{(ab)^\perp}\bigr),
  \]
  and the polar triangle has vertices \((bc)^\perp,(ac)^\perp,(ab)^\perp\), so the same equation appears with \(p,q\) interchanged.

- **Arbitrary rank-three projective plane with polarity.** The same proof works. Concurrent lines through a point \(x\) polarize to collinear points on \(x^\perp\), and noncollinear triangles polarize to noncollinear triangles. Thus the three-term incidence equations are paired by triangle polarity.

---

## 4. Rank \(\le 3\): order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Assume \(r=\operatorname{rk}M\le 3\). Then every nontrivial proper tropical subspace is either a point or a codimension-one subspace. Define \(\Phi\) by

\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\qquad
\Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0,
\]

\[
\boxed{\Phi(p)=\alpha(p)\quad\text{for points }p,}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L)\quad\text{for codimension-one subspaces }L.}
\]

This is involutive because \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\) and \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\).

It is order-reversing. The only nontrivial incidence in rank \(3\) is a point \(p\) lying in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then

\[
p\subseteq L
\iff p\in\alpha(q)
\iff q\in\alpha(p)
\iff \Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]

using incidence symmetry. The bottom/top cases are automatic.

Finally, \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), let

\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If \(F=H\) is a hyperplane, then \(X_H\) is a point and

\[
\Phi(X_H)=\alpha(e_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

If \(F=a\) is an atom, then \(X_a\) is codimension-one and

\[
\Phi(X_a)=\alpha^{-1}(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

The cases \(F=\varnothing,E\) are precisely top and bottom. Hence

\[
\boxed{\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}}
\]

for all flats \(F\) when \(r\le 3\).

---

## 5. What is still needed for all ranks

The point-hyperplane incidence symmetry is not by itself enough to define \(\Phi\) on all of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The needed extra theorem is:

> **Required all-rank polar theorem.**  
> For every rank \(k\) tropical linear subspace \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\), the set
> \[
> X^{\perp_\alpha}:=\{p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
> \]
> is a tropical linear subspace in \(\operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M)\), and biduality holds:
> \[
> (X^{\perp_\alpha})^{\perp_\alpha}=X.
> \]

Equivalently, for every valuated quotient \(V\) of \(M\) of rank \(k\), one needs a canonical complementary valuated quotient \(V^{\perp_\alpha}\) of rank \(r-k\) such that

\[
p\in \operatorname{Trop}(V^{\perp_\alpha})
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(V)\subseteq \alpha(p).
\]

The difficulty is that

\[
X^{\perp_\alpha}
=
\bigcap_{x\in X}\alpha(x)
\]

is an ordinary intersection of tropical hyperplanes. Ordinary intersections of tropical linear spaces need not have the expected dimension, need not be balanced, and need not themselves be tropical linear spaces. One would need a theorem identifying this incidence intersection with an appropriate stable intersection, or proving that it is independent of a choice of tropical spanning flag.

Thus the missing all-rank input is a stable-intersection/flag-completion theorem: every \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\) should admit compatible complete flags, reversing such a flag using the point-hyperplane polarity should give a complementary flag, and the resulting rank \(r-k\) member should be independent of the chosen flag. With that theorem, the formula

\[
\Phi(X)=\{p:X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
\]

would give the desired order-reversing involution in all ranks.

Main local lemma to prove or refute: Let Y be a rank k+2 flat of M, and let H1,H2,H3,H4 be hyperplanes of the interval [0,Y]. Then the tropical minimum of the three sums p(H1 meet H2)+p(H3 meet H4), p(H1 meet H3)+p(H2 meet H4), and p(H1 meet H4)+p(H2 meet H3) is attained at least twice, with infinity allowed. This is exactly the Plücker relation needed for the polar p^perp.

Please attack this lemma directly. In the generic Boolean-position case, the meet Z=H1 meet H2 meet H3 meet H4 should have rank k-2, the triple meets should be four rank k-1 flats over Z, and the displayed relation should become an ordinary Plücker relation for p. The hard part is to handle degeneracies: repeated hyperplanes, total meet of rank greater than k-2, triple meets not covering the total meet, or projective dependencies among the four hyperplanes. Use the circuit-incidence relations coming from p being a quotient of M and modularity of the lattice.

Also specialize to rank r=4 and k=2. Then the lemma says: for four hyperplanes/planes in a projective 3-space or in a direct sum of modular components, the six intersection lines satisfy the rank-2 Plücker four-point relation for p. writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Produce a fully self-contained mathematical write-up proving the following theorem. Theorem: Let M be a finite loopless simple modular matroid of rank at most 3, and suppose the lattice of flats L(M) has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Let Trop(M) be the projective tropical linear space of M, and let Dr(M) be the poset of projective tropical linear subspaces of Trop(M), ordered by inclusion. Embed L(M)^op into Dr(M) by sending a flat F to Trop(M/F direct-sum U_{0,F}) on the same ground set. Then there is an order-reversing involution Phi of Dr(M) extending F -> F^perp on this embedded flat subposet.

The write-up must define all notation and be readable without any access to this conversation. It may use standard facts, but they must be stated explicitly: inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces corresponds to valuated matroid quotients; for a simple modular matroid the codimension-one relative Dressian is identified with Trop(M) via the polarity on hyperplanes; and a tropical linear space is determined by its valuated Plücker vector up to global additive scalar. Then give the construction: define alpha from points q in Trop(M) to codimension-one tropical subspaces by hyperplane weights alpha(q)_H = q_{H^perp}; prove or state with proof the basis-equation incidence symmetry p in alpha(q) iff q in alpha(p); define Phi on bottom, top, points, and hyperplanes; prove it is involutive and order-reversing; and verify that flat points and flat hyperplanes are sent to their polar flats. Do not refer to task IDs, previous solvers, or the problem statement outside the theorem itself.

I want one solver to attack this missing mixed-rank incidence relation directly, using a modular-lattice dual-basis construction, and a second solver to try the equivalent-looking annihilator route: prove L(p^dagger) is exactly the set of points whose polar hyperplanes contain L(p). If the annihilator theorem works, order reversal is immediate.

task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are Solver t5. Your goal is to prove the missing mixed-rank flag-duality lemma for the flat-coordinate polarity constructed in t3. Work under the following hypotheses. M is a finite simple modular matroid of rank r with an order-reversing involution F -> F⊥ on its lattice of flats. Let p and q be valuated matroid quotients of M of ranks k <= l, and assume p ↞ q, equivalently Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q). By t3, p and q are constant on M-independent subsets with the same M-closure; write pbar(F) and qbar(F) for the resulting values on rank-k and rank-l flats, with value infinity on other ranks. Define p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)⊥) if J is M-independent of size r-k, and infinity otherwise; define q^dagger similarly. Prove q^dagger ↞ p^dagger. Equivalently, for every A with |A| = r-l-1 and every B with |B| = r-k+1, prove that the minimum over i in B minus A of qbar(cl(A union i)⊥) + pbar(cl(B minus i)⊥), with dependent terms treated as infinity, has no unique finite minimum.

Suggested route to investigate carefully. First prove an adapted incidence lemma. Assume A is independent with X = cl(A), B is independent with C = cl(B), and B contains a basis of T = X meet C. Let U = X join C. For i in B not contained in T, put C_i = cl(B minus i), F_i = C_i⊥, and G_i = (X join i)⊥. The desired terms are pbar(F_i) + qbar(G_i). Let Z = C⊥ and W = U⊥. Try to choose a basis A_prime of Z containing a basis of W, and for each relevant i choose an atom e_i in (X join C_i)⊥ but not in Z. Check rigorously that cl(Z union e_i) = F_i, that e_j lies in G_i for j != i, that e_i does not lie in G_i, and that a basis of W together with the e_i forms a basis B_prime of X⊥ whose omission of e_i spans G_i. Then the original flag incidence relation for p ↞ q applied to A_prime and B_prime should give exactly the adapted polar relation.

After proving the adapted case, remove the adapted assumption. Possible approaches: show arbitrary relations propagate from adapted ones using the Plucker relations for p^dagger and q^dagger; use a unique-minimum contradiction and exchange B to an adapted basis without increasing the alleged minimal term; or use the valuated quotient exchange axiom rather than the incidence-polynomial form. Be very explicit about all degeneracies: A dependent, B dependent, repeated closures, terms equal to infinity, and cases where B does not span enough modulo X. If you cannot finish the arbitrary case, give a precise partial theorem and identify the exact obstruction. As a red-team fallback, test the ordinary 0/infinity case in PG(3,2) for ranks k=1,l=2 and k=2,l=3, looking for a counterexample to the mixed lemma.
