## Task t6 — Point-annihilator characterization of the polar
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
The point-annihilator theorem **does succeed**. The clean way around the mixed-rank flag-duality lemma is to restrict to each Boolean apartment determined by an \(M\)-basis; on that apartment, the coordinate polar \(p^\dagger\) is just the ordinary valuated-matroid dual of \(p\).

Below, all tropical orthogonality/minimum conditions use the convention “no unique finite minimum.”

---

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be finite simple modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(p^\dagger\) be the coordinate polar from t3:
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J \text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J \text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\alpha(x)_H=x_{H^\perp}
\]
for hyperplanes \(H\) of \(M\). Then
\[
\boxed{
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
}
\]

The boundary cases \(k=0,r\) are immediate, so assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

---

## 1. Boolean apartments and ordinary duality

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis
\[
b_i^\star:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
By modularity and polarity, \(B^\star=\{b_1^\star,\dots,b_r^\star\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and for every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Let \(p|_B\) be the ordinary valuated matroid on \([r]\) defined by
\[
(p|_B)_T:=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis spans the underlying matroid of \(p\), so \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^\star\). If \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^\star})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
\boxed{
p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*
}
\]
as an ordinary valuated matroid dual on the Boolean ground set \([r]\).

We also use the standard Boolean annihilator fact:

> If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set and \(\mu^*\) its ordinary dual, then
> \[
> u\in \operatorname{Trop}(\mu^*)
> \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
> \operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
> \]
> where
> \[
> H_u=\{v:\min_i(u_i+v_i)\text{ is attained at least twice}\}.
> \]
> This follows because the circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\) is the tropical span of its valuated cocircuits.

---

## 2. Direction \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\Rightarrow \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\)

Assume \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), and set
\[
u_B:=(x_{b_i^\star})_{i=1}^r.
\]
Since \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\), its restriction to \(B^\star\) lies in
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star})
=
\operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*).
\]
By the Boolean annihilator fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Now let \(C_A\) be any valuated cocircuit of \(p\), with
\[
(C_A)_e=p_{A\cup e}
\]
for \(|A|=k-1\). Since cocircuits of \(p\) lie in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), the restriction
\[
(C_A)|_B=(p_{A\cup b_i})_i
\]
lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\star}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

But this is exactly the basis-incidence equation for
\[
C_A\in \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Because this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), every valuated cocircuit of \(p\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\).

Finally, \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of \(p\), and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) is tropically convex. Hence
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
}
\]

---

## 3. Direction \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\Rightarrow x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\)

Assume
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_i^\star})_i.
\]

We claim
\[
u_B\in \operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*).
\]
By the Boolean annihilator fact, it is enough to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\).

A cocircuit of \(p|_B\) has the form
\[
D_S=(p_{S\cup b_i})_i
\]
for some \((k-1)\)-subset \(S\subseteq B\). This is precisely the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S=(p_{S\cup e})_{e\in E}.
\]
Since \(C_S\in \operatorname{Trop}(p)\), the containment assumption gives
\[
C_S\in \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Testing membership in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\star}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. That is exactly
\[
D_S\perp u_B.
\]
Thus \(u_B\) is orthogonal to every cocircuit of \(p|_B\), so
\[
u_B\in \operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be any \(M\)-independent subset of size
\[
|J|=r-k+1.
\]
Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^\star\). From the previous paragraph,
\[
x|_{B^\star}\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}).
\]
Therefore the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\subseteq B^\star\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Substituting the definition of \(p^\dagger\),
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)^\perp),
\]
we obtain exactly the coordinate condition
\[
\min_{j\in J}
\left(
x_j+\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)^\perp)
\right)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

These are precisely the independent-\(J\) point equations for
\[
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\): either all terms are \(\infty\), or the finite terms differ by a constant on the unique \(M\)-circuit in \(J\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
}
\]

This proves the theorem.

---

## 4. Consequence: full order reversal

Define
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))
:=
\{x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M):\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
By the theorem,
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore, if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(q))
\subseteq
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)),
\]
because any hyperplane containing \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\) also contains \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\).

Using t3’s involutivity
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
we obtain an order-reversing involution
\[
\boxed{
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)):=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
}
\]
on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The flat-extension property from t3 gives
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}),
\]
so \(\Phi\) extends the prescribed flat-lattice polarity.

writeup_tasks.statement: For a finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, there exists an order-reversing involution Phi on the relative Dressian Dr(M) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded copy of L(M)^op.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Write a fully self-contained mathematical proof of the following claim. Claim: Let M be a finite matroid with flat lattice L(M). Suppose L(M) has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Let Dr(M) be the poset of tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M), ordered by inclusion, and let the embedded flat subposet send a flat F to Trop(M/F direct-sum U_{0,F}). Then there is an order-reversing involution Phi of Dr(M) extending F -> F^perp. The write-up must be understandable to a reader who has not seen any solver history. Define every symbol and convention used. Do not refer to tasks, previous rounds, or shared notes. Suggested proof structure: first state the standard valuated-matroid facts being used, including that relative tropical linear subspaces of Trop(M) are valuated matroid quotients of M, inclusion corresponds to the quotient relation, and a tropical linear space determines its valuated Pluecker vector up to additive scalar. Next reduce to the simplification of M, proving that loops force infinity coordinates and parallel classes force equal coordinates, so Dr(M) is canonically identified with Dr(si(M)) and the flat subposet is compatible. Then prove that the simple matroid is modular because its flat lattice is anti-isomorphic to itself. In the simple modular case, define for a rank-k quotient p a flat-valued function pbar(X) on rank-k flats X by pbar(X)=p_I for any M-independent k-set I with cl_M(I)=X, and infinity if no such I is a p-basis; prove flat-constancy. Define p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J of size r-k, and infinity otherwise. State and prove the technical lemma that p^dagger is a valuated quotient of M, that (p^dagger)^dagger=p, and that flat quotients are sent to the F^perp flat quotients. For order reversal, use the point-annihilator theorem: for a tropical point x in Trop(M), let alpha(x) be the relative hyperplane whose coordinate at a hyperplane H is x_{H^perp}; then prove x is in Trop(p^dagger) if and only if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(alpha(x)). Prove this by choosing an M-basis B={b_1,...,b_r}, defining the polar basis b_i^* = cl_M(B minus b_i)^perp, showing cl({b_i^*: i in S}) = cl({b_i: i notin S})^perp, observing that p^dagger restricted to B^* is the ordinary dual of p restricted to B, and applying ordinary tropical annihilator duality for valuated matroids on a Boolean ground set. Finally, if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q), use the annihilator characterization to show Trop(q^dagger) is contained in Trop(p^dagger), proving that Phi(Trop(p))=Trop(p^dagger) is order reversing. Check boundary cases k=0 and k=r, and explicitly verify that Phi extends the given F -> F^perp on the embedded flat subposet. End with a clear affirmative answer to the question.

The next tasks should therefore be adversarial verification plus a rigorous non-simple reduction, while a full write-up agent attempts to assemble the theorem end-to-end.

task_assignments.solver_prompt: You are a skeptical mathematical referee. The claimed final theorem is: for any finite matroid M whose lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, there is an order-reversing involution Phi on the relative Dressian Dr(M) extending this polarity on the embedded flat subposet. Read the attached t7 and t8 outputs carefully. Your job is not to produce a polished proof, but to attack the proof strategy and find any fatal gap or missing hypothesis. Focus especially on: (1) whether every M-basis really contains a finite p-basis for a valuated quotient p of M, so that Boolean apartment restrictions used in the point-annihilator proof are valid; (2) whether ordinary valuated-matroid annihilator duality is being used correctly with infinities and projective tropical spaces; (3) whether the local dual Plucker lemma and codimension-one polarity lemma cover all repeated, dependent, loop, coloop, and boundary-rank cases; (4) whether the simplification reduction is actually a poset isomorphism Dr(si(M)) -> Dr(M), including loops and parallel classes; and (5) whether the flat-extension statement for M/F plus loops on F is exactly compatible with simplification. Try small examples, including rank 0, rank 1, rank 2 uniform matroids, a matroid with a nontrivial parallel class, and direct sums. If you find a counterexample, state it precisely. If you find only repairable gaps, give exact patched statements and proof sketches. End with a verdict: sound, sound after repairs, or false.

# Referenced Prior Solver Outputs
Below are the full outputs of the prior tasks the orchestrator asked you to read. Each section starts with a metadata line that tells you whether the artifact has been verified by an independent verifier+refiner: a `verified ✓` block is the canonical, refined text and its claim/relaxation flags are authoritative; an `unverified self-report` block is a raw draft whose claim flags are the agent's own self-declaration and have NOT been checked. Examine the actual arguments — do not rely solely on summaries elsewhere in this prompt.

## Task t7 — Skeptical referee check of the claimed full proof
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Referee verdict

I do **not** see a counterexample to the claimed theorem. The strategy appears sound **after several repairs and explicit lemmas are added**. As written, however, the proof is not yet referee-ready: a few statements are either under-proved, slightly false as stated, or rely on hidden standard facts that must be stated precisely.

The most serious issues are **patchable**, not fatal:

1. the simplification reduction needs a precise valuated-parallel-class lemma;
2. the coordinate formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) must be shown to define a map on **tropical linear spaces**, not merely on chosen Plücker vectors;
3. the proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient must explicitly handle dependent and boundary cases;
4. the point-annihilator proof is basically correct but uses apartment restrictions in a way that needs two clarifications;
5. the flat-extension property is correct, but the complement argument should be written carefully.

I would recommend accepting the proof strategy only after the final write-up states the lemmas below.

---

## 1. Simplification reduction: essentially correct, but must be stated carefully

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The lattice isomorphism
\[
L(M)\cong L(S)
\]
is correct: flats of \(M\) contain all loops and are unions of parallel classes.

The key missing lemma is:

> **Lemma.** If \(Y=\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(\theta\), and every parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(\theta\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class in \(\theta\). Hence \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and
> \[
> \operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
> \]

This is true, but the proof must explicitly use valuated cocircuits: if a nonloop coordinate were always \(\infty\), or if two originally parallel elements were separated by a cocircuit, the inclusion in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) would fail. If they are valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, the two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\neq 0\), again contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

With that lemma, the poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
via the diagonal map \(\Delta\) is valid. The flat embedding is also compatible with simplification by the rank calculation for \(M/F\).

One minor correction: the statement “conversely, every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\)” should be read as “every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), and it lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) exactly when its zero-parallel extension lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).”

---

## 2. Modularity: correct, but use the clean lattice argument

The draft invokes a black-box adjoint theorem. A shorter self-contained argument is preferable.

Since \(L(S)\) is geometric and admits an order-reversing self-isomorphism, \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also geometric. Thus \(L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular. In a finite ranked lattice this forces
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(L(S)\) is modular.

So the simple reduction is indeed to finite simple modular matroids with a polarity.

One caution: the component classification in the draft is too strong if “finite projective geometry” means Desarguesian \(PG(r-1,q)\). For example \(U_{2,7}\) is a connected simple modular matroid with a self-dual rank-two flat lattice, but it is not \(PG(1,q)\) over a finite field. This is not fatal, because the later proof only needs modularity, not field coordinatization.

---

## 3. The coordinate polar \(p^\dagger\): plausible, but final proof needs the full lemma

For a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), the flat-constancy lemma is correct:

> If \(I,J\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), then \(p_I=p_J\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\).

The circuit-incidence proof is fine.

Then the formula
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}
\end{cases}
\]
is well-defined.

The proof that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies Plücker relations is credible, but the final write-up must explicitly handle these cases:

- \(r-k=0,1\): Plücker relations are vacuous.
- In a Plücker relation with base set \(S\), if \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If one of the four added elements lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.
- If two added elements are parallel in \(M/\operatorname{cl}(S)\), this corresponds exactly to repeated hyperplanes in the local dual Plücker lemma.

The local dual Plücker lemma from t4 is the right repair. It should be stated as a standalone lemma for modular intervals.

The quotient proof for \(p^\dagger\) also needs one explicit two-sided version of the codimension-one polarity:

> **Interval codimension-one duality lemma.** Let \(X\) be a flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). If \(v\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\), then the function on atoms of \(M/X\) defined by
> \[
> u(X\vee e)=v(Y\wedge e^\perp)
> \]
> is a tropical point of \(M/X\).

The draft uses this implicitly. It is true, but t2 proves only the self-polar version, so the final write-up should state and prove the interval version.

With those repairs, the claims
\[
p^\dagger\in \operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M),\qquad (p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
are justified.

---

## 4. Well-definedness on tropical linear spaces

This is the main conceptual point.

The formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is written in Plücker coordinates, but \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) was defined as a poset of **tropical linear spaces**. Thus the proof must ensure that if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)=\operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)=\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger).
\]

There are two ways to repair this:

1. cite the theorem that a valuated matroid is determined up to tropical scalar by its tropical linear space; or
2. define the map intrinsically by the point-annihilator formula
   \[
   \Psi(X)=\{x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M): X\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\}.
   \]

The second repair is better. The point-annihilator theorem proves
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
so the output depends only on the tropical linear space, not on the chosen Plücker vector.

---

## 5. Point-annihilator theorem: essentially correct after two clarifications

The apartment argument is good. The key facts needed are:

For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the polar basis
\[
b_i^\star=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Therefore
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids on the Boolean apartment.

Two details need correction in the written proof:

- In the forward direction, for arbitrary \(A\), the vector \((p_{A\cup b_i})_i\) is not necessarily a cocircuit of \(p|_B\). What is true, and sufficient, is that it is the restriction to \(B\) of a point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), hence lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\).
- In the converse direction, the dependent \(J\)-case should be written carefully: if \(|J|=r-k+1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\); if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), the finite \(p^\dagger\)-terms are constant on \(j\in D\), and the circuit condition for \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) gives the required nonunique minimum.

With those clarifications, the point-annihilator theorem is valid:
\[
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

Then order reversal is immediate:
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\implies
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## 6. Flat subposet extension: correct

Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Then \(p^F_I\) is finite exactly when \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\).

Because polarity exchanges complements:
\[
G\text{ complements }F
\iff
G^\perp\text{ complements }F^\perp,
\]
one gets
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Together with the simplification compatibility, this proves the extension property for the original, possibly nonsimple \(M\).

---

## Final referee conclusion

No fatal counterexample found. The proof is sound **provided** the final version explicitly states and proves the following lemmas:

1. simplification invariance of the relative Dressian;
2. modularity of the simplified matroid from self-duality of its flat lattice;
3. flat-constancy for valuated quotients of \(M\);
4. the local dual Plücker lemma, including dependent cases;
5. the interval codimension-one polarity lemma used to prove \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient;
6. apartment duality \(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*\);
7. the point-annihilator theorem;
8. complement compatibility for flat quotients.

The t5 “opposition invariance of valuated flag matroids” should either be fully proved or omitted; it is not needed if the point-annihilator proof is used.

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below is a self-contained proof in the finite simple modular case. I use the convention that a tropical minimum is “attained twice” if it has no unique finite minimizer.

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\), and suppose its flat lattice \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Then:

1. \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Write this value as \(\bar p(F)\).
2. Define a rank-\((r-k)\) function
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).
3. The operation is involutive: \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).
4. If \(p=p^F\) is the constant quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
5. If \(p\) is a quotient of \(q\), then \(q^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(p^\dagger\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## 1. Circuit incidence and flat-constancy

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), being a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\) is equivalent to the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
having no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the usual quotient relation with \(M\) says that for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) and every \((r+1)\)-set \(T\),
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+M_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), then every \(M_{T\setminus i}=\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C_T\), and the finite terms are exactly those with \(i\in C_T\). Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) appears as such a \(C_T\) after extending \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis and adding \(c\).

Now let \(I\) be a \(k\)-set. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I\setminus c\), we have \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Next suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Thus \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.

Boundary ranks are harmless: for \(k=0\), \(\bar p(0)=p_\varnothing\); for \(k=r\), flat-constancy says all \(M\)-bases of \(E\) have the same value.

---

## 2. \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the Plücker relations

The cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate: \(p^\dagger\) is respectively the constant matroid \(M\) or the rank-zero all-loop matroid, up to the scalar \(p_\varnothing\) or \(\bar p(E)\). Assume \(1\le k\le r-1\), and put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

We prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every Plücker sum contains a dependent term involving \(e\), so again the relation is trivial.

Thus assume \(S\) is independent and \(a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
So the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two \(H_i\)’s are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\) and the other two sums are equal, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(L(M)\) is modular, \(H_i\vee H_j=Y\) and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k.
\]
Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j=Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\), \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\), and in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n
\]
whenever \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then, for example,
\[
[12]=p_{A\cup t_3\cup t_4},\qquad [34]=p_{A\cup t_1\cup t_2}.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a simple modular rank-three matroid after simplification. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For any circuit \(D\) in the simplification of \([Z,Y]\), lift its points to elements \(e_P\) with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_P)=P\). The lifted set together with possibly some elements of \(Z\) contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), and applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) shows that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in \operatorname{Trop}([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge fact.

### Rank-three edge fact

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in \operatorname{Trop}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof: For a vector \(w\) on a matroid \(N\), the condition \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) is equivalent to saying that every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. This follows directly from the circuit definition: a non-flat superlevel set gives a circuit with a unique element outside it, hence a unique minimum; conversely, a circuit with a unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set.

In rank three, after subtracting a global constant, the proper nonempty superlevel flats of \(w\) form a flag
\[
a\le B
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, with coefficients \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\):
\[
w(x)=\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]
If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let \(y_i=L_i\wedge B\). Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs of equal colors \(y_i\). For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: the multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case either all matchings tie or at least two avoid the repeated color.

If one \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), set \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\) for \(j=2,3,4\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the edge fact.

Applying the edge fact to the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma, hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

---

## 3. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

It remains to prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1=r-k-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. We must show
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the term is \(\infty\).

We need one codimension-one fact.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((r-k-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp).
\]
Then \(u\in \operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Proof: By the superlevel-flat criterion above, it suffices to show that for every \(\lambda\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P\text{ point of }M/X:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\). Since \(M/X\) is modular, a set of points is a flat iff it is closed under lines. So let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(H_3\) contains \(H_1\wedge H_2\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two contraction over \(B\), the three points \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit. Applying (CI) to \(B\) and this circuit gives
\[
u(P_3)\ge \min(u(P_1),u(P_2)).
\]
Thus if \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), then \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, therefore a flat, and \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Now return to the circuit \(C\). The set \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\): every element \(c\in C\setminus X\) lies in the closure of \((C\setminus X)\setminus c\) in the contraction. Since \(u\in \operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. The elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies (CI), so \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

---

## 4. Involutivity

Let \(I\) be a \(k\)-set. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

---

## 5. Flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat, and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). For a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]
Under the polarity, complements of \(F\) are sent to complements of \(F^\perp\). Indeed,
\[
G\wedge F=0,\ G\vee F=E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
G^\perp\vee F^\perp=E,\ G^\perp\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Therefore \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite exactly on complements of \(F^\perp\), so
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 6. Order reversal

We prove order reversal using an apartmentwise annihilator theorem.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis
\[
b_i^\ast=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp .
\]
Modularity gives, for every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Thus \(B^\ast=\{b_1^\ast,\dots,b_r^\ast\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

Let
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis: start with an \(M\)-basis containing a finite \(p\)-basis and move to any other \(M\)-basis by basis exchanges; if an exchanged-out element lies in the finite \(p\)-basis, the circuit-incidence relation supplies a replacement. Hence \(\mu_B\) is an ordinary rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}=\mu_B^\ast,
\]
because for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus S).
\]

We use the following ordinary valuated-matroid fact.

### Ordinary annihilator fact

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(E\), and let \(\mu^\ast\) be its ordinary valuated dual. For \(u\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E\), define
\[
H_u=\{y:\min_{e\in E}(u_e+y_e)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \operatorname{Trop}(\mu^\ast)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This is the standard circuit-cocircuit duality for valuated matroids: the circuits of \(\mu^\ast\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\), cocircuits lie in and tropically span \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\), and tropical hyperplanes \(H_u\) are tropically convex.

Now let \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Define the polar hyperplane \(\alpha(x)\) by
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},& I\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\ |I|=r-1,\\
\infty,& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is exactly the dagger construction applied to the rank-one quotient \(x\), so \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) quotient of \(M\).

We claim:
\[
\tag{*}
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

First assume \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_i^\ast})_{i=1}^r.
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}=\mu_B^\ast\), we have
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast).
\]
By the ordinary annihilator fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]
Now take \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(p)\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), also \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Its restriction \(y|_B\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^\ast})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the circuit equation for \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\). Dependent \(r\)-subsets impose no additional conditions, because \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Hence
\[
y\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely assume
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and again set \(u_B=(x_{b_i^\ast})\). To prove
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast),
\]
it is enough, by the ordinary annihilator fact, to show that \(u_B\) is orthogonal to every cocircuit of \(\mu_B\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{S\cup b_i},
\qquad |S|=k-1,\ S\subseteq B.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S(e)=p_{S\cup e}.
\]
If \(C_S\) is nonzero, then \(C_S\in\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), hence \(C_S\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\). Testing the defining equation for \(\alpha(x)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\ast}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\perp u_B\). Therefore
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be any \(M\)-independent subset of size \(r-k+1\). Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^\ast\). From the previous paragraph,
\[
x|_{B^\ast}\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}),
\]
so the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\): either all \(J\setminus j\) are dependent, or \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are constant for \(j\in D\), and the relation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\) on \(D\). Hence
\[
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves \((*)\).

Finally, suppose \(p\) is a quotient of \(q\), so
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(q).
\]
If \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\), then by \((*)\),
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)),
\]
and applying \((*)\) again gives
\[
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
i.e. \(q^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(p^\dagger\). This proves order reversal.

Therefore
\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\]
is an order-reversing involution on the relative Dressian of \(M\), extending the given flat-lattice polarity.

---

The claimed final theorem is: for any finite matroid M whose lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, there is an order-reversing involution Phi on the relative Dressian Dr(M) extending this polarity on the embedded flat subposet. Read the attached t7 and t8 outputs carefully. Your job is not to produce a polished proof, but to attack the proof strategy and find any fatal gap or missing hypothesis. Focus especially on: (1) whether every M-basis really contains a finite p-basis for a valuated quotient p of M, so that Boolean apartment restrictions used in the point-annihilator proof are valid; (2) whether ordinary valuated-matroid annihilator duality is being used correctly with infinities and projective tropical spaces; (3) whether the local dual Plucker lemma and codimension-one polarity lemma cover all repeated, dependent, loop, coloop, and boundary-rank cases; (4) whether the simplification reduction is actually a poset isomorphism Dr(si(M)) -> Dr(M), including loops and parallel classes; and (5) whether the flat-extension statement for M/F plus loops on F is exactly compatible with simplification. Try small examples, including rank 0, rank 1, rank 2 uniform matroids, a matroid with a nontrivial parallel class, and direct sums. If you find a counterexample, state it precisely. If you find only repairable gaps, give exact patched statements and proof sketches. End with a verdict: sound, sound after repairs, or false.

task_assignments.description: Rigorous non-simple reduction and simplification invariance
task_assignments.solver_prompt: Produce a standalone rigorous reduction from an arbitrary finite matroid M with a flat-lattice anti-involution to its simplification S = si(M), suitable for insertion into the final proof. Prove the following in detail. First, the flat lattice of M is canonically isomorphic to the flat lattice of S, so the anti-involution descends to S, and S is modular because a finite geometric lattice anti-isomorphic to its opposite is both semimodular and lower semimodular. Second, define the diagonal embedding of projective tropical spaces that identifies coordinates in each parallel class of M and sets loop coordinates to infinity. Prove that it induces a poset isomorphism Dr(S) -> Dr(M). The key missing lemma is valuated parallel-class descent: if q is a valuated matroid whose tropical linear space is contained in Trop(M), then each loop of M is a loop of q, and each parallel class of M is either entirely looped in q or is a zero-offset valuated parallel class in q; hence q descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on S. Prove this using valuated cocircuits or another precise argument. Conversely, prove that zero parallel extension of any relative quotient of S is a relative quotient of M. Third, prove compatibility with the natural flat embedding F -> Trop(M/F plus loops on F). Include all boundary cases: all-loop matroids, rank zero, singleton parallel classes, and classes that become loops in q. State clearly what standard facts about projective tropical linear spaces or valuated matroid quotients you use.

## Task t1 — Structural reduction to modular matroids and simplification issues
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Conventions

Let \(E\) be the ground set of \(M\). Write  
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
for the set of loops. This is the **bottom flat** of \(L(M)\); when \(M\) has loops, the bottom flat is not \(\varnothing\).

Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of parallel classes.

---

# 1. Simplification inherits the order-reversing involution

Every flat \(F\in L(M)\) contains \(L_0\), and if \(e\in F\) then the whole parallel class of \(e\) lies in \(F\). Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly sets of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\longrightarrow L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
In particular,
\[
\psi(L_0)=\varnothing,
\qquad
\psi(E)=\bar E.
\]

Let
\[
\alpha:L(M)\to L(M),
\qquad
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
be the given order-reversing involution. Since \(L_0\) is the bottom flat and \(E\) is the top flat,
\[
\alpha(L_0)=E,
\qquad
\alpha(E)=L_0.
\]
Define
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
Then \(\bar\alpha\) is again an order-reversing involution, and
\[
\bar\alpha(\varnothing)=\bar E,
\qquad
\bar\alpha(\bar E)=\varnothing.
\]

Because \(S\) is simple and \(L(S)\cong L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\), the matroid \(S\) is its own adjoint in the sense of (A1). Therefore, by (A1),

\[
\boxed{\operatorname{si}(M)\text{ is modular}.}
\]

The degenerate case \(S=\varnothing\) is harmless: the one-element lattice is self-dual and the empty matroid is modular.

---

# 2. Component decomposition and action of the involution

By (A3), since \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is simple and modular, its connected components are of the following types:
\[
S=\bigoplus_{i\in I} S_i,
\]
where each \(S_i\) is either

\[
U_{1,1}
\]

or a finite projective geometry
\[
\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i).
\]

The flat lattice decomposes as a product:
\[
L(S)\cong \prod_{i\in I} L(S_i).
\]

Write a flat \(F\in L(S)\) as a tuple
\[
F=(F_i)_{i\in I}.
\]

## Atoms and coatoms

An atom of \(L(S)\) is a flat supported in one component:
\[
(0,\dots,0,p,0,\dots,0),
\]
where \(p\) is a point of some \(S_i\).

A coatom of \(L(S)\) is a flat of the form
\[
(1,\dots,1,H,1,\dots,1),
\]
where \(H\) is a hyperplane of some \(S_j\).

Because \(\bar\alpha\) is order-reversing, it maps atoms bijectively to coatoms.

For points \(p,q\) in the same projective-geometry component \(S_i\), the interval
\[
[0,p\vee q]
\]
is a projective line and has \(q_i+1\ge 3\) atoms. If \(p,q\) lie in different components, then \([0,p\vee q]\) is Boolean of rank \(2\), hence has exactly \(2\) atoms.

Applying \(\bar\alpha\), the interval \([0,p\vee q]\) is anti-isomorphic to
\[
[\bar\alpha(p)\wedge \bar\alpha(q),1].
\]
Thus two atoms in the same projective component must map to coatoms supported in the same component; otherwise the upper interval would have only two coatoms. Conversely, atoms in distinct components cannot all map into the same projective-geometry component, because then the corresponding upper interval would have at least three coatoms.

Therefore \(\bar\alpha\) permutes the connected components.

More precisely, there is an involutive permutation
\[
\sigma:I\to I,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\mathrm{id},
\]
and for each \(i\in I\) an order-reversing lattice isomorphism
\[
\delta_i:L(S_i)\longrightarrow L(S_{\sigma(i)})
\]
such that
\[
\bar\alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\bar\alpha((F_i)_{i\in I})
=
\bigl(\delta_{\sigma^{-1}(j)}(F_{\sigma^{-1}(j)})\bigr)_{j\in I}.
\]

Since \(\bar\alpha^2=\mathrm{id}\), we also have
\[
\delta_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i^{-1}.
\]

## Constraints on paired components

Because \(\delta_i\) reverses ranks, \(S_i\) and \(S_{\sigma(i)}\) must have the same rank. Moreover:

- A \(U_{1,1}\)-component can only be paired with another \(U_{1,1}\)-component.
- A component \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\) can only be paired with another \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a polarity/order-reversing involution of \(L(S_i)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=j\neq i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a projective duality from \(S_i\) to \(S_j\), and \(\delta_j=\delta_i^{-1}\).

In atom/coatom language:

- A point \(p\in S_i\) maps to a hyperplane \(\delta_i(p)\subseteq S_{\sigma(i)}\).
- A hyperplane \(H\subseteq S_i\) maps to a point \(\delta_i(H)\in S_{\sigma(i)}\).

---

# 3. Relationship between \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) and \(\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\)

Work in extended tropical coordinates \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Define the diagonal embedding
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]

Indeed, loops of \(M\) force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and a parallel pair \(e,f\) gives a two-element circuit, forcing \(x_e=x_f\). After imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

Now let \(\theta\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\theta\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The following facts are useful.

### Loop/parallel detection in a valuated matroid

For a valuated matroid \(\theta\):

1. An element \(e\) is a loop of \(\theta\) iff every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\) has \(x_e=\infty\).

2. If \(e,f\) are nonloops and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \{x_e=x_f\}\), then \(e,f\) are parallel in the underlying matroid of \(\theta\), and their valuated parallel offset is zero.

The reason is that valuated cocircuits are points of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\). If \(e,f\) were not parallel, some cocircuit would contain exactly one of them, giving a point with one coordinate finite and the other \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel with nonzero offset, their valuated two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\) to equal that nonzero offset, contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

Applying this to the inclusion
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
we get:

- Every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(\theta\).
- Each parallel class of \(M\) is not split by \(\theta\): it becomes either an all-loop class, or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offsets.

Thus \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\bar E\), obtained by identifying each original parallel class and deleting the original loops. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Conversely, if \(\bar\theta\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), then its zero parallel extension to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops, satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar\theta)
=
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]

Hence the diagonal map induces an isomorphism of posets
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
}
\]
given by
\[
X\longmapsto \Delta(X).
\]
The inverse sends a tropical linear subspace \(Y\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) to \(\Delta^{-1}(Y)\).

A correction to a tempting stronger statement: a quotient may create new parallelisms between different simplified elements. For example,
\[
U_{1,1}\oplus U_{1,1}=U_{2,2}
\]
has quotient \(U_{1,2}\), whose tropical space is the diagonal \(x_1=x_2\). Thus \(\operatorname{Dr}\) does not factor componentwise in any naive way.

---

# 4. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on the ground set \(E\). Similarly define
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on the ground set \(\bar E\).

The matroid \(N_F\) is exactly the zero parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops added back as loops. One way to see this is through ranks. For \(A\subseteq E\setminus F\),
\[
r_{M/F}(A)
=
r_M(A\cup F)-r_M(F).
\]
Since \(M\) is a parallel extension of \(S\),
\[
r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup \bar F),
\qquad
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F),
\]
so
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_{S/\bar F}(\pi(A)).
\]
Adding \(U_{0,F}\) and \(U_{0,\bar F}\) gives the claimed compatibility on the full ground sets.

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
}
\]

Thus the diagram
\[
L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
corresponds under simplification to
\[
L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Moreover, the involution is preserved: if \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) and \(\bar\alpha=\psi\alpha\psi^{-1}\), then
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]

Consequently, existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending \(\alpha\) is equivalent to existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\).

---

# 5. Clean reduced version of the original question

After the reduction, one may assume:

1. \(M\) is loopless and simple.
2. \(M\) is modular.
3. Therefore
   \[
   M=\bigoplus_{i\in I} M_i,
   \]
   where each \(M_i\) is either \(U_{1,1}\) or \(\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i)\).
4. The prescribed flat-lattice involution has the form
   \[
   \alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i),
   \]
   where \(\sigma:I\to I\) is an involution and each
   \[
   \delta_i:L(M_i)\to L(M_{\sigma(i)})
   \]
   is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism.

The remaining question is therefore:

> For a direct sum of singleton components and finite projective geometries, equipped with such a componentwise projective duality on its flat lattice, does there exist an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded flat subposet?

One should not assume \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) factors over connected components: quotients of a direct sum can mix components, as already shown by \(U_{2,2}\twoheadrightarrow U_{1,2}\).

## Referee verdict

I do **not** see a counterexample to the claimed theorem. The strategy appears sound **after several repairs and explicit lemmas are added**. As written, however, the proof is not yet referee-ready: a few statements are either under-proved, slightly false as stated, or rely on hidden standard facts that must be stated precisely.

The most serious issues are **patchable**, not fatal:

1. the simplification reduction needs a precise valuated-parallel-class lemma;
2. the coordinate formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) must be shown to define a map on **tropical linear spaces**, not merely on chosen Plücker vectors;
3. the proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient must explicitly handle dependent and boundary cases;
4. the point-annihilator proof is basically correct but uses apartment restrictions in a way that needs two clarifications;
5. the flat-extension property is correct, but the complement argument should be written carefully.

I would recommend accepting the proof strategy only after the final write-up states the lemmas below.

---

## 1. Simplification reduction: essentially correct, but must be stated carefully

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The lattice isomorphism
\[
L(M)\cong L(S)
\]
is correct: flats of \(M\) contain all loops and are unions of parallel classes.

The key missing lemma is:

> **Lemma.** If \(Y=\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(\theta\), and every parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(\theta\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class in \(\theta\). Hence \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and
> \[
> \operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
> \]

This is true, but the proof must explicitly use valuated cocircuits: if a nonloop coordinate were always \(\infty\), or if two originally parallel elements were separated by a cocircuit, the inclusion in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) would fail. If they are valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, the two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\neq 0\), again contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

With that lemma, the poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
via the diagonal map \(\Delta\) is valid. The flat embedding is also compatible with simplification by the rank calculation for \(M/F\).

One minor correction: the statement “conversely, every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\)” should be read as “every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), and it lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) exactly when its zero-parallel extension lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).”

---

## 2. Modularity: correct, but use the clean lattice argument

The draft invokes a black-box adjoint theorem. A shorter self-contained argument is preferable.

Since \(L(S)\) is geometric and admits an order-reversing self-isomorphism, \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also geometric. Thus \(L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular. In a finite ranked lattice this forces
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(L(S)\) is modular.

So the simple reduction is indeed to finite simple modular matroids with a polarity.

One caution: the component classification in the draft is too strong if “finite projective geometry” means Desarguesian \(PG(r-1,q)\). For example \(U_{2,7}\) is a connected simple modular matroid with a self-dual rank-two flat lattice, but it is not \(PG(1,q)\) over a finite field. This is not fatal, because the later proof only needs modularity, not field coordinatization.

---

## 3. The coordinate polar \(p^\dagger\): plausible, but final proof needs the full lemma

For a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), the flat-constancy lemma is correct:

> If \(I,J\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), then \(p_I=p_J\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\).

The circuit-incidence proof is fine.

Then the formula
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}
\end{cases}
\]
is well-defined.

The proof that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies Plücker relations is credible, but the final write-up must explicitly handle these cases:

- \(r-k=0,1\): Plücker relations are vacuous.
- In a Plücker relation with base set \(S\), if \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If one of the four added elements lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.
- If two added elements are parallel in \(M/\operatorname{cl}(S)\), this corresponds exactly to repeated hyperplanes in the local dual Plücker lemma.

The local dual Plücker lemma from t4 is the right repair. It should be stated as a standalone lemma for modular intervals.

The quotient proof for \(p^\dagger\) also needs one explicit two-sided version of the codimension-one polarity:

> **Interval codimension-one duality lemma.** Let \(X\) be a flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). If \(v\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\), then the function on atoms of \(M/X\) defined by
> \[
> u(X\vee e)=v(Y\wedge e^\perp)
> \]
> is a tropical point of \(M/X\).

The draft uses this implicitly. It is true, but t2 proves only the self-polar version, so the final write-up should state and prove the interval version.

With those repairs, the claims
\[
p^\dagger\in \operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M),\qquad (p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
are justified.

---

## 4. Well-definedness on tropical linear spaces

This is the main conceptual point.

The formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is written in Plücker coordinates, but \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) was defined as a poset of **tropical linear spaces**. Thus the proof must ensure that if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)=\operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)=\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger).
\]

There are two ways to repair this:

1. cite the theorem that a valuated matroid is determined up to tropical scalar by its tropical linear space; or
2. define the map intrinsically by the point-annihilator formula
   \[
   \Psi(X)=\{x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M): X\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\}.
   \]

The second repair is better. The point-annihilator theorem proves
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
so the output depends only on the tropical linear space, not on the chosen Plücker vector.

---

## 5. Point-annihilator theorem: essentially correct after two clarifications

The apartment argument is good. The key facts needed are:

For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the polar basis
\[
b_i^\star=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Therefore
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids on the Boolean apartment.

Two details need correction in the written proof:

- In the forward direction, for arbitrary \(A\), the vector \((p_{A\cup b_i})_i\) is not necessarily a cocircuit of \(p|_B\). What is true, and sufficient, is that it is the restriction to \(B\) of a point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), hence lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\).
- In the converse direction, the dependent \(J\)-case should be written carefully: if \(|J|=r-k+1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\); if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), the finite \(p^\dagger\)-terms are constant on \(j\in D\), and the circuit condition for \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) gives the required nonunique minimum.

With those clarifications, the point-annihilator theorem is valid:
\[
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

Then order reversal is immediate:
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\implies
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## 6. Flat subposet extension: correct

Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Then \(p^F_I\) is finite exactly when \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\).

Because polarity exchanges complements:
\[
G\text{ complements }F
\iff
G^\perp\text{ complements }F^\perp,
\]
one gets
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Together with the simplification compatibility, this proves the extension property for the original, possibly nonsimple \(M\).

---

## Final referee conclusion

No fatal counterexample found. The proof is sound **provided** the final version explicitly states and proves the following lemmas:

1. simplification invariance of the relative Dressian;
2. modularity of the simplified matroid from self-duality of its flat lattice;
3. flat-constancy for valuated quotients of \(M\);
4. the local dual Plücker lemma, including dependent cases;
5. the interval codimension-one polarity lemma used to prove \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient;
6. apartment duality \(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*\);
7. the point-annihilator theorem;
8. complement compatibility for flat quotients.

## Task t8 — Consolidate the core dagger-duality theorem
