## Task t3 — All-rank flat-coordinate polarity theorem
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Here is the strongest coordinate result I can justify cleanly. It proves the candidate polar is an **involutive rank-reversing map on the underlying set** of the relative Dressian and that it extends the flat polarity. The remaining gap is the **mixed-rank flag/order-reversal lemma**, stated precisely at the end.

Throughout, “the minimum is attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimum”; if all relevant terms are \(\infty\), the relation is considered satisfied.

---

## 1. Circuit-incidence and flat-constancy

Let \(p\) be rank \(k\), and let \(M\) be the constant rank \(r\) valuated matroid. The flag incidence relations for \(p\) to be a valuated quotient of \(M\) are equivalent to:

\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\quad\text{has no unique finite minimum}
\]

for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, given \(C\), choose \(U\) so that \(T=C\cup U\) has size \(r+1\) and \(T-i\) is an \(M\)-basis exactly for \(i\in C\). The flag relation for \(A,T\) gives (CI). Conversely, for any \(T\) of size \(r+1\), if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), the incidence relation is trivial; if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique circuit \(C_T\), and the incidence relation reduces to (CI) for \(C_T\).

Now (CI) implies:

### Lemma: \(p_I\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of the same flat.

First, if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(|I|=k\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.

Next, suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit

\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]

containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Hence \(C\setminus A=\{a,b\}\), and (CI) gives

\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]

The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), so \(p_I\) is constant over all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\).

Thus define

\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]

for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of the rank \(k\) flat \(F\). If all such \(I\) have \(p_I=\infty\), then \(\bar p(F)=\infty\).

---

## 2. The coordinate polar and involutivity

Define a rank \(r-k\) candidate \(p^\perp\) by

\[
\boxed{
p^\perp_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp), & J \text{ is } M\text{-independent of size } r-k,\\
\infty, & J \text{ is } M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}}
\]

This is automatically constant on \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(r-k\) flat.

Moreover, once \(p^\perp\) is known to be a valuated quotient of \(M\), the construction is exactly involutive:

\[
(p^\perp)^\perp_I
=
\overline{p^\perp}(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)
=
\bar p((\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(I))
=
p_I.
\]

So there is no scalar ambiguity except the usual projective one: adding a global constant to \(p\) adds the same constant to \(p^\perp\).

It also extends the flat polarity. If \(p^F\) is the constant valuated matroid of

\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]

then \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\). Since \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) iff \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\), one gets

\[
\boxed{(p^F)^\perp=p^{F^\perp}.}
\]

---

## 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\perp\)

The key local statement is the following dual four-hyperplane relation.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define

\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j), & H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty, & H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimum.

Sketch of proof:

- If two \(H_i\)’s are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while

  \[
  [13]+[24]=[13]+[14]=[14]+[23],
  \]

  so the relation is immediate.

- Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put \(T=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4\). Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), one has \(\operatorname{rk}(T)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\).

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(T\), so all three sums are equal.

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k-2\), then in the rank \(4\) interval \([T,Y]\), the four hyperplanes are in general position. Let

  \[
  P_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
  \]

  Then each \(P_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and

  \[
  H_i\wedge H_j=P_m\vee P_n
  \]

  where \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Choosing a basis \(A\) of \(T\) and elements \(x_i\) with \(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup x_i)=P_i\), the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4\) gives exactly the desired relation.

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k-1\), then \([T,Y]\) has rank \(3\). The function

  \[
  w(P)=\bar p(P)
  \]

  on the atoms \(P\) of \([T,Y]\) is a tropical point of that rank-three modular matroid, by the circuit-incidence relations from Section 1. Applying the rank-three codimension-one polarity from t2, the polar rank-two valuated matroid has coordinates

  \[
  u_{ab}=w(\delta(a\vee b)),
  \]

  and its rank-two Plücker relation is precisely the displayed relation for the four hyperplanes \(H_i/T\).

Now take a Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\). Let

\[
S\subseteq E,\qquad |S|=r-k-2,
\]

and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, the relation is trivial. Otherwise put

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),\qquad Y=X^\perp,
\]

so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define

\[
H_i=Y\wedge i^\perp=(X\vee i)^\perp.
\]

Then

\[
p^\perp_{S\cup i\cup j}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j), & S\cup i\cup j \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\
\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local dual Plücker relation above. Hence:

\[
\boxed{p^\perp \text{ is a valuated matroid of rank } r-k.}
\]

---

## 4. \(p^\perp\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\)

It remains to prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\perp\).

Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms \(p^\perp_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\), so assume \(A\) is independent. Put

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]

Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). For \(c\notin X\),

\[
p^\perp_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

The interval \([X,E]\), i.e. the contraction \(M/X\), is anti-isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by

\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]

The restriction of \(p\) to the hyperplanes of \(Y\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\). By the codimension-one polarity from t2, the function

\[
u_X(X\vee c):=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]

is a tropical point of the contraction \(M/X\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Hence a unique finite minimum of \(u_X\) on \(C\setminus X\) would restrict to a unique finite minimum on some circuit of \(M/X\), impossible because \(u_X\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). Therefore

\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\perp_{A\cup c}
\]

has no unique finite minimum. By the equivalence in Section 1, \(p^\perp\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Thus the coordinate construction gives a rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\):

\[
\boxed{
p\longmapsto p^\perp.
}
\]

---

## 5. Remaining missing lemma: full order reversal for arbitrary flags

What is still not proved is the mixed-rank flag statement.

Let \(p,q\) be valuated quotients of \(M\) of ranks \(k\le \ell\), with

\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]

equivalently \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). To prove that the coordinate polar is an order-reversing involution, one must prove

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\perp)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp).
}
\]

In coordinates, the missing statement is:

### Missing flag-duality lemma

For every \(A\subseteq E\) with \(|A|=r-\ell-1\) and every \(B\subseteq E\) with \(|B|=r-k+1\), the minimum

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
q^\perp_{A\cup i}
+
p^\perp_{B\setminus i}
\right)
\]

has no unique finite minimum.

Equivalently, after substituting the coordinate definitions,

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
\bar q(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup i)^\perp)
+
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus i)^\perp)
\right)
\]

with the usual \(\infty\)-convention for dependent sets, should follow from the original flag incidence relations for \(p\subseteq q\).

This lemma would immediately finish the original problem: it would show that

\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)):=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp)
\]

is order-reversing, involutive, and extends \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

I do not see a counterexample. The lemma holds in the Boolean case by ordinary valuated matroid duality, in rank \(\le 3\) by t2, and in the boundary case \(q=M\) by Section 4 above. The unresolved part is the genuinely mixed-rank flag incidence when neither side is the ambient \(M\).

## Task t4 — Local dual Plücker lemma and rank-four stress test
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I believe the local lemma is **true** under the stated hypotheses. The degeneracies collapse into a small number of modular-rank cases; the only genuinely non-Plücker case is a rank-three “edge relation,” which follows from the circuit-incidence relations for the localized rank-one quotient.

Throughout, extend \(p\) to all flats by
\[
p(F)=\infty\quad\text{if } \operatorname{rk}F\ne k.
\]

## Local lemma

Let
\[
K_{ij}:=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(p(K_{12})=\infty\), and the other two sums are identical:
\[
p(K_{13})+p(K_{24})
=
p(K_{23})+p(K_{14}).
\]
So repeated hyperplanes are harmless.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Then \(H_i\vee H_j=Y\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(K_{ij})
=(k+1)+(k+1)-(k+2)=k.
\]
Thus all six \(K_{ij}\) are rank-\(k\) flats.

Put
\[
Z:=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4,
\qquad
d:=\operatorname{rk}(Y)-\operatorname{rk}(Z).
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one in a modular lattice, so \(d\le 4\). Since the \(H_i\) are distinct, \(d\ge 2\). Hence \(d=2,3,\) or \(4\).

### Case \(d=2\)

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\). Since each \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), all six pairwise meets equal \(Z\). Therefore all three displayed sums are equal to \(2p(Z)\).

### Case \(d=4\): the Boolean-position case is forced

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Define
\[
T_i:=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Indeed, intersecting three hyperplanes lowers rank by at most three, so \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k-1\); if \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\).

Moreover,
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z,
\]
and for \(\{a,b,c,d\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
T_a\vee T_b=H_c\wedge H_d.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) so that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Then \(B\cup\{t_a,t_b\}\) spans \(T_a\vee T_b\). Applying the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for the rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) to \(B,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) gives exactly
\[
\min\begin{cases}
p(K_{12})+p(K_{34}),\\
p(K_{13})+p(K_{24}),\\
p(K_{14})+p(K_{23})
\end{cases}
\]
attained at least twice.

So in modular rank \(4\), the supposedly “generic” tetrahedral situation is not merely generic: if the total meet has rank \(k-2\), then the triple meets automatically have rank \(k-1\), and the ordinary Plücker relation applies.

### Case \(d=3\): reduction to a rank-three edge lemma

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\). Contract by \(Z\) inside \(Y\). After simplification, the interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a loopless simple modular rank-three matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) become hyperplanes \(L_i\) of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(K_{ij}\) correspond to points
\[
x_{ij}:=L_i\wedge L_j.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). The contraction \(p/B\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M/B\), unless all relevant values are \(\infty\), in which case the desired relation is trivial. Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), this contraction satisfies the circuit-incidence relations in the rank-three interval. Thus the function
\[
w(x):=p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(P)\).

So it remains to prove the following rank-three edge lemma.

## Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(P\) be a loopless simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(P)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(P\). Then
\[
\min\begin{cases}
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\end{cases}
\]
is attained at least twice.

If some \(L_i=L_j\), this is again trivial because two of the three sums become equal and the repeated meet contributes \(\infty\). So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant, \(w\) has the form
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]
for a flag \(a\le B\), where \(a\) is a point, \(B\) is a hyperplane, and \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\). One-flat cases are included by allowing one coefficient to be \(0\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i:=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) iff \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs whose \(y\)-colors agree. For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case either all three matchings tie or two avoid the repeated color equally.

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\ne a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Hence the minimum is again attained at least twice.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, and therefore the original local lemma.

## Rank \(4\), \(k=2\)

In \(PG(3,q)\), the statement says: for four planes \(\Pi_1,\Pi_2,\Pi_3,\Pi_4\), the six intersection lines satisfy the tropical opposite-edge relation
\[
\min\begin{cases}
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_2)+p(\Pi_3\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_3)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_4)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_3)
\end{cases}
\]
with the minimum attained at least twice.

The cases are exactly:

1. **No common point.** The four planes form a tetrahedron. Their triple intersections are four vertices, and the opposite-edge relation is the ordinary rank-two Plücker relation for those four vertices.

2. **Common point.** Contract the common point. The four planes become four lines in a projective plane, and the relation is the rank-three edge lemma above.

3. **Common line.** All six pairwise intersections are the same line, so all three sums are equal.

4. **Repeated planes.** Two sums are identical, and the repeated meet contributes \(\infty\).

Thus there is no counterexample in \(PG(3,2)\). The same proof covers mixed modular direct sums such as a projective plane plus a point: the interval generated by the four hyperplanes has modular rank \(2,3,\) or \(4\), and the corresponding case above applies.

For the desired rank-four polarity, if \(p\) is rank \(2\), define
\[
p^\perp(L):=p(L^\perp)
\]
on rank-two flats \(L\). For four points \(a_1,a_2,a_3,a_4\), put \(H_i=a_i^\perp\). Then
\[
(a_i\vee a_j)^\perp=H_i\wedge H_j,
\]
so the rank-two Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local lemma for \(p\). Hence the lemma verifies the **middle-rank Plücker part** of the rank-four involution. What remains for the full poset involution is the global incidence/order-reversal statement, not this local Plücker check.

## Task t8 — Consolidate the core dagger-duality theorem
[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
Below is a self-contained proof in the finite simple modular case. I use the convention that a tropical minimum is “attained twice” if it has no unique finite minimizer.

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\), and suppose its flat lattice \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Then:

1. \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Write this value as \(\bar p(F)\).
2. Define a rank-\((r-k)\) function
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).
3. The operation is involutive: \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\).
4. If \(p=p^F\) is the constant quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
5. If \(p\) is a quotient of \(q\), then \(q^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(p^\dagger\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## 1. Circuit incidence and flat-constancy

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), being a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\) is equivalent to the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
having no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the usual quotient relation with \(M\) says that for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) and every \((r+1)\)-set \(T\),
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+M_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), then every \(M_{T\setminus i}=\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C_T\), and the finite terms are exactly those with \(i\in C_T\). Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) appears as such a \(C_T\) after extending \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis and adding \(c\).

Now let \(I\) be a \(k\)-set. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). With \(A=I\setminus c\), we have \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Next suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Since the basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Thus \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.

Boundary ranks are harmless: for \(k=0\), \(\bar p(0)=p_\varnothing\); for \(k=r\), flat-constancy says all \(M\)-bases of \(E\) have the same value.

---

## 2. \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the Plücker relations

The cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate: \(p^\dagger\) is respectively the constant matroid \(M\) or the rank-zero all-loop matroid, up to the scalar \(p_\varnothing\) or \(\bar p(E)\). Assume \(1\le k\le r-1\), and put
\[
n=r-k.
\]

We prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every Plücker sum contains a dependent term involving \(e\), so again the relation is trivial.

Thus assume \(S\) is independent and \(a,b,c,d\notin X:=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(p^\dagger_{Sef}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}=\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]
So the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two \(H_i\)’s are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\) and the other two sums are equal, so the claim is immediate. Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(L(M)\) is modular, \(H_i\vee H_j=Y\) and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k.
\]
Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with each hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j=Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), set
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\), \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\), and in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n
\]
whenever \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then, for example,
\[
[12]=p_{A\cup t_3\cup t_4},\qquad [34]=p_{A\cup t_1\cup t_2}.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) is exactly the desired relation.

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a simple modular rank-three matroid after simplification. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with \(Z<P<Y\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For any circuit \(D\) in the simplification of \([Z,Y]\), lift its points to elements \(e_P\) with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_P)=P\). The lifted set together with possibly some elements of \(Z\) contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), and applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) shows that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in \operatorname{Trop}([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge fact.

### Rank-three edge fact

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in \operatorname{Trop}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Proof: For a vector \(w\) on a matroid \(N\), the condition \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) is equivalent to saying that every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. This follows directly from the circuit definition: a non-flat superlevel set gives a circuit with a unique element outside it, hence a unique minimum; conversely, a circuit with a unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set.

In rank three, after subtracting a global constant, the proper nonempty superlevel flats of \(w\) form a flag
\[
a\le B
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, with coefficients \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\):
\[
w(x)=\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]
If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let \(y_i=L_i\wedge B\). Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs of equal colors \(y_i\). For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: the multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case either all matchings tie or at least two avoid the repeated color.

If one \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\), set \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\) for \(j=2,3,4\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the edge fact.

Applying the edge fact to the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma, hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

---

## 3. \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

It remains to prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1=r-k-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. We must show
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent, and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp .
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
For \(c\in X\), the term is \(\infty\).

We need one codimension-one fact.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((r-k-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp).
\]
Then \(u\in \operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Proof: By the superlevel-flat criterion above, it suffices to show that for every \(\lambda\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P\text{ point of }M/X:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\). Since \(M/X\) is modular, a set of points is a flat iff it is closed under lines. So let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then the \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\), and \(H_3\) contains \(H_1\wedge H_2\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two contraction over \(B\), the three points \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit. Applying (CI) to \(B\) and this circuit gives
\[
u(P_3)\ge \min(u(P_1),u(P_2)).
\]
Thus if \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), then \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, therefore a flat, and \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Now return to the circuit \(C\). The set \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\): every element \(c\in C\setminus X\) lies in the closure of \((C\setminus X)\setminus c\) in the contraction. Since \(u\in \operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. The elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies (CI), so \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

---

## 4. Involutivity

Let \(I\) be a \(k\)-set. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\). If \(I\) is independent, put \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

---

## 5. Flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat, and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). For a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]
Under the polarity, complements of \(F\) are sent to complements of \(F^\perp\). Indeed,
\[
G\wedge F=0,\ G\vee F=E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
G^\perp\vee F^\perp=E,\ G^\perp\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Therefore \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite exactly on complements of \(F^\perp\), so
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 6. Order reversal

We prove order reversal using an apartmentwise annihilator theorem.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis
\[
b_i^\ast=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp .
\]
Modularity gives, for every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Thus \(B^\ast=\{b_1^\ast,\dots,b_r^\ast\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

Let
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis: start with an \(M\)-basis containing a finite \(p\)-basis and move to any other \(M\)-basis by basis exchanges; if an exchanged-out element lies in the finite \(p\)-basis, the circuit-incidence relation supplies a replacement. Hence \(\mu_B\) is an ordinary rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}=\mu_B^\ast,
\]
because for \(|S|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\ast:i\in S\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus S).
\]

We use the following ordinary valuated-matroid fact.

### Ordinary annihilator fact

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(E\), and let \(\mu^\ast\) be its ordinary valuated dual. For \(u\in(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\})^E\), define
\[
H_u=\{y:\min_{e\in E}(u_e+y_e)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \operatorname{Trop}(\mu^\ast)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This is the standard circuit-cocircuit duality for valuated matroids: the circuits of \(\mu^\ast\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\), cocircuits lie in and tropically span \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\), and tropical hyperplanes \(H_u\) are tropically convex.

Now let \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Define the polar hyperplane \(\alpha(x)\) by
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},& I\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\ |I|=r-1,\\
\infty,& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is exactly the dagger construction applied to the rank-one quotient \(x\), so \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) quotient of \(M\).

We claim:
\[
\tag{*}
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

First assume \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_i^\ast})_{i=1}^r.
\]
Since \(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}=\mu_B^\ast\), we have
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast).
\]
By the ordinary annihilator fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]
Now take \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(p)\). Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), also \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Its restriction \(y|_B\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B)\), so
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^\ast})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This is precisely the circuit equation for \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\). Dependent \(r\)-subsets impose no additional conditions, because \(y\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Hence
\[
y\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely assume
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\), and again set \(u_B=(x_{b_i^\ast})\). To prove
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast),
\]
it is enough, by the ordinary annihilator fact, to show that \(u_B\) is orthogonal to every cocircuit of \(\mu_B\). Such a cocircuit has the form
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{S\cup b_i},
\qquad |S|=k-1,\ S\subseteq B.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S(e)=p_{S\cup e}.
\]
If \(C_S\) is nonzero, then \(C_S\in\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), hence \(C_S\in\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\). Testing the defining equation for \(\alpha(x)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\ast}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\perp u_B\). Therefore
\[
u_B\in\operatorname{Trop}(\mu_B^\ast)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be any \(M\)-independent subset of size \(r-k+1\). Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^\ast\). From the previous paragraph,
\[
x|_{B^\ast}\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\ast}),
\]
so the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\): either all \(J\setminus j\) are dependent, or \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are constant for \(j\in D\), and the relation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\) on \(D\). Hence
\[
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves \((*)\).

Finally, suppose \(p\) is a quotient of \(q\), so
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(q).
\]
If \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\), then by \((*)\),
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)),
\]
and applying \((*)\) again gives
\[
x\in\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
i.e. \(q^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(p^\dagger\). This proves order reversal.

Therefore
\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\]
is an order-reversing involution on the relative Dressian of \(M\), extending the given flat-lattice polarity.

---

## Verdict

I did **not** find a counterexample. The theorem looks **sound after repairs**, but the t8 proof is **not referee-ready as written**. The most serious-looking gap—the claim that every \(M\)-basis supports a finite \(p\)-basis—is actually repairable. The main remaining issues are precise conventions and missing lemmas, especially for projective/all-\(\infty\) boundary cases and simplification.

---

## 1. Apartment restriction gap: repairable

The statement

> every \(M\)-basis \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis

is true for a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), but t8’s proof is too compressed.

**Patch.** First, circuit-incidence with \(M\) implies every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\), and connect \(B_0\) to any target \(M\)-basis \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). For a basis exchange \(B'\mapsto B'-b+e\):

- if \(b\notin I\), keep \(I\);
- if \(b\in I\), put \(A=I-b\), and use the fundamental \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq B'\cup e\) containing \(b,e\).  
  The circuit-incidence relation for \(A,C\) has \(p_{A\cup b}<\infty\), so it forces some \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})\) with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Since \(c\in B'-b+e\), replace \(b\) by \(c\).

Thus every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(p|_B\) is a genuine rank-\(k\) valuated matroid. This validates the Boolean-apartment restrictions used in the point-annihilator argument.

---

## 2. Ordinary annihilator duality: needs precise cone/projective conventions

The ordinary annihilator fact is basically correct, but as stated it is ambiguous with infinities.

The safe statement is for **affine tropical cones including the all-\(\infty\) vector**:

\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\iff
\forall y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu),\ 
\min_e(u_e+y_e)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer.}
\]

Then projectivize by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector.

Without this convention, the statement is literally false in small boundary examples. For instance, for a rank-one valuated matroid on two elements, \(u=(\infty,\infty)\) annihilates everything but is not a projective point. Similarly, rank-zero and full-rank matroids require a convention about whether their tropical spaces are empty projectively or represented by the all-\(\infty\) cone.

This is repairable, but the final proof must explicitly handle \(k=0\), \(k=r\), and all-\(\infty\) restrictions.

---

## 3. Local dual Plücker and codimension-one lemmas: no counterexample, but statements need tightening

I do not see a bad repeated/dependent configuration, but several parts must be stated more carefully.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

The repeated-hyperplane case is okay in the actual application: if \(H_i=H_j\), then the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\), and the two remaining matching sums agree.

However, the rank-three edge fact in t8 is **ill-defined if hyperplanes repeat**, because \(w(L_i\wedge L_j)\) is then a value on a line, not on a point. The final statement should either assume the \(L_i\) are distinct, or define the repeated case separately with \([ii]=\infty\). In the application this is harmless because repetitions were already removed.

Boundary ranks also need explicit treatment:

- \(k=0\) and \(k=r\): no local Plücker argument needed.
- \(r-k=0,1\): Plücker relations are vacuous.
- If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If an added element lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), each matching sum has an \(\infty\)-term.
- If two added elements are parallel in \(M/\operatorname{cl}(S)\), this is exactly the repeated-hyperplane case.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

The proof is plausible, but it needs two details:

1. In a simple modular contraction, a set of points closed under lines is a flat. This should be proved, e.g. by induction using modularity.
2. The circuit-lifting step in the contraction must be stated: a circuit of \(M/X\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit contained in the chosen representatives together with \(X\), so circuit-incidence for \(p\) applies.

With those additions, the quotient proof for \(p^\dagger\) appears sound.

---

## 4. Simplification reduction: true, but must be formulated as zero-parallel extension

The simplification reduction is valid, but only after making the following precise.

Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the map to nonloop parallel classes. For a valuated matroid \(q\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), define its zero-parallel extension by

\[
(\Delta q)_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)}, & I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta q)
=
\{x:\ x_\ell=\infty \text{ for loops } \ell,\ 
x_e=x_f \text{ for parallel } e,f,\ 
\bar x\in \operatorname{Trop}(q)\}.
\]

For a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), circuit-incidence with:

- loop circuits \(\{\ell\}\), and
- parallel-pair circuits \(\{e,f\}\)

forces loops to remain loops and parallel classes to remain zero-offset valuated parallel classes. Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to \(\operatorname{si}(M)\). Conversely, zero-parallel extension sends quotients of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) to quotients of \(M\). Therefore

\[
\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]

as posets.

This also fixes the flat compatibility: if \(F\) corresponds to \(\bar F\), then

\[
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{si}(M)/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\bigr)
=
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

So the flat-extension statement survives simplification, including loops and parallel classes.

---

## 5. Small examples checked

No counterexample appears in the requested test cases.

- **Rank 0 / all loops.** Trivial after choosing a convention for the rank-zero tropical space.
- **Rank 1 with one parallel class.** Simplifies to \(U_{1,1}\); the Dressian is a two-element chain, and polarity swaps top and bottom.
- **Rank 2 uniform \(U_{2,m}\).** The lattice is modular and self-dual; dagger acts on rank-one points by the atom polarity, and flat quotients match complements.
- **Nontrivial parallel class.** Circuit-incidence for the two-element circuit \(\{e,f\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}\), so no quotient can split the class or introduce a nonzero offset.
- **Direct sums.** The modular product structure behaves correctly. The polar-basis identity
  \[
  \operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
  =
  \operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}
  \]
  still holds, so the apartmentwise duality argument survives.

---

## Final referee verdict

**Sound after repairs.** I found no fatal counterexample. But the final proof must explicitly add:

1. the \(M\)-basis finite \(p\)-basis lemma;
2. the affine-cone version of ordinary annihilator duality, with projective/all-\(\infty\) conventions;
3. boundary-rank handling for \(k=0,r\);
4. carefully stated repeated/dependent cases in the local dual Plücker lemma;
5. the line-closed-implies-flat argument in modular contractions;
6. the zero-parallel simplification isomorphism;
7. flat-quotient compatibility under simplification;
8. an intrinsic definition of \(\Phi\), preferably via the point-annihilator formula, to avoid dependence on a chosen Plücker vector.

With those repairs, the proof strategy appears valid.

writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Write a fully self-contained proof of the following theorem.

Theorem. Let M be a finite matroid. Let Trop(M) be the projective tropical linear space associated to M, and let Dr(M) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M), ordered by inclusion. Suppose the lattice of flats L(M) has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Under the natural embedding L(M)^op -> Dr(M), F maps to Trop(M/F with F added back as loops). Then there is an order-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) extending F -> F^perp.

Your artifact must be standalone. Define all notation and conventions, including the tropical semifield T = R union {infinity}, projective tropical space, valuated matroids, projective tropical linear spaces, valuated quotients, and the relative Dressian. You may cite as standard the theorem that a valuated matroid quotient p <- q is equivalent to Trop(p) subset Trop(q), but state it precisely.

Structure the proof as follows.

Step 1: Reduction to the simple modular case. Let S = si(M). Prove that L(M) is canonically isomorphic to L(S), the involution descends to S, and S is modular because its geometric lattice is anti-isomorphic to its opposite. Prove that the diagonal zero-parallel-extension map induces a poset isomorphism Dr(S) -> Dr(M), including loops and parallel classes. Prove compatibility with the embedded flat subposets.

Step 2: Simple modular case. Assume M is simple modular of rank r and has a flat-lattice polarity. For a rank k valuated quotient p of M, prove p is constant on M-independent k-subsets with the same M-closure. Define pbar on rank k flats. Define p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J of size r-k and infinity for dependent J.

Step 3: Prove p^dagger is a rank r-k valuated quotient of M. Include the local dual Plucker lemma and the quotient/circuit-incidence lemma, with enough detail for a reader to check repeated/dependent and boundary cases. It is acceptable to package long local verifications as explicitly stated lemmas, but each lemma must be proved or at least reduced to a standard valuated-matroid fact stated precisely.

Step 4: Prove p -> p^dagger is involutive and sends the embedded flat quotient indexed by F to the embedded flat quotient indexed by F^perp.

Step 5: Prove order reversal. Prefer the point-annihilator theorem: for every tropical point x in Trop(M), x lies in Trop(p^dagger) if and only if Trop(p) is contained in the polar hyperplane Trop(x^dagger). Prove this using Boolean apartments and ordinary tropical annihilator duality, with affine-cone conventions for infinity coordinates. Then show that if Trop(p) subset Trop(q), then Trop(q^dagger) subset Trop(p^dagger). This also proves that Phi(Trop(p)) = Trop(p^dagger) is well-defined on tropical linear spaces, not just on chosen Plucker coordinates.

Step 6: Transfer Phi back from S to M via the diagonal isomorphism and conclude the theorem.

Do not refer to previous solver outputs, the shared KB, or this prompt as context. The final write-up should read like a paper proof from scratch. Be honest about any lemma used as standard, and avoid unsupported leaps in the local Plucker and annihilator arguments.

Below is a standalone reduction theorem. I use \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) and allow the all-\(\infty\) point in extended tropical projective space. If one uses the convention that projective tropical space deletes the all-\(\infty\) point, then every occurrence of the rank-zero/all-loop tropical space below should be read as the empty space; the statements and proofs are unchanged.

---

## Standard facts used

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(E\). I use the following standard facts.

1. **Tropical linear space.**  
   \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subset \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}\) is the set of \(x=(x_e)\) satisfying the valuated circuit equations; equivalently, for every valuated circuit \(C\) of \(p\),
   \[
   \min_{e\in E}(C_e+x_e)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

2. **Quotients and containment.**  
   For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same ground set, \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) iff
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q).
   \]
   Thus the relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) may be viewed as the poset of valuated quotients \(q\) of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces.

3. **Coordinate cocircuits.**  
   For each \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), define
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   If \(C_A^p\) is not identically \(\infty\), it is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), hence lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\). If it is identically \(\infty\), it is harmless for all coordinate-equality arguments below.

4. **Loop and zero-parallel extension.**  
   Adding loops and replacing an element by a parallel class with zero offset preserves valuated matroids. If \(\Delta\) is the diagonal map identifying the coordinates in each parallel class and setting loop coordinates to \(\infty\), then
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*p)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(p)).
   \]

---

# Reduction theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops, and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\). Write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
for the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed canonically as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

Suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
\alpha:F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Then:

1. \(\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S)\) canonically, so \(\alpha\) descends to an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(S)\).
2. \(S\) is modular.
3. The diagonal embedding
   \[
   \Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
   \]
   given by
   \[
   (\Delta x)_e=
   \begin{cases}
   \infty,& e\in L_0,\\
   x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0
   \end{cases}
   \]
   induces a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
   \qquad
   X\mapsto \Delta(X).
   \]
4. This isomorphism is compatible with the natural flat embedding
   \[
   F\longmapsto \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
   \]

Thus the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for the loopless simple modular matroid \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

---

## 1. Flat lattices and modularity of \(S\)

Every flat \(F\) of \(M\) contains \(L_0\). Also, if \(e\in F\) and \(f\) is parallel to \(e\), then \(f\in\operatorname{cl}_M(e)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_M(F)=F\). Hence every flat of \(M\) is a union of \(L_0\) and whole non-loop parallel classes.

Define
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Its inverse is
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Indeed, for any \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Therefore \(F\) is closed in \(M\) iff \(\psi(F)\) is closed in \(S\). Thus \(\psi\) is a canonical lattice isomorphism.

The given anti-involution descends by conjugation:
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]
This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now \(S\) is simple, so \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence finite, ranked, atomistic, and semimodular. Because \(\mathcal L(S)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself, \(\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while lower semimodularity gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). In a finite ranked lattice, this rank equality for all pairs implies modularity. Therefore \(S\) is modular.

The all-loop case is harmless: then \(\bar E=\varnothing\), \(S\) is the empty rank-zero matroid, and its one-element lattice is modular.

---

## 2. The diagonal embedding and \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))\)

The map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
sets loop coordinates to \(\infty\) and makes all coordinates inside a parallel class equal. It descends to extended projective tropical spaces because adding a scalar to \(x\) adds the same scalar to all non-loop coordinates of \(\Delta x\), while \(\infty+c=\infty\).

The ordinary matroid \(M\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(S\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops. Therefore, by the standard zero-parallel extension fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]
Concretely, this says:

- if \(e\in L_0\), then every \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=\infty\);
- if \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then every \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=x_f\);
- after imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

---

## 3. Valuated parallel-class descent

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

### 3.1 Loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\)

If \(k=0\), then \(q\) is rank zero and every element is a loop, so there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(e\in L_0\). Since \(e\) is a loop of \(M\), every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=\infty\). Suppose \(e\) were not a loop of \(q\). Then \(e\) lies in some \(q\)-basis \(B\). Put \(A=B\setminus\{e\}\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) satisfies
\[
C_A^q(e)=q_B<\infty.
\]
Since \(C_A^q\in\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), this contradicts the fact that the \(e\)-coordinate is always \(\infty\) on \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Thus every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

### 3.2 Parallel classes of \(M\) are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), so every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) satisfies
\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

For every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\), unless it is identically \(\infty\), in which case the following equality is trivial. Since \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), we get
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]
with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

This has several consequences.

First, \(e\) is a loop of \(q\) iff \(f\) is a loop of \(q\). Thus an entire parallel class of \(M\) either becomes looped in \(q\), or no element of it is looped.

Second, if the class is not looped, no \(q\)-basis can contain two elements from the same original parallel class. Indeed, if \(B\) were a \(q\)-basis containing \(e\) and \(f\), take \(A=B\setminus\{f\}\). Then \(e\in A\), so
\[
q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
\]
while
\[
q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
\]
contradicting \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\).

Third, if \(B=A\cup\{e\}\) is a \(q\)-basis and \(f\) is another element in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
Hence replacing \(e\) by \(f\) preserves both being a basis and its valuation.

Therefore every non-loop original parallel class is, in \(q\), either entirely looped or a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

### 3.3 Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any subset \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
This is independent of the chosen representatives because replacing one representative of a parallel class by another does not change the value of \(q_I\).

If a class of \(M\) became looped in \(q\), then every representative choice involving that class has value \(\infty\), so the corresponding element of \(\bar E\) is a loop of \(\bar q\).

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow by lifting each relation to representatives in \(E\) and applying the Plücker relations for \(q\). Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\) back to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops. Explicitly, for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\),
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)).
\]

This proves the valuated parallel-class descent lemma.

---

## 4. The induced isomorphism \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Define
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
X\mapsto \Delta(X).
\]

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(S)\), let \(q\) be the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\) to \(E\). Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q))
\subseteq
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Hence \(\Delta(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Conversely, let \(Y=\operatorname{Trop}(q)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). By the descent lemma, \(q\) descends to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), and
\[
Y=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)).
\]
Since
\[
Y\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, it follows that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]
Therefore \(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\in\operatorname{Dr}(S)\), and \(Y\) lies in the image of \(\Delta_*\).

Finally, because \(\Delta\) is injective,
\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq\Delta(X_2).
\]
Thus \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

---

## 5. Compatibility with the flat embedding

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid on \(E\)
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]
where the elements of \(F\) are added back as loops. Similarly define the matroid on \(\bar E\)
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]

We claim that \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Indeed, for any \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Using the rank relation between \(M\) and \(S\),
\[
r_M(F\cup A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
and
\[
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F).
\]
Therefore
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is precisely the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops of \(M\) included as loops.

Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

In other words, the diagram
\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}
\hookrightarrow
\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
is carried by \(\Delta\) to the diagram
\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}
\hookrightarrow
\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Moreover,
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]
Hence any order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) of \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\) transfers to
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}
\]
on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), and this \(\Phi_M\) extends the original involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Thus the arbitrary finite matroid case reduces without loss to the loopless simple modular matroid \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

---

## Boundary cases

- If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\bar E=\varnothing\), \(S\) is the empty rank-zero matroid, both flat lattices are one-point lattices, and both relative Dressians have a single rank-zero/all-loop tropical space.
- Rank-zero valuated matroids cause no issue: every element is a loop, and descent gives the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).
- Singleton parallel classes impose no equality beyond copying their coordinate through \(\Delta\).
- If an original parallel class of \(M\) becomes looped in a quotient \(q\), then the corresponding element of \(\bar q\) is simply a loop, and \(\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q))\) sets the whole original class to \(\infty\).

---

I found no counterexample. The Boolean-apartment proof is sound once the affine/all-\(\infty\) conventions and the converse direction are stated carefully.

Below, \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.” Thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

---

## Affine conventions

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(q\) on \(E\), write
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
for the affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector. We use the circuit-equation form:
\[
z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\iff
\forall S\in \binom{E}{d+1},\quad
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(z_e+q_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this says \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\{\infty^E\}\). For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\mathbb T^E\).

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) by the equations
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x
\iff
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\quad
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer},
\]
where
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
If \(x\neq \infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(x^\dagger)\). If \(x=\infty^E\), the same equations give \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

---

## Theorem: affine point-annihilator duality

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(p^\dagger\) be the flat-coordinate polar:
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
\boxed{
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
}
\]

### 1. Quotient incidence and finite bases inside every \(M\)-basis

For \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
The standard valuated-matroid fact is that \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), which is harmless. Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Therefore, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Call this the quotient circuit-incidence relation.

It implies:

1. Every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.
2. If two \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets \(I,I'\) are bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat of \(M\), then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). Hence \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.
3. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

For (3), start with one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\), and connect \(B_0\) to a target \(M\)-basis \(B\) through the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose \(B'=B''+b-e\) is one exchange step, so \(B''=B'-b+e\). If the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), keep it. If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\), and let \(C\subseteq B'\cup e\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit containing \(b,e\). The term \(p_{A\cup b}=p_I\) is finite, so circuit-incidence forces another finite term \(p_{A\cup c}\) with \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup b)\). Such \(c\) lies in \(B''\). Thus \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis.

---

### 2. Polar Boolean apartments

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and set
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),\qquad b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom; because \(M\) is simple, we identify it with an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Indeed, an element lying in every \(H_i\) for \(i\notin S\) has fundamental circuit over \(B\) supported inside \(S\), so it lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\).

Applying the order-reversing involution gives
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
}
\]
In particular, \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

---

### 3. Restriction to an apartment

Define the ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma, \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). For \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})(R)
&=p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)\\
&=p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}\\
&=\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
}
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids.

Also, if \(z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), then \(z|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B)\), because the circuit equations of \(\mu_B\) are among those of \(p\). Similarly, if \(z\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), then \(z|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\).

---

### 4. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), and \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\boxed{
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
}
\]

Indeed, the circuit equations of \(\mu^*\) are exactly orthogonality to the coordinate cocircuits
\[
D_A(i)=\mu_{A\cup i},\qquad |A|=\operatorname{rk}(\mu)-1.
\]
These \(D_A\)’s tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\), including the all-\(\infty\) point, and \(H_u\) is tropically convex. Hence orthogonality to all \(D_A\)’s is equivalent to containing all of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\) in \(H_u\). The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases match this statement exactly: rank \(0\) gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty\}\), while full rank gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), so \(H_u\) contains it only when \(u=\infty^N\).

---

## Proof of the theorem for \(1\le k\le r-1\)

Let \(d=r-k\).

### Forward direction

Assume
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). We must prove \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B=x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also,
\[
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

This holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\). Therefore \(y\in\widetilde H_x\), and hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### Reverse direction

Assume
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(d+1=r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. By the containment assumption,
\[
C_A^p\in \widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is precisely the circuit equation for \(u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup i)
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, this holds after restricting \(x\) to every \(M\)-basis of \(M\).

Now check the circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(d+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since
\[
x|_C\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C),
\]
the ordinary circuit equation on the subset \(J\subseteq C\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<d\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=d\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) is constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer.

Thus all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\) hold, and
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Boundary ranks

### Case \(k=0\)

Then \(p\) has rank \(0\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
Moreover \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant matroid \(M\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), the left side is true, and the right side is true because \(\infty^E\in \widetilde H_x\). So the theorem holds.

### Case \(k=r\)

Then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Its polar \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), so the right side holds.

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus b_i.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Testing \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives a minimum whose only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite; hence \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Varying \(B\) and \(i\), and using that polar bases cover all elements of \(E\), we get \(x=\infty^E\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\iff
x=\infty^E
\iff
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Projectivization

Projective tropical spaces are obtained from affine cones by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. For \(0<k<r\), the affine theorem descends immediately:
\[
[x]\in \Trop(p^\dagger)
\iff
\Trop(p)\subseteq H_x.
\]
The statement is independent of the representative of \(x\), since adding a scalar to \(x\) adds the same scalar to every hyperplane equation.

For \(k=0\), \(\Trop(p)\) is empty projectively, while \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Trop(M)\), so the equivalence is true for every projective \(x\in\Trop(M)\).

For \(k=r\), \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) is empty projectively. If a projective \(x\) had \(\Trop(M)\subseteq H_x\), conicality would imply the affine containment
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
forcing \(x=\infty^E\), impossible projectively. Thus both sides are false for every projective \(x\).

---

## Consequence: order reversal

If
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(q),
\]
and \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\), then by the theorem
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
After projectivization, the same inclusion holds. Combined with \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), this gives the desired order-reversing involution.
