Below, all tropical relations use the convention: **“the minimum is attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.”** Thus a relation with all terms equal to \(\infty\) is satisfied.

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular of rank \(r\), and let \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) be an order-reversing involution of its flat lattice. Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Put \(n=r-k\).

I work with valuated matroids in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), up to the usual addition of a global scalar.

---

## 0. Standard facts used

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) to be a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\), it is equivalent to require the **circuit-incidence relations**
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the valuated flag relation with the constant matroid \(M\) reduces to the unique circuit in each rank-\(r\), size-\((r+1)\) subset. Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit appears as such a fundamental circuit after extending \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis.

Since \(M\) is modular, for flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
The polarity satisfies
\[
(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp,\qquad
(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

---

## 1. Flat-constancy of \(p\)

First, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup a,\qquad I'=A\cup b
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), we get
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\).

The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

## 2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) for some \(M\)-independent \(k\)-set \(I\), let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to the scalar \(p_\varnothing\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the rank-zero valuated matroid, up to the scalar \(\bar p(E)\).

So assume \(0<k<r\) unless stated otherwise.

---

# 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It suffices to check the standard three-term tropical Plücker relations. Let \(|S|=n-2\) and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(S\cup i\cup j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set involving \(e\), so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then for distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which means \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent.

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

---

## 3.1 Local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Repeated hyperplanes

If some two hyperplanes coincide, relabel so that \(H_1=H_2\). Then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],\qquad [14]=[24].
\]
Hence
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23].
\]
Thus either both of those sums are finite and equal, or all three sums are \(\infty\). So the relation holds.

Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

For \(i\neq j\), since \(H_i,H_j\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\), we have
\[
H_i\vee H_j=Y.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)
=
(k+1)+(k+1)-(k+2)
=
k.
\]

Set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

We handle these three cases.

---

### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\)

Each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank-\(k\) flat containing \(Z\), so
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z
\]
for all \(i\neq j\). Therefore all three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

---

### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is contained in a pairwise meet, hence has rank at most \(k\), while intersecting three hyperplanes lowers rank by at most three, hence gives rank at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k\), then
\[
Z=T_i\wedge H_i
\]
would have rank at least \(k-1\), contradiction.

Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Moreover, \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is independent and spans \(T_m\vee T_n\). Therefore
\[
p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
=
\bar p(T_m\vee T_n).
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

---

### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\)

Consider the rank-three interval
\[
[Z,Y].
\]
Let \(N\) be its simplification: the elements of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid.

Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P)
\]
for each point \(P\) of \(N\).

We first prove that
\[
w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). Since \(D\) is a circuit in the simplification of the contraction by \(Z\), the chosen representatives form a circuit in the contraction, hence lift to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) and the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), because \(A\cup z\) is \(M\)-dependent for \(z\in Z\). For \(e_P\), we have
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\).

Now the images
\[
L_i=H_i/Z
\]
are hyperplanes of \(N\), and
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) of \(N\). Therefore the desired relation follows from the following rank-three edge lemma.

---

## 3.2 Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two \(L_i\)’s coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies. So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

We use the standard superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans: for a vector \(w\), the condition \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) is equivalent to saying that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global constant so that \(\min_x w_x=0\). Since \(N\) has rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane, possibly with one of the two levels absent. Thus there exist \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]
Here \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) covers the cases where one level is absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes a nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\).

For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique. The color multiplicities are one of
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In each case either all matchings tie, or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum. This remains true when some costs are \(\infty\).

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal \(\beta\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two of the \(y_j\)’s are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Therefore the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, hence the local four-hyperplane lemma, hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated matroid.

---

# 4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, we must show:

For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\tag{\(\dagger\)-CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent.

Set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For \(c\notin X\), the flat \(X\vee c\) is a point of the contraction \(M/X\), and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent and the term is \(\infty\).

Thus we need the following codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.1 Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We first show that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\). For \(i=1,2,3\), choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Hence, by circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\) and the circuit \(D\). Terms coming from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces
\[
u(P_3)\ge\lambda.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

It remains to recall that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat.

Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed. We prove that every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(S)\) lies in \(S\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S\) be an independent set spanning some flat \(F\subseteq\operatorname{cl}(S)\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(S\).

For \(m\le 2\), this is exactly line-closedness. Assume \(m\ge 3\). Let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of
\[
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
If \(p\le G\), we are done. If \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise, consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
By modularity inside \(F\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\). By induction, \(q\in S\), and \(b_m\in S\). Since \(S\) is line-closed, every point on the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(S\). Thus \(S\) is a flat.

Hence each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), so \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). This proves the codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.2 Applying the codimension-one lemma to circuits of \(M\)

Return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and the independent \((n-1)\)-set \(A\), with \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\).

Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). On \(C\setminus X\), the terms are exactly the lift of the tropical vector \(u\) from the contraction \(M/X\):
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(X\vee c).
\]

The vector \(u\) is in the Bergman fan of the simplification of \(M/X\), and its lift is in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\): parallel elements have equal values, and loop elements have value \(\infty\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Indeed, for every \(e\in C\setminus X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus e),
\]
and after contracting \(X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus e\bigr).
\]
Hence \(e\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus X\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then choosing a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would give a circuit on which \(u\) has a unique finite minimum, contradicting \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

# 5. Involutivity

Since \(p^\dagger\) is now known to be a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\) as well.

Let \(I\) be a \(k\)-subset.

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty
\]
and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.}
\]

---

# 6. Compatibility with ordinary flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\) satisfies
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), namely
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]

Let \(H\) be a rank-\(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when
\[
H^\perp
\]
is a complement of \(F\). By polarity,
\[
H^\perp\wedge F=0,\quad H^\perp\vee F=E
\]
is equivalent to
\[
H\vee F^\perp=E,\quad H\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Thus \(H\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}}
\]
with the same normalization of finite coordinates.

---

Hence, in the finite simple modular case, the coordinate construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is fully rigorous: it sends valuated quotients of \(M\) to valuated quotients of \(M\), satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, satisfies the quotient incidence relations with \(M\), is involutive, and sends ordinary flat quotients indexed by \(F\) to those indexed by \(F^\perp\).

Below is a standalone reduction theorem. I use \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) and allow the all-\(\infty\) point in extended tropical projective space. If one uses the convention that projective tropical space deletes the all-\(\infty\) point, then every occurrence of the rank-zero/all-loop tropical space below should be read as the empty space; the statements and proofs are unchanged.

---

## Standard facts used

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(E\). I use the following standard facts.

1. **Tropical linear space.**  
   \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subset \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}\) is the set of \(x=(x_e)\) satisfying the valuated circuit equations; equivalently, for every valuated circuit \(C\) of \(p\),
   \[
   \min_{e\in E}(C_e+x_e)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

2. **Quotients and containment.**  
   For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same ground set, \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) iff
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q).
   \]
   Thus the relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) may be viewed as the poset of valuated quotients \(q\) of the constant valuated matroid \(M\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces.

3. **Coordinate cocircuits.**  
   For each \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), define
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   If \(C_A^p\) is not identically \(\infty\), it is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), hence lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\). If it is identically \(\infty\), it is harmless for all coordinate-equality arguments below.

4. **Loop and zero-parallel extension.**  
   Adding loops and replacing an element by a parallel class with zero offset preserves valuated matroids. If \(\Delta\) is the diagonal map identifying the coordinates in each parallel class and setting loop coordinates to \(\infty\), then
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*p)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(p)).
   \]

---

# Reduction theorem

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops, and let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\). Write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
for the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed canonically as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

Suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
\alpha:F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
Then:

1. \(\mathcal L(M)\cong \mathcal L(S)\) canonically, so \(\alpha\) descends to an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(S)\).
2. \(S\) is modular.
3. The diagonal embedding
   \[
   \Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
   \]
   given by
   \[
   (\Delta x)_e=
   \begin{cases}
   \infty,& e\in L_0,\\
   x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0
   \end{cases}
   \]
   induces a poset isomorphism
   \[
   \operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
   \qquad
   X\mapsto \Delta(X).
   \]
4. This isomorphism is compatible with the natural flat embedding
   \[
   F\longmapsto \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
   \]

Thus the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for the loopless simple modular matroid \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

---

## 1. Flat lattices and modularity of \(S\)

Every flat \(F\) of \(M\) contains \(L_0\). Also, if \(e\in F\) and \(f\) is parallel to \(e\), then \(f\in\operatorname{cl}_M(e)\subseteq\operatorname{cl}_M(F)=F\). Hence every flat of \(M\) is a union of \(L_0\) and whole non-loop parallel classes.

Define
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Its inverse is
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Indeed, for any \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Therefore \(F\) is closed in \(M\) iff \(\psi(F)\) is closed in \(S\). Thus \(\psi\) is a canonical lattice isomorphism.

The given anti-involution descends by conjugation:
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]
This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now \(S\) is simple, so \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence finite, ranked, atomistic, and semimodular. Because \(\mathcal L(S)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself, \(\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while lower semimodularity gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). In a finite ranked lattice, this rank equality for all pairs implies modularity. Therefore \(S\) is modular.

The all-loop case is harmless: then \(\bar E=\varnothing\), \(S\) is the empty rank-zero matroid, and its one-element lattice is modular.

---

## 2. The diagonal embedding and \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))\)

The map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
sets loop coordinates to \(\infty\) and makes all coordinates inside a parallel class equal. It descends to extended projective tropical spaces because adding a scalar to \(x\) adds the same scalar to all non-loop coordinates of \(\Delta x\), while \(\infty+c=\infty\).

The ordinary matroid \(M\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(S\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops. Therefore, by the standard zero-parallel extension fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]
Concretely, this says:

- if \(e\in L_0\), then every \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=\infty\);
- if \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then every \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=x_f\);
- after imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

---

## 3. Valuated parallel-class descent

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

### 3.1 Loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\)

If \(k=0\), then \(q\) is rank zero and every element is a loop, so there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(e\in L_0\). Since \(e\) is a loop of \(M\), every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) has \(x_e=\infty\). Suppose \(e\) were not a loop of \(q\). Then \(e\) lies in some \(q\)-basis \(B\). Put \(A=B\setminus\{e\}\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) satisfies
\[
C_A^q(e)=q_B<\infty.
\]
Since \(C_A^q\in\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), this contradicts the fact that the \(e\)-coordinate is always \(\infty\) on \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Thus every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

### 3.2 Parallel classes of \(M\) are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), so every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) satisfies
\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

For every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\), unless it is identically \(\infty\), in which case the following equality is trivial. Since \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), we get
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]
with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

This has several consequences.

First, \(e\) is a loop of \(q\) iff \(f\) is a loop of \(q\). Thus an entire parallel class of \(M\) either becomes looped in \(q\), or no element of it is looped.

Second, if the class is not looped, no \(q\)-basis can contain two elements from the same original parallel class. Indeed, if \(B\) were a \(q\)-basis containing \(e\) and \(f\), take \(A=B\setminus\{f\}\). Then \(e\in A\), so
\[
q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
\]
while
\[
q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
\]
contradicting \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\).

Third, if \(B=A\cup\{e\}\) is a \(q\)-basis and \(f\) is another element in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
\]
Hence replacing \(e\) by \(f\) preserves both being a basis and its valuation.

Therefore every non-loop original parallel class is, in \(q\), either entirely looped or a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

### 3.3 Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any subset \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
This is independent of the chosen representatives because replacing one representative of a parallel class by another does not change the value of \(q_I\).

If a class of \(M\) became looped in \(q\), then every representative choice involving that class has value \(\infty\), so the corresponding element of \(\bar E\) is a loop of \(\bar q\).

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) follow by lifting each relation to representatives in \(E\) and applying the Plücker relations for \(q\). Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\) back to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops. Explicitly, for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\),
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)).
\]

This proves the valuated parallel-class descent lemma.

---

## 4. The induced isomorphism \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Define
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
X\mapsto \Delta(X).
\]

If \(X=\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(S)\), let \(q\) be the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\) to \(E\). Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q))
\subseteq
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Hence \(\Delta(X)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Conversely, let \(Y=\operatorname{Trop}(q)\in\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). By the descent lemma, \(q\) descends to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), and
\[
Y=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)).
\]
Since
\[
Y\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S))
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, it follows that
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq\operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]
Therefore \(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q)\in\operatorname{Dr}(S)\), and \(Y\) lies in the image of \(\Delta_*\).

Finally, because \(\Delta\) is injective,
\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq\Delta(X_2).
\]
Thus \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

---

## 5. Compatibility with the flat embedding

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in\mathcal L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid on \(E\)
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]
where the elements of \(F\) are added back as loops. Similarly define the matroid on \(\bar E\)
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]

We claim that \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Indeed, for any \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
Using the rank relation between \(M\) and \(S\),
\[
r_M(F\cup A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]
and
\[
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F).
\]
Therefore
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is precisely the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops of \(M\) included as loops.

Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

In other words, the diagram
\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}
\hookrightarrow
\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
is carried by \(\Delta\) to the diagram
\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}
\hookrightarrow
\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

Moreover,
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]
Hence any order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) of \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\) transfers to
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}
\]
on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), and this \(\Phi_M\) extends the original involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

Thus the arbitrary finite matroid case reduces without loss to the loopless simple modular matroid \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

---

## Boundary cases

- If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\bar E=\varnothing\), \(S\) is the empty rank-zero matroid, both flat lattices are one-point lattices, and both relative Dressians have a single rank-zero/all-loop tropical space.
- Rank-zero valuated matroids cause no issue: every element is a loop, and descent gives the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).
- Singleton parallel classes impose no equality beyond copying their coordinate through \(\Delta\).
- If an original parallel class of \(M\) becomes looped in a quotient \(q\), then the corresponding element of \(\bar q\) is simply a loop, and \(\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar q))\) sets the whole original class to \(\infty\).

---

## Verdict

I did **not** find a counterexample. The theorem looks **sound after repairs**, but the t8 proof is **not referee-ready as written**. The most serious-looking gap—the claim that every \(M\)-basis supports a finite \(p\)-basis—is actually repairable. The main remaining issues are precise conventions and missing lemmas, especially for projective/all-\(\infty\) boundary cases and simplification.

---

## 1. Apartment restriction gap: repairable

The statement

> every \(M\)-basis \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis

is true for a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), but t8’s proof is too compressed.

**Patch.** First, circuit-incidence with \(M\) implies every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. Choose one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\), and connect \(B_0\) to any target \(M\)-basis \(B\) in the basis graph of \(M\). For a basis exchange \(B'\mapsto B'-b+e\):

- if \(b\notin I\), keep \(I\);
- if \(b\in I\), put \(A=I-b\), and use the fundamental \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq B'\cup e\) containing \(b,e\).  
  The circuit-incidence relation for \(A,C\) has \(p_{A\cup b}<\infty\), so it forces some \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup\{b\})\) with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Since \(c\in B'-b+e\), replace \(b\) by \(c\).

Thus every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(p|_B\) is a genuine rank-\(k\) valuated matroid. This validates the Boolean-apartment restrictions used in the point-annihilator argument.

---

## 2. Ordinary annihilator duality: needs precise cone/projective conventions

The ordinary annihilator fact is basically correct, but as stated it is ambiguous with infinities.

The safe statement is for **affine tropical cones including the all-\(\infty\) vector**:

\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\iff
\forall y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu),\ 
\min_e(u_e+y_e)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer.}
\]

Then projectivize by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector.

Without this convention, the statement is literally false in small boundary examples. For instance, for a rank-one valuated matroid on two elements, \(u=(\infty,\infty)\) annihilates everything but is not a projective point. Similarly, rank-zero and full-rank matroids require a convention about whether their tropical spaces are empty projectively or represented by the all-\(\infty\) cone.

This is repairable, but the final proof must explicitly handle \(k=0\), \(k=r\), and all-\(\infty\) restrictions.

---

## 3. Local dual Plücker and codimension-one lemmas: no counterexample, but statements need tightening

I do not see a bad repeated/dependent configuration, but several parts must be stated more carefully.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

The repeated-hyperplane case is okay in the actual application: if \(H_i=H_j\), then the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\), and the two remaining matching sums agree.

However, the rank-three edge fact in t8 is **ill-defined if hyperplanes repeat**, because \(w(L_i\wedge L_j)\) is then a value on a line, not on a point. The final statement should either assume the \(L_i\) are distinct, or define the repeated case separately with \([ii]=\infty\). In the application this is harmless because repetitions were already removed.

Boundary ranks also need explicit treatment:

- \(k=0\) and \(k=r\): no local Plücker argument needed.
- \(r-k=0,1\): Plücker relations are vacuous.
- If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If an added element lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), each matching sum has an \(\infty\)-term.
- If two added elements are parallel in \(M/\operatorname{cl}(S)\), this is exactly the repeated-hyperplane case.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

The proof is plausible, but it needs two details:

1. In a simple modular contraction, a set of points closed under lines is a flat. This should be proved, e.g. by induction using modularity.
2. The circuit-lifting step in the contraction must be stated: a circuit of \(M/X\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit contained in the chosen representatives together with \(X\), so circuit-incidence for \(p\) applies.

With those additions, the quotient proof for \(p^\dagger\) appears sound.

---

## 4. Simplification reduction: true, but must be formulated as zero-parallel extension

The simplification reduction is valid, but only after making the following precise.

Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the map to nonloop parallel classes. For a valuated matroid \(q\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), define its zero-parallel extension by

\[
(\Delta q)_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)}, & I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta q)
=
\{x:\ x_\ell=\infty \text{ for loops } \ell,\ 
x_e=x_f \text{ for parallel } e,f,\ 
\bar x\in \operatorname{Trop}(q)\}.
\]

For a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), circuit-incidence with:

- loop circuits \(\{\ell\}\), and
- parallel-pair circuits \(\{e,f\}\)

forces loops to remain loops and parallel classes to remain zero-offset valuated parallel classes. Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to \(\operatorname{si}(M)\). Conversely, zero-parallel extension sends quotients of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) to quotients of \(M\). Therefore

\[
\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]

as posets.

This also fixes the flat compatibility: if \(F\) corresponds to \(\bar F\), then

\[
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{si}(M)/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\bigr)
=
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

So the flat-extension statement survives simplification, including loops and parallel classes.

---

## 5. Small examples checked

No counterexample appears in the requested test cases.

- **Rank 0 / all loops.** Trivial after choosing a convention for the rank-zero tropical space.
- **Rank 1 with one parallel class.** Simplifies to \(U_{1,1}\); the Dressian is a two-element chain, and polarity swaps top and bottom.
- **Rank 2 uniform \(U_{2,m}\).** The lattice is modular and self-dual; dagger acts on rank-one points by the atom polarity, and flat quotients match complements.
- **Nontrivial parallel class.** Circuit-incidence for the two-element circuit \(\{e,f\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}\), so no quotient can split the class or introduce a nonzero offset.
- **Direct sums.** The modular product structure behaves correctly. The polar-basis identity
  \[
  \operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
  =
  \operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}
  \]
  still holds, so the apartmentwise duality argument survives.

---

## Final referee verdict

**Sound after repairs.** I found no fatal counterexample. But the final proof must explicitly add:

1. the \(M\)-basis finite \(p\)-basis lemma;
2. the affine-cone version of ordinary annihilator duality, with projective/all-\(\infty\) conventions;
3. boundary-rank handling for \(k=0,r\);
4. carefully stated repeated/dependent cases in the local dual Plücker lemma;
5. the line-closed-implies-flat argument in modular contractions;
6. the zero-parallel simplification isomorphism;
7. flat-quotient compatibility under simplification;
8. an intrinsic definition of \(\Phi\), preferably via the point-annihilator formula, to avoid dependence on a chosen Plücker vector.

With those repairs, the proof strategy appears valid.

Assume its lattice of flats L(M) admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Let Dr(M) be the poset, ordered by inclusion, of projective tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M), equivalently the projectivized valuated matroid quotients of the constant valuated matroid M. Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) such that for every flat F of M, Phi sends the embedded flat tropical linear space Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) to Trop(M/F^perp ⊕ U_{0,F^perp}). Requirements: define all notation and conventions inline, including the tropical semiring, affine cones versus projective tropical spaces, valuated matroid quotients, and the convention that a tropical minimum condition means no unique finite minimizer. State explicitly the standard facts you use: containment of tropical linear spaces is equivalent to the valuated quotient relation, and a tropical linear space determines its valuated Pluecker vector up to global scalar. First, reduce to the simplification S = si(M): prove or clearly state with proof that the diagonal zero-parallel-extension map gives a poset isomorphism Dr(S) -> Dr(M), compatible with flat quotients and the induced flat-lattice involution. Also prove that S is modular because L(S) is geometric and anti-isomorphic to its opposite. Second, handle the finite simple modular case. For a rank k valuated quotient p of M, prove flat-constancy on rank-k flats and define p-dagger on independent rank r-k subsets J by pbar(cl_M(J)^perp), with infinity on dependent subsets. Include the key lemmas showing p-dagger satisfies the tropical Pluecker relations and the quotient circuit-incidence relations with M; it is acceptable to organize these as named sublemmas, but their proofs must be sufficiently detailed for a referee. Include repeated hyperplanes, dependent subsets, and boundary ranks. Prove involutivity and compatibility with flat quotients. Third, prove order reversal using the affine point-annihilator theorem: define the polar hyperplane H_x for x in the affine cone of Trop(M), prove that x is in Trop(p-dagger) if and only if the affine cone Trop(p) is contained in H_x using Boolean apartments and ordinary tropical annihilator duality, and then deduce inclusion reversal. Finally transfer the simple modular result back to the original M through the simplification isomorphism. which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

## Referee verdict

I do **not** find a fatal mathematical gap or a counterexample. The claimed theorem appears correct, provided the final proof explicitly imports a few standard valuated-matroid facts and tightens several boundary/convention points. As written, the proof is not yet publication-ready because some key facts are used implicitly, especially uniqueness of Plücker coordinates from a tropical linear space, the quotient/containment theorem, and affine-versus-projective rank-zero conventions.

Below is the adversarial check by topic.

---

## 1. Standard theorem: containment, quotients, and uniqueness

This is the most important external input. The proof is valid only if the following theorem is stated or cited:

**Needed theorem.** For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set \(E\), using affine tropical cones \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\):

1. \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q)\) iff \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\).
2. Equivalently, if \(q\) is the constant valuated matroid of an ordinary matroid \(M\), then \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((\operatorname{rk}p-1)\)-subset \(A\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.
3. The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde L(p)\) determines \(p\) up to global tropical scalar.

Without item 3, the map
\[
L(p)\longmapsto L(p^\dagger)
\]
would not be well-defined on \(\Dr(M)\) viewed as a poset of subsets. With item 3, scalar ambiguity is harmless because \(p\mapsto p+c\) sends \(p^\dagger\mapsto p^\dagger+c\), hence does not change the tropical linear space.

The rank-zero/projective-empty case must be handled explicitly: affine rank-zero spaces are \(\{\infty^E\}\), while projectively they become empty.

---

## 2. Non-simple reduction

The reduction through simplification is essentially correct, but one useful missing line should be inserted.

Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loop set \(L_0\) and non-loop parallel classes \(\bar E\). The diagonal map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
sets loop coordinates to \(\infty\) and identifies coordinates inside each parallel class.

The draft correctly proves that \(\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S))\). For the descent of a quotient \(q\) of \(M\), add the following patch:

If \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(I\) contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus c\). The coordinate cocircuit
\[
D_A^q(e)=q_{A\cup e}
\]
has a unique finite minimum on \(C\), namely at \(c\), contradicting \(D_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. This also rules out loops and multiple representatives from a parallel class; the later equality argument then proves zero-offset parallelism.

The remaining descent argument is sound:

- loops of \(M\) remain loops in \(q\);
- parallel classes of \(M\) are either entirely looped in \(q\) or remain zero-offset valuated parallel classes;
- therefore \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(S\);
- \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q))\);
- injectivity of \(\Delta\) gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S)\).

The compatibility with flat quotients is also correct via the rank formula
\[
r_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]
This shows that \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of the corresponding quotient for \(S\).

Projectivization is harmless if the proof first works with affine cones including \(\infty^E\), then deletes \(\infty^E\). The all-loop case should be stated separately.

---

## 3. Modularity of the simplification

This part is correct.

For the simple loopless simplification \(S\), \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular. An order-reversing involution \(\alpha\) implies
\[
\rho(\alpha F)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(\alpha X,\alpha Y\) gives the reverse inequality to ordinary semimodularity:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Together with semimodularity, equality holds for all \(X,Y\). Thus \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular, so \(S\) is a modular matroid.

No gap here.

---

## 4. The \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) construction

The construction in the simple modular case is the technical heart. I find it sound, but the final proof should isolate the following lemmas.

### 4.1 Flat constancy

For a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), the value \(p_I\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat. The proof using circuit-incidence is correct. Also, every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-set has \(p_I=\infty\).

Thus \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined for rank-\(k\) flats \(F\).

### 4.2 Definition

For \(|J|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,&J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is well-defined and nonzero.

Boundary ranks are correct:

- \(k=0\): \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to scalar.
- \(k=r\): \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero.

### 4.3 Plücker relations

The proof via the local four-hyperplane lemma is acceptable. The repeated-hyperplane case is handled correctly by assigning \([ij]=\infty\).

The rank-three edge lemma is also correct, but the final proof should explicitly state the superlevel-flat criterion with the convention “minimum has no unique finite minimizer.” For a Bergman vector \(w\), the sets
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
are flats. In rank three, this forces \(w\) to be supported on a point-line flag, possibly with infinite weights. The matching-cost argument for four lines is then valid.

### 4.4 Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

The codimension-one polarity lemma is sound. The line-closed-superlevel argument is correct because in a finite simple modular matroid, line-closed sets of points are flats.

Minor wording fix: when an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) lies entirely in \(X=\operatorname{cl}(A)\), the desired incidence relation is automatic because all terms are \(\infty\). If \(C\setminus X\neq\varnothing\), the contraction argument works.

### 4.5 Involutivity and flat compatibility

The proof of
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
is correct.

The compatibility
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
for ordinary flat quotients is also correct. The complement condition uses modularity:
\[
G\text{ complements }F
\iff
G\wedge F=0,\quad G\vee F=E.
\]

---

## 5. Point-annihilator theorem and order reversal

This proof is sound, with two required clarifications.

First, the ordinary annihilator duality must be stated as a lemma:

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on \(N\),
\[
u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This follows because the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\) tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\), and the hyperplane \(H_u\) is tropically convex. The rank-zero and full-rank cases must be included.

Second, in the converse direction, the proof correctly avoids the false need to extend every point of \(\Trop(\mu_B)\) to a point of \(\Trop(p)\). Instead, it uses global coordinate cocircuits of \(p\). That is the right argument.

The Boolean-apartment calculation is correct:

\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]

Thus \(B^*\) is an \(M\)-basis and
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

Minor wording fix in the dependent-\(J\) part: when \(J\) has rank \(d\) and size \(d+1\), it has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). For \(j\in C\), the terms \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) are equal, but they may all be \(\infty\). If they are finite, the equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation for \(x\); if they are \(\infty\), it is automatic.

The affine-to-projective passage is correct if rank-zero spaces are treated as projectively empty.

---

## Final assembly

In the simple loopless modular case, define
\[
\Phi(\Trop(p))=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined by Plücker uniqueness, maps \(\Dr(M)\) to itself by the quotient proof, is involutive by \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), reverses inclusion by the point-annihilator theorem, and sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

For a general finite matroid \(M\), pass to the simplification \(S\), use the diagonal isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\cong \Dr(M),
\]
construct the involution on \(\Dr(S)\), and conjugate back. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet follows from the flat-quotient compatibility of the diagonal map.

So my referee conclusion is: **the theorem is correct after the above clarifications and minor patches. I find no counterexample.**

---

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

## Simplification reduction

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let

\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]

be its loop set, let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let

\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]

be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Work first with affine tropical cones \(\widetilde{\Trop}(-)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\), \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Projectivization is obtained by quotienting by \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\). If one deletes the all-\(\infty\) point projectively, the rank-zero spaces below become empty on both sides; the statements remain unchanged.

Define

\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]

by

\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

This commutes with tropical scaling:

\[
\Delta(x+\lambda\mathbf 1)=\Delta(x)+\lambda\mathbf 1,
\]

so it descends to tropical projective space. It is injective, except for the harmless all-loop degenerate case where both sides have only the rank-zero object.

---

### 1. Zero-parallel extension of valuated matroids

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta \bar q\) on \(E\) by

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]

for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\).

This is exactly the valuated matroid obtained from \(\bar q\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each class \(a\in \bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\). Indeed, in a Plücker relation indexed by \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\) and \(B\in \binom{E}{k+1}\), a term can be finite only if both \(A\cup b\) and \(B-b\) avoid loops and meet every original parallel class at most once. If \(B\) has no repeated class, the relation is the projected relation for \(\bar q\). If \(B\) has exactly one repeated class, the only possibly finite terms are the two terms obtained by deleting one or the other copy, and those two terms are equal. All other obstructed cases give only \(\infty\)-terms. Thus \(q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

The loop equations force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\). The two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) whenever \(e,f\) lie in the same original non-loop parallel class. After imposing these equations, the remaining valuated circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(\bar q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

---

### 2. Coordinate cocircuits

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(k>0\) and \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define

\[
C_A^q(e)=
\begin{cases}
q_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\). This is just the tropical Plücker relation for \(q\): for every \(T\in \binom{E}{k+1}\),

\[
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+C_A^q(e)\bigr)
=
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+q_{A\cup e}\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer. If \(C_A^q\) is all \(\infty\), it is harmless; projectively it is simply ignored.

For \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(q\), so the loop-forcing statements below are automatic.

---

### 3. Descent of a quotient of \(M\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

#### Loops remain loops

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(\ell\in L_0\). Every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate \(\infty\), because \(\ell\) is a loop of \(M\).

If \(\ell\) were not a loop of \(q\), there would be a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni \ell\). Put \(A=B-\ell\). Then

\[
C_A^q(\ell)=q_B<\infty.
\]

But \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), contradiction. Hence every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

#### Original parallel classes are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies

\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

Therefore, for every \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\),

\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]

with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

Consequences:

1. No finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\).  
   Indeed, if \(B\) contained both, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
   \]

   while

   \[
   q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
   \]

   contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation.  
   If \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original class, then \(f\notin A\), and

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
   \]

Thus each original non-loop parallel class is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

#### Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set

\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]

This is independent of the choice of representatives by the equality \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\) inside each original parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved classes. Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\):

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Therefore

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Since also

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]

and \(\Delta\) is injective on the non-loop coordinates, the containment

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]

implies

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). The construction is unique because the values \(\bar q_{\bar I}\) are forced by any representative set \(I\).

The rank-zero case is included: a rank-zero valuated matroid has only the empty basis and all elements are loops, so it descends to the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

---

### 4. Converse: zero-parallel extension preserves quotients

Conversely, let \(\bar q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\), so

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Let \(q=\Delta \bar q\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\). Then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

Hence \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Poset isomorphism of relative Dressians

Define

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(M)
\]

by

\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Trop(\Delta \bar q).
\]

The previous two steps show that \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined and surjective.

It is injective and order-reflecting because \(\Delta\) is injective:

\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq \Delta(X_2).
\]

Therefore

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 6. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Hence

\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]

is a canonical lattice isomorphism, with inverse

\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Put

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

on ground set \(E\), and

\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]

on ground set \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

we get

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]

Thus \(N_F\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

So the diagram

\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]

is carried by \(\Delta_*\) to

\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(M).
\]

---

### 7. Transfer of anti-involutions

Suppose

\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M)
\]

is an order-reversing involution. Since \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S)\) is a lattice isomorphism, \(\alpha\) descends to

\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}
:
\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]

This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now suppose one has constructed an order-reversing involution

\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(S)
\]

extending \(\bar\alpha\). Then define

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). By the flat-compatibility proved above, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),

\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/\alpha(F)\oplus U_{0,\alpha(F)}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original anti-involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\).

So the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loops and parallel classes removed.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
I found no counterexample. The Boolean-apartment proof is sound once the affine/all-\(\infty\) conventions and the converse direction are stated carefully.

Below, \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.” Thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

---

## Affine conventions

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(q\) on \(E\), write
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
for the affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector. We use the circuit-equation form:
\[
z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\iff
\forall S\in \binom{E}{d+1},\quad
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(z_e+q_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this says \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\{\infty^E\}\). For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\mathbb T^E\).

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) by the equations
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x
\iff
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\quad
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer},
\]
where
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
If \(x\neq \infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(x^\dagger)\). If \(x=\infty^E\), the same equations give \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

---

## Theorem: affine point-annihilator duality

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(p^\dagger\) be the flat-coordinate polar:
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
\boxed{
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
}
\]

### 1. Quotient incidence and finite bases inside every \(M\)-basis

For \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
The standard valuated-matroid fact is that \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), which is harmless. Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Therefore, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Call this the quotient circuit-incidence relation.

It implies:

1. Every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.
2. If two \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets \(I,I'\) are bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat of \(M\), then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). Hence \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.
3. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

For (3), start with one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\), and connect \(B_0\) to a target \(M\)-basis \(B\) through the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose \(B'=B''+b-e\) is one exchange step, so \(B''=B'-b+e\). If the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), keep it. If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\), and let \(C\subseteq B'\cup e\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit containing \(b,e\). The term \(p_{A\cup b}=p_I\) is finite, so circuit-incidence forces another finite term \(p_{A\cup c}\) with \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup b)\). Such \(c\) lies in \(B''\). Thus \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis.

---

### 2. Polar Boolean apartments

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and set
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),\qquad b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom; because \(M\) is simple, we identify it with an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Indeed, an element lying in every \(H_i\) for \(i\notin S\) has fundamental circuit over \(B\) supported inside \(S\), so it lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\).

Applying the order-reversing involution gives
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
}
\]
In particular, \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

---

### 3. Restriction to an apartment

Define the ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma, \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). For \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})(R)
&=p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)\\
&=p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}\\
&=\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
}
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids.

Also, if \(z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), then \(z|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B)\), because the circuit equations of \(\mu_B\) are among those of \(p\). Similarly, if \(z\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), then \(z|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\).

---

### 4. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), and \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\boxed{
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
}
\]

Indeed, the circuit equations of \(\mu^*\) are exactly orthogonality to the coordinate cocircuits
\[
D_A(i)=\mu_{A\cup i},\qquad |A|=\operatorname{rk}(\mu)-1.
\]
These \(D_A\)’s tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\), including the all-\(\infty\) point, and \(H_u\) is tropically convex. Hence orthogonality to all \(D_A\)’s is equivalent to containing all of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\) in \(H_u\). The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases match this statement exactly: rank \(0\) gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty\}\), while full rank gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), so \(H_u\) contains it only when \(u=\infty^N\).

---

## Proof of the theorem for \(1\le k\le r-1\)

Let \(d=r-k\).

### Forward direction

Assume
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). We must prove \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B=x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also,
\[
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

This holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\). Therefore \(y\in\widetilde H_x\), and hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### Reverse direction

Assume
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(d+1=r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. By the containment assumption,
\[
C_A^p\in \widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is precisely the circuit equation for \(u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup i)
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, this holds after restricting \(x\) to every \(M\)-basis of \(M\).

Now check the circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(d+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since
\[
x|_C\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C),
\]
the ordinary circuit equation on the subset \(J\subseteq C\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<d\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=d\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) is constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer.

Thus all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\) hold, and
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Boundary ranks

### Case \(k=0\)

Then \(p\) has rank \(0\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
Moreover \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant matroid \(M\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), the left side is true, and the right side is true because \(\infty^E\in \widetilde H_x\). So the theorem holds.

### Case \(k=r\)

Then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Its polar \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), so the right side holds.

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus b_i.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Testing \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives a minimum whose only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite; hence \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Varying \(B\) and \(i\), and using that polar bases cover all elements of \(E\), we get \(x=\infty^E\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\iff
x=\infty^E
\iff
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Projectivization

Projective tropical spaces are obtained from affine cones by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. For \(0<k<r\), the affine theorem descends immediately:
\[
[x]\in \Trop(p^\dagger)
\iff
\Trop(p)\subseteq H_x.
\]
The statement is independent of the representative of \(x\), since adding a scalar to \(x\) adds the same scalar to every hyperplane equation.

For \(k=0\), \(\Trop(p)\) is empty projectively, while \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Trop(M)\), so the equivalence is true for every projective \(x\in\Trop(M)\).

For \(k=r\), \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) is empty projectively. If a projective \(x\) had \(\Trop(M)\subseteq H_x\), conicality would imply the affine containment
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
forcing \(x=\infty^E\), impossible projectively. Thus both sides are false for every projective \(x\).

---

## Consequence: order reversal

If
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(q),
\]
and \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\), then by the theorem
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
After projectivization, the same inclusion holds. Combined with \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), this gives the desired order-reversing involution.
