Below, all tropical relations use the convention: **“the minimum is attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.”** Thus a relation with all terms equal to \(\infty\) is satisfied.

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular of rank \(r\), and let \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) be an order-reversing involution of its flat lattice. Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Put \(n=r-k\).

I work with valuated matroids in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), up to the usual addition of a global scalar.

---

## 0. Standard facts used

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) to be a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\), it is equivalent to require the **circuit-incidence relations**
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the valuated flag relation with the constant matroid \(M\) reduces to the unique circuit in each rank-\(r\), size-\((r+1)\) subset. Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit appears as such a fundamental circuit after extending \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis.

Since \(M\) is modular, for flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
The polarity satisfies
\[
(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp,\qquad
(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

---

## 1. Flat-constancy of \(p\)

First, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup a,\qquad I'=A\cup b
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), we get
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\).

The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

## 2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) for some \(M\)-independent \(k\)-set \(I\), let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to the scalar \(p_\varnothing\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the rank-zero valuated matroid, up to the scalar \(\bar p(E)\).

So assume \(0<k<r\) unless stated otherwise.

---

# 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It suffices to check the standard three-term tropical Plücker relations. Let \(|S|=n-2\) and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(S\cup i\cup j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set involving \(e\), so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then for distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which means \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent.

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

---

## 3.1 Local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Repeated hyperplanes

If some two hyperplanes coincide, relabel so that \(H_1=H_2\). Then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],\qquad [14]=[24].
\]
Hence
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23].
\]
Thus either both of those sums are finite and equal, or all three sums are \(\infty\). So the relation holds.

Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

For \(i\neq j\), since \(H_i,H_j\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\), we have
\[
H_i\vee H_j=Y.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)
=
(k+1)+(k+1)-(k+2)
=
k.
\]

Set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

We handle these three cases.

---

### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\)

Each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank-\(k\) flat containing \(Z\), so
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z
\]
for all \(i\neq j\). Therefore all three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

---

### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is contained in a pairwise meet, hence has rank at most \(k\), while intersecting three hyperplanes lowers rank by at most three, hence gives rank at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k\), then
\[
Z=T_i\wedge H_i
\]
would have rank at least \(k-1\), contradiction.

Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Moreover, \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is independent and spans \(T_m\vee T_n\). Therefore
\[
p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
=
\bar p(T_m\vee T_n).
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

---

### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\)

Consider the rank-three interval
\[
[Z,Y].
\]
Let \(N\) be its simplification: the elements of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid.

Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P)
\]
for each point \(P\) of \(N\).

We first prove that
\[
w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). Since \(D\) is a circuit in the simplification of the contraction by \(Z\), the chosen representatives form a circuit in the contraction, hence lift to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) and the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), because \(A\cup z\) is \(M\)-dependent for \(z\in Z\). For \(e_P\), we have
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\).

Now the images
\[
L_i=H_i/Z
\]
are hyperplanes of \(N\), and
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) of \(N\). Therefore the desired relation follows from the following rank-three edge lemma.

---

## 3.2 Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two \(L_i\)’s coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies. So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

We use the standard superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans: for a vector \(w\), the condition \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) is equivalent to saying that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global constant so that \(\min_x w_x=0\). Since \(N\) has rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane, possibly with one of the two levels absent. Thus there exist \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]
Here \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) covers the cases where one level is absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes a nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\).

For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique. The color multiplicities are one of
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In each case either all matchings tie, or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum. This remains true when some costs are \(\infty\).

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal \(\beta\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two of the \(y_j\)’s are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Therefore the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, hence the local four-hyperplane lemma, hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated matroid.

---

# 4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, we must show:

For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\tag{\(\dagger\)-CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent.

Set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For \(c\notin X\), the flat \(X\vee c\) is a point of the contraction \(M/X\), and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent and the term is \(\infty\).

Thus we need the following codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.1 Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We first show that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\). For \(i=1,2,3\), choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Hence, by circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\) and the circuit \(D\). Terms coming from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces
\[
u(P_3)\ge\lambda.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

It remains to recall that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat.

Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed. We prove that every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(S)\) lies in \(S\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S\) be an independent set spanning some flat \(F\subseteq\operatorname{cl}(S)\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(S\).

For \(m\le 2\), this is exactly line-closedness. Assume \(m\ge 3\). Let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of
\[
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
If \(p\le G\), we are done. If \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise, consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
By modularity inside \(F\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\). By induction, \(q\in S\), and \(b_m\in S\). Since \(S\) is line-closed, every point on the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(S\). Thus \(S\) is a flat.

Hence each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), so \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). This proves the codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.2 Applying the codimension-one lemma to circuits of \(M\)

Return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and the independent \((n-1)\)-set \(A\), with \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\).

Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). On \(C\setminus X\), the terms are exactly the lift of the tropical vector \(u\) from the contraction \(M/X\):
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(X\vee c).
\]

The vector \(u\) is in the Bergman fan of the simplification of \(M/X\), and its lift is in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\): parallel elements have equal values, and loop elements have value \(\infty\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Indeed, for every \(e\in C\setminus X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus e),
\]
and after contracting \(X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus e\bigr).
\]
Hence \(e\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus X\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then choosing a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would give a circuit on which \(u\) has a unique finite minimum, contradicting \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

# 5. Involutivity

Since \(p^\dagger\) is now known to be a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\) as well.

Let \(I\) be a \(k\)-subset.

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty
\]
and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.}
\]

---

# 6. Compatibility with ordinary flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\) satisfies
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), namely
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]

Let \(H\) be a rank-\(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when
\[
H^\perp
\]
is a complement of \(F\). By polarity,
\[
H^\perp\wedge F=0,\quad H^\perp\vee F=E
\]
is equivalent to
\[
H\vee F^\perp=E,\quad H\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Thus \(H\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}}
\]
with the same normalization of finite coordinates.

---

Hence, in the finite simple modular case, the coordinate construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is fully rigorous: it sends valuated quotients of \(M\) to valuated quotients of \(M\), satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, satisfies the quotient incidence relations with \(M\), is involutive, and sends ordinary flat quotients indexed by \(F\) to those indexed by \(F^\perp\).

## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Throughout, “matroid” means a matroid on a finite ground set. Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\), let \(L(M)\) be its lattice of flats, and suppose there is an order-reversing lattice involution
\[
\alpha:L(M)\to L(M),\qquad \alpha(F)=F^\perp,\qquad \alpha^2=\mathrm{id}.
\]
Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\) with min-plus conventions, and let \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}\) denote the tropical linear space of a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\). Let \(\Trop(M)\) mean the tropical linear space of the constant valuated matroid whose finite bases are the bases of \(M\). Let
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p\text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of }M\},
\]
equivalently the set of tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\), ordered by inclusion. For a flat \(F\), let \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) be the matroid on \(E\) whose elements in \(F\) are loops and whose restriction to \(E\setminus F\) is the contraction \(M/F\). Then there exists a bijection
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that \(\Phi^2=\mathrm{id}\), such that
\[
X\subseteq Y\implies \Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X),
\]
and such that, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Thus the original question has an affirmative answer, under the standard finite-ground-set convention.

## Proof

### Conventions and standard facts

Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). We use min-plus tropical conventions. A minimum is said to be attained twice if it has no unique finite minimizer; in particular, if all terms are \(\infty\), the condition is satisfied.

For a finite set \(E\), tropical projective space is
\[
\mathbb{TP}^{E-1}
=
(\mathbb T^E\setminus\{(\infty,\ldots,\infty)\})/\mathbb R(1,\ldots,1),
\]
where adding a real scalar to all coordinates does not change the point.

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We write \(p_I=\infty\) for any \(I\subseteq E\) with \(|I|\neq k\). Its tropical linear space is
\[
\Trop(p)=
\left\{
x\in \mathbb{TP}^{E-1}:
\text{ for every }J\in\binom{E}{k+1},
\min_{j\in J}(x_j+p_{J\setminus j})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\), we also write \(M\) for its constant valuated matroid:
\[
M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,& B\text{ a basis of }M,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\Trop(M)\) is the usual tropical linear space, or Bergman fan closure, of \(M\).

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Quotients and containment.**  
   If \(p\) has rank \(k\), \(q\) has rank \(\ell\ge k\), then \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(q\) if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q).
   \]
   Equivalently, for every \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\) and every \(T\in\binom{E}{\ell+1}\),
   \[
   \min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+q_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer.

2. **Valuated cocircuits.**  
   For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), every nonzero vector of the form
   \[
   C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e},\qquad |A|=k-1,
   \]
   is a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), and valuated cocircuits lie in \(\Trop(p)\).

3. **Ordinary annihilator theorem.**  
   If \(\mu\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on a finite set \(B\), and \(\mu^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual,
   \[
   \mu^*_S=\mu_{B\setminus S},
   \]
   then, for \(u\in\mathbb T^B\),
   \[
   u\in \Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
   \]
   where
   \[
   H_u=\{y\in\mathbb{TP}^{B-1}:\min_{b\in B}(u_b+y_b)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
   \]
   Equivalently, \(u\in\Trop(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\).

We now prove the theorem.

---

## 1. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\): delete the loops and identify each nonloop parallel class to one element. Write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
for the map to the set \(\bar E\) of nonloop parallel classes.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\) and is a union of parallel classes. Hence the flats of \(M\) are exactly the sets
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S\). Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim} L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Conjugating the given involution by \(\psi\), we get an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar\alpha:L(S)\to L(S),
\qquad
\bar\alpha=\psi\alpha\psi^{-1}.
\]

Define the diagonal embedding
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
It descends to tropical projective space.

### Valuated parallel-class descent lemma

Let \(q\) be a valuated matroid on \(E\). If
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq \Trop(M),
\]
then:

- every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\);
- every nonloop parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class in \(q\);
- consequently \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), and
  \[
  \Trop(q)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar q)).
  \]

Conversely, every valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) has a zero-parallel extension \(\Delta_*\bar q\) to \(E\), with all elements of \(L_0\) added as loops, and
\[
\Trop(\Delta_*\bar q)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar q)).
\]

Indeed, \(\Trop(M)\) is cut out by the equations \(x_e=\infty\) for loops \(e\in L_0\) and \(x_e=x_f\) for elements \(e,f\) in the same nonloop parallel class. If \(\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), then all points of \(\Trop(q)\) have \(x_e=\infty\) for \(e\in L_0\), so \(e\) is a loop of \(q\). Similarly, if \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), then every point of \(\Trop(q)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). In a valuated matroid, two nonloop elements that are not parallel can be separated by a valuated cocircuit, giving a point of \(\Trop(q)\) with one of the two coordinates finite and the other infinite. Thus \(e,f\) cannot be separated in \(q\). If they are valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, then their two-element valuated circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\) to be that nonzero offset on \(\Trop(q)\), contradicting \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore the offset is zero.

Thus \(q\) is precisely the zero-parallel extension of a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on the simplified ground set. The equality of tropical linear spaces follows from the coordinate description. The converse is immediate from the construction.

It follows that
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq \Trop(M)
\]
if and only if
\[
\Trop(\bar q)\subseteq \Trop(S).
\]
Hence \(\Delta\) induces an isomorphism of posets
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M),
\qquad
X\mapsto \Delta(X).
\]

### Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and write \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Thus
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Consider the matroid
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on \(E\). Its elements in \(F\) are loops, and on \(E\setminus F\) it is the contraction \(M/F\). For \(A\subseteq E\setminus F\),
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_M(A\cup F)-r_M(F).
\]
Because \(M\) is obtained from \(S\) by adding loops and parallel copies,
\[
r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup\bar F),
\qquad
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F).
\]
Therefore
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_{S/\bar F}(\pi(A)).
\]
Thus \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Consequently
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]

So it is enough to prove the theorem for the simplification \(S\), equipped with the induced involution \(\bar\alpha\). At the end we transport the construction back to \(M\) using \(\Delta\).

---

## 2. The simplified matroid is modular

We now show that \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is modular.

Because \(S\) is simple, \(L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is finite, atomic, ranked, and semimodular. The involution
\[
\bar\alpha:L(S)\to L(S)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism, so \(L(S)\) is isomorphic to its dual lattice \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\). Hence \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also geometric and therefore semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be the rank function of \(L(S)\), and let \(r=\rho(\bar E)\). Semimodularity of \(L(S)\) gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Semimodularity of the dual lattice gives the reverse inequality. Indeed, the dual rank is \(\rho^\vee(X)=r-\rho(X)\), so semimodularity in the dual says
\[
\rho^\vee(X)+\rho^\vee(Y)
\ge
\rho^\vee(X\vee Y)+\rho^\vee(X\wedge Y),
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)= \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Therefore \(L(S)\) is modular. Equivalently, \(S\) is a simple modular matroid.

We now prove the theorem in the simple modular case.

---

## 3. Flat-constancy for valuated quotients of a simple modular matroid

Let \(M\) now be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp.
\]
The bottom flat is \(\varnothing\), and the top flat is \(E\). Because the polarity reverses maximal chains,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), i.e.
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(M).
\]

### Circuit-incidence form of the quotient condition

Since \(p\) is a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\), the quotient incidence relations say that for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\) and every \((r+1)\)-subset \(T\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus A}\bigl(p_{A\cup i}+M_{T\setminus i}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), then every \(M_{T\setminus i}\) is \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C_T\), and \(M_{T\setminus i}\) is finite exactly for \(i\in C_T\). Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit appears as such a \(C_T\) by extending \(C\setminus c\) to a basis and adding \(c\).

Therefore the quotient condition is equivalent to:

\[
\tag{CI}
\text{For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\), }
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer.}
\]

### Dependent sets have infinite value

Let \(I\subseteq E\) have \(|I|=k\). If \(I\) is dependent in \(M\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]

### Constancy on bases of a flat

Suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin \operatorname{cl}_M(A)\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
The basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases \(I\) of any fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

We denote this common value by
\[
\bar p(F).
\]
Thus
\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of the flat \(F\).

For \(k=0\), this means \(\bar p(\varnothing)=p_\varnothing\). For \(k=r\), it says all \(M\)-bases of \(E\) have the same \(p\)-value.

---

## 4. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a rank-\((r-k)\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&
J\text{ is \(M\)-independent and }|J|=r-k,\\[4pt]
\infty,&
J\text{ is \(M\)-dependent and }|J|=r-k.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl}_M(J))=r-k\), its polar has rank \(k\), so \(\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) is defined.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), and let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(F^\perp\), then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

We prove next that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

## 5. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\)

### Boundary cases

If \(k=0\), then \(p\) is rank zero and \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to adding the scalar \(p_\varnothing\). Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

If \(k=r\), then flat-constancy says that \(p\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to scalar, and \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero. Again \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Assume from now on that
\[
1\le k\le r-1,
\qquad
n=r-k.
\]

### 5.1 Tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must show that
\[
\min\bigl(
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup b}+p^\dagger_{S\cup c\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup c}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup d},\,
p^\dagger_{S\cup a\cup d}+p^\dagger_{S\cup b\cup c}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every term is \(\infty\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\]
then every Plücker sum contains a dependent \(n\)-set, so again all sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume \(S\) is independent and
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]
Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2=r-k-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
If \(H_e=H_f\), then \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent, and \(p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}=\infty\). If \(H_e\neq H_f\), then modularity gives
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f).
\]

So the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\,[13]+[24],\,[14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23],
\]
so the claim is immediate.

Assume therefore that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting four hyperplanes can lower rank by at most four, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\)

Then every pairwise intersection \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is equal to \(Z\). Hence all three sums are equal.

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\)

Work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). Define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Then
\[
[12]=p_{A\cup t_3\cup t_4},
\qquad
[34]=p_{A\cup t_1\cup t_2},
\]
and similarly for the other pairings. Therefore the desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\)

The interval \([Z,Y]\) has rank three. Its points are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying \(Z<P<Y\), and its hyperplanes are the \(H_i\). Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We first show that \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\), where \([Z,Y]\) is viewed as the simple rank-three matroid whose lattice of flats is the interval \([Z,Y]\).

Let \(D\) be a circuit in this interval. Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For every point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup e_P)=P.
\]
By the standard circuit-lifting property for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) whose image in the contraction by \(Z\) is exactly \(D\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), all elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we obtain that
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\Trop([Z,Y])\).

We now use a rank-three edge fact.

### Rank-three edge fact

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\Trop(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Then
\[
\min\bigl(
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

For a vector \(w\) on the points of a matroid \(N\), the condition \(w\in\Trop(N)\) is equivalent to saying that every superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat. Indeed, a non-flat superlevel set produces a circuit with a unique minimum, and a circuit with a unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set.

If all values of \(w\) are \(\infty\), the edge claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract the minimum value of \(w\). In rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, allowing degenerate cases. Thus for some \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\),
\[
w(x)=\lambda\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\mu\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]

If none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), let
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j=a\). Hence a matching receives positive cost only from pairs with equal colors \(y_i\). For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: according to the multiplicities of the colors, either all three matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color.

If one line, say \(L_1\), equals \(B\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\) for \(j=2,3,4\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\neq a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of the equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the edge fact.

Applying the rank-three edge fact to the interval \([Z,Y]\) proves the local dual Plücker lemma. Therefore \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

### 5.2 The quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It remains to show that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, we must prove that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1=r-k-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp),
\]
while for \(c\in X\), the term is \(\infty\).

We need the following codimension-one polarity lemma.

### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((r-k-1)\) flat, and let \(Y=X^\perp\). Define a function \(u\) on the points of the contraction \(M/X\) by
\[
u(X\vee e)=\bar p((X\vee e)^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\Trop(M/X).
\]

Proof. We use the superlevel-flat criterion. It is enough to show that for every \(\lambda\), the set of points
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda
=
\{P\text{ a point of }M/X: u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(M/X\).

Because \(M/X\) is modular, a set of points is a flat if and only if it is closed under lines. Indeed, if \(Q\) is a point in the join of a line-closed set, one reaches \(Q\) inductively by repeatedly using modularity to place \(Q\) on a line spanned by two already obtained points.

So take \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(M/X\). Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and because \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3\ge H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), and we are done. Otherwise choose a basis \(B\) of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two contraction over \(H_1\wedge H_2\), the three points \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) form a circuit. Applying (CI) to \(B\) and this circuit gives
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Therefore \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\). ∎

Now return to the circuit \(C\) of \(M\). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). More precisely, for every \(c\in C\setminus X\),
\[
c\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}((C\setminus X)\setminus c),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus c)\) and \(X\) is a flat. Hence each element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\).

Since \(u\in\Trop(M/X)\), the minimum of \(u\) on \(C\setminus X\) has no unique finite minimizer. The elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\) to the circuit-incidence relation for \(p^\dagger\). Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Involutivity and flat quotients

### Involutivity

We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]
Let \(I\subseteq E\) have size \(k\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### Flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). If \(G\) is a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat, then
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\), i.e.
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,
\qquad
G\vee F=E.
\]
Polarity exchanges complements:
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing,\ G\vee F=E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
G^\perp\vee F^\perp=E,\ G^\perp\wedge F^\perp=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
=
p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Trop((p^F)^\dagger)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

## 7. Order reversal via the point-annihilator theorem

We now prove that the dagger operation reverses inclusion of tropical linear spaces.

### Boolean apartment duality

Let \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define the polar basis
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is a point of \(M\). For every \(S\subseteq \{1,\ldots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^\perp.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),
\]
and applying polarity converts the meet into the join of the points \(b_i^*\). Hence \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\ldots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis. Also \((B^*)^*=B\), so \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the set of \(M\)-bases.

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on the set \(\{1,\ldots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
This is a valuated matroid. Indeed, the Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\), and \(\mu_B\) is not identically \(\infty\): every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. To see this, start with an \(M\)-basis containing a finite \(p\)-basis, and move to the desired basis through the connected basis graph of \(M\). If an exchange removes an element of the finite \(p\)-basis, the circuit-incidence relation (CI) supplies a replacement inside the new basis.

The dagger operation restricts on polar apartments to ordinary valuated duality:
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]
Indeed, if \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^\perp
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}
\right)
=
\mu_B(\{1,\ldots,r\}\setminus S).
\]

### Polar hyperplanes

Let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Since \(x\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M\), we may apply the dagger construction to \(x\). Define
\[
\alpha(x):=x^\dagger.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(x)\) is a rank-\((r-1)\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Explicitly, for an \(M\)-independent \((r-1)\)-set \(I\),
\[
\alpha(x)_I
=
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},
\]
where \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp\) is a point of \(M\); and \(\alpha(x)_I=\infty\) if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent.

We call \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) the polar hyperplane of \(x\).

### Point-annihilator theorem

For every rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in\Trop(M)\),
\[
\tag{\(*\)}
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

#### Forward implication

Assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\), and put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\ldots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
we have
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*).
\]
By the ordinary annihilator theorem,
\[
\Trop(\mu_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Now let \(y\in\Trop(p)\). Its restriction \(y|_B\) lies in \(\Trop(\mu_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_i (y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. But
\[
\alpha(x)_{B\setminus b_i}=x_{b_i^*},
\]
so this is exactly the tropical circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on the \(M\)-basis \(B\).

It remains to explain why dependent \(r\)-subsets impose no extra conditions. Let \(K\subseteq E\) have size \(r\) and be \(M\)-dependent. If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)\le r-2\), then every \((r-1)\)-subset of \(K\) is dependent, so all terms in the \(\alpha(x)\)-equation are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(K)=r-1\), then \(K\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in
\[
\min_{i\in K}(y_i+\alpha(x)_{K\setminus i})
\]
occur exactly for \(i\in D\), and for those \(i\), the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(K\setminus i)\) is constant. Hence the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(y\in\Trop(M)\) on \(D\), which holds because
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M).
\]
Thus \(y\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Since \(y\in\Trop(p)\) was arbitrary,
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

#### Reverse implication

Assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\ldots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\ldots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove \(u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)\). By the ordinary annihilator theorem, it is enough to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\).

Let \(S\subseteq\{1,\ldots,r\}\) have size \(k-1\), and set
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The corresponding valuated cocircuit of \(\mu_B\) is
\[
D_S(i)=\mu_B(S\cup i)=p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
This is the restriction to \(B\) of the valuated cocircuit
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e}
\]
of \(p\). If \(C_A\) is nonzero, then \(C_A\in\Trop(p)\), hence by assumption
\[
C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Testing the tropical equation for \(\alpha(x)\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\). If \(C_A\) is zero, there is nothing to prove.

Therefore
\[
u_B\in\Trop(\mu_B^*)
=
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on the \(M\)-bases, this shows that \(x\) restricts to \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment.

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). We must prove
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). The equation holds because
\[
x|_{B^*}\in\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in D\), and for those \(j\), the flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)\) are equal. Hence the equation reduces to the circuit equation for \(x\in\Trop(M)\) on \(D\). Thus it holds.

Therefore
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves the point-annihilator theorem \((*)\).

### Order reversal

For a tropical linear space \(X\in\Dr(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then the point-annihilator theorem gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi(X)\) is a tropical linear space contained in \(\Trop(M)\), and the definition depends only on \(X\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector \(p\).

Since \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\),
\[
\Phi^2=\mathrm{id}.
\]
If
\[
X=\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)=Y,
\]
and \(x\in\Phi(Y)=\Trop(q^\dagger)\), then by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
and applying the point-annihilator theorem again gives
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Phi(X).
\]
Therefore
\[
X\subseteq Y\implies \Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
So \(\Phi\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

By the flat-quotient computation from Section 6,
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\). Thus the theorem is proved in the simple modular case.

---

## 8. Transport back from \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) to \(M\)

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\), possibly with loops and parallel elements. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M),
\]
and let
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
be the poset isomorphism from Section 1. The flat-lattice involution on \(M\) induces an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar\alpha:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
By Sections 2 through 7, there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending \(\bar\alpha\).

Define
\[
\Phi_M:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
by
\[
\Phi_M(\Delta(X))=\Delta(\Phi_S(X)).
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism and \(\Phi_S\) is an order-reversing involution, \(\Phi_M\) is also an order-reversing involution.

Finally, let \(F\in L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S)\). Section 1 showed that
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]
Since
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\bar F),
\]
we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta\!\left(
\Trop(S/\bar\alpha(\bar F)\oplus U_{0,\bar\alpha(\bar F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the theorem for the original matroid \(M\). ∎

writeup_tasks.statement: For every finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp, the relative Dressian Dr(M), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, admits an order-reversing involution extending the given involution on the embedded copy of L(M)^op.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Produce a fully self-contained proof of the following theorem: Let M be a finite matroid. Suppose the lattice of flats L(M) has an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp. Let Dr(M) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M), ordered by inclusion, with the standard embedded flat subposet F ↦ Trop(M/F plus loops on F). Then Dr(M) admits an order-reversing involution Phi extending F ↦ F^perp. The proof must be readable by someone who has not seen any prior solver outputs. Define all conventions inline: tropical semiring T = R union {infinity}, affine tropical cones including the all-infinity vector, projectivization, valuated matroids, tropical linear spaces, valuated quotients, and the relative Dressian. State precisely the standard external theorems you use, especially: a tropical linear space determines its valuated matroid up to global scalar; L(p) subset L(q) if and only if p is a valuated quotient of q; tropical linear spaces are tropical spans of valuated cocircuits; and ordinary valuated-matroid annihilator duality. Prove all nonstandard ingredients from the project, not merely cite them. The write-up should have the following structure. Section 1: affine/projective conventions and standard valuated-matroid facts. Section 2: simplification invariance: define the diagonal map from si(M) to M, prove loops remain loops in any quotient contained in Trop(M), prove original parallel classes remain zero-offset parallel classes using coordinate cocircuits, prove Dr(si(M)) ≅ Dr(M), and prove compatibility with flat quotients. Section 3: reduction to the simple modular case: show the anti-involution descends to si(M), and prove a finite simple geometric lattice with an order-reversing involution is modular by comparing semimodularity with its opposite. Section 4: the simple modular dagger construction. For a rank k valuated quotient p of a simple modular matroid M of rank r, prove finite p-bases are M-independent and p is constant on M-bases spanning the same rank-k flat; define pbar on rank-k flats, and define p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J of size r-k and infinity otherwise. Prove p^dagger is a valuated matroid quotient of M. Include explicit statements and proofs of the local dual Pluecker lemma and the codimension-one polarity or quotient-incidence lemma, including repeated hyperplanes, dependent subsets, and boundary ranks. Section 5: prove involutivity and flat compatibility, including (p^dagger)^dagger = p and the calculation that the ordinary flat quotient indexed by F is sent to the flat quotient indexed by F^perp. Section 6: prove the affine point-annihilator theorem: for every affine tropical point x in Trop(M), x lies in Trop(p^dagger) if and only if Trop(p) is contained in the polar hyperplane Trop(x^dagger). Use Boolean apartments carefully: prove every M-basis contains a finite p-basis, prove the polar atoms of a basis form a basis, identify p^dagger restricted to the polar apartment with the ordinary valuated dual of p restricted to the original apartment, and invoke ordinary annihilator duality. Section 7: use the point-annihilator theorem to prove inclusion reversal: if Trop(p) subset Trop(q), then Trop(q^dagger) subset Trop(p^dagger). Then conjugate through the simplification isomorphism to define Phi for the original M and verify that it is an order-reversing involution extending the flat-lattice involution. Avoid references to the conversation or to attached notes in the proof itself. Do not rely on classification of modular matroids as projective geometries. Do not leave core project-specific lemmas as routine.

I found no counterexample. The Boolean-apartment proof is sound once the affine/all-\(\infty\) conventions and the converse direction are stated carefully.

Below, \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.” Thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

---

## Affine conventions

For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(q\) on \(E\), write
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \mathbb T^E
\]
for the affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector. We use the circuit-equation form:
\[
z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\iff
\forall S\in \binom{E}{d+1},\quad
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(z_e+q_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this says \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\{\infty^E\}\). For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\mathbb T^E\).

For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) by the equations
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x
\iff
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},\quad
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer},
\]
where
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
If \(x\neq \infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(x^\dagger)\). If \(x=\infty^E\), the same equations give \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

---

## Theorem: affine point-annihilator duality

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(p^\dagger\) be the flat-coordinate polar:
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then for every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
\boxed{
x\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
}
\]

### 1. Quotient incidence and finite bases inside every \(M\)-basis

For \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define the coordinate cocircuit
\[
C_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
The standard valuated-matroid fact is that \(C_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), which is harmless. Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\),
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Therefore, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Call this the quotient circuit-incidence relation.

It implies:

1. Every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.
2. If two \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets \(I,I'\) are bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat of \(M\), then \(p_I=p_{I'}\). Hence \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined.
3. Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis.

For (3), start with one finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis \(B_0\), and connect \(B_0\) to a target \(M\)-basis \(B\) through the basis graph of \(M\). Suppose \(B'=B''+b-e\) is one exchange step, so \(B''=B'-b+e\). If the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), keep it. If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\), and let \(C\subseteq B'\cup e\) be the fundamental \(M\)-circuit containing \(b,e\). The term \(p_{A\cup b}=p_I\) is finite, so circuit-incidence forces another finite term \(p_{A\cup c}\) with \(c\in C\setminus(A\cup b)\). Such \(c\) lies in \(B''\). Thus \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis.

---

### 2. Polar Boolean apartments

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and set
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),\qquad b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom; because \(M\) is simple, we identify it with an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Indeed, an element lying in every \(H_i\) for \(i\notin S\) has fundamental circuit over \(B\) supported inside \(S\), so it lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\).

Applying the order-reversing involution gives
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
}
\]
In particular, \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and the operation \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

---

### 3. Restriction to an apartment

Define the ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma, \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from \(p\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). For \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})(R)
&=p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)\\
&=\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)\\
&=p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}\\
&=\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*
}
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids.

Also, if \(z\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), then \(z|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B)\), because the circuit equations of \(\mu_B\) are among those of \(p\). Similarly, if \(z\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\), then \(z|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\).

---

### 4. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), and \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\boxed{
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
}
\]

Indeed, the circuit equations of \(\mu^*\) are exactly orthogonality to the coordinate cocircuits
\[
D_A(i)=\mu_{A\cup i},\qquad |A|=\operatorname{rk}(\mu)-1.
\]
These \(D_A\)’s tropically generate \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\), including the all-\(\infty\) point, and \(H_u\) is tropically convex. Hence orthogonality to all \(D_A\)’s is equivalent to containing all of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\) in \(H_u\). The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases match this statement exactly: rank \(0\) gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty\}\), while full rank gives \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), so \(H_u\) contains it only when \(u=\infty^N\).

---

## Proof of the theorem for \(1\le k\le r-1\)

Let \(d=r-k\).

### Forward direction

Assume
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). We must prove \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Since \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\),
\[
u_B=x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also,
\[
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

This holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\). Therefore \(y\in\widetilde H_x\), and hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### Reverse direction

Assume
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(d+1=r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), unless it is all \(\infty\), in which case the desired equation is automatic. By the containment assumption,
\[
C_A^p\in \widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(C_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is precisely the circuit equation for \(u_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup i)
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, this holds after restricting \(x\) to every \(M\)-basis of \(M\).

Now check the circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(d+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since
\[
x|_C\in \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger|_C),
\]
the ordinary circuit equation on the subset \(J\subseteq C\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<d\), then every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=d\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) is constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer.

Thus all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\) hold, and
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Boundary ranks

### Case \(k=0\)

Then \(p\) has rank \(0\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
Moreover \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant matroid \(M\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), the left side is true, and the right side is true because \(\infty^E\in \widetilde H_x\). So the theorem holds.

### Case \(k=r\)

Then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
Its polar \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), so the right side holds.

Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B\setminus b_i.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Testing \(C_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives a minimum whose only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Therefore this term cannot be finite; hence \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Varying \(B\) and \(i\), and using that polar bases cover all elements of \(E\), we get \(x=\infty^E\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x
\iff
x=\infty^E
\iff
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## Projectivization

Projective tropical spaces are obtained from affine cones by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. For \(0<k<r\), the affine theorem descends immediately:
\[
[x]\in \Trop(p^\dagger)
\iff
\Trop(p)\subseteq H_x.
\]
The statement is independent of the representative of \(x\), since adding a scalar to \(x\) adds the same scalar to every hyperplane equation.

For \(k=0\), \(\Trop(p)\) is empty projectively, while \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Trop(M)\), so the equivalence is true for every projective \(x\in\Trop(M)\).

For \(k=r\), \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\) is empty projectively. If a projective \(x\) had \(\Trop(M)\subseteq H_x\), conicality would imply the affine containment
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x,
\]
forcing \(x=\infty^E\), impossible projectively. Thus both sides are false for every projective \(x\).

---

## Consequence: order reversal

If
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(q),
\]
and \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\), then by the theorem
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
After projectivization, the same inclusion holds. Combined with \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), this gives the desired order-reversing involution.
