Below, all tropical relations use the convention: **“the minimum is attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.”** Thus a relation with all terms equal to \(\infty\) is satisfied.

Let \(M\) be finite, simple, modular of rank \(r\), and let \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) be an order-reversing involution of its flat lattice. Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Put \(n=r-k\).

I work with valuated matroids in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), up to the usual addition of a global scalar.

---

## 0. Standard facts used

For a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) to be a quotient of the constant rank-\(r\) valuated matroid \(M\), it is equivalent to require the **circuit-incidence relations**
\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
to have no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, the valuated flag relation with the constant matroid \(M\) reduces to the unique circuit in each rank-\(r\), size-\((r+1)\) subset. Conversely, every \(M\)-circuit appears as such a fundamental circuit after extending \(C\setminus c\) to an \(M\)-basis.

Since \(M\) is modular, for flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
The polarity satisfies
\[
(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp,\qquad
(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]

---

## 1. Flat-constancy of \(p\)

First, if \(I\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup a,\qquad I'=A\cup b
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\) and \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying (CI) to \(A\) and \(C\), we get
\[
\min\{p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}
\]
in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\).

The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\). Therefore \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

---

## 2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) for some \(M\)-independent \(k\)-set \(I\), let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to the scalar \(p_\varnothing\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the rank-zero valuated matroid, up to the scalar \(\bar p(E)\).

So assume \(0<k<r\) unless stated otherwise.

---

# 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It suffices to check the standard three-term tropical Plücker relations. Let \(|S|=n-2\) and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(S\cup i\cup j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all relevant \(p^\dagger\)-coordinates are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set involving \(e\), so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\), we have
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2.
\]
For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then for distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\neq H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) iff \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which means \(S\cup e\cup f\) is \(M\)-dependent.

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) follows from the following local lemma.

---

## 3.1 Local four-hyperplane lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let
\[
H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4
\]
be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Repeated hyperplanes

If some two hyperplanes coincide, relabel so that \(H_1=H_2\). Then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],\qquad [14]=[24].
\]
Hence
\[
[13]+[24]=[14]+[23].
\]
Thus either both of those sums are finite and equal, or all three sums are \(\infty\). So the relation holds.

Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

For \(i\neq j\), since \(H_i,H_j\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\), we have
\[
H_i\vee H_j=Y.
\]
By modularity,
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)
=
(k+1)+(k+1)-(k+2)
=
k.
\]

Set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

We handle these three cases.

---

### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\)

Each \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank-\(k\) flat containing \(Z\), so
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=Z
\]
for all \(i\neq j\). Therefore all three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

---

### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i} H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). Indeed, \(T_i\) is contained in a pairwise meet, hence has rank at most \(k\), while intersecting three hyperplanes lowers rank by at most three, hence gives rank at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k\), then
\[
Z=T_i\wedge H_i
\]
would have rank at least \(k-1\), contradiction.

Also
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z.
\]
If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then \(T_m,T_n\le H_i\wedge H_j\), and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(T_m\vee T_n)
=
(k-1)+(k-1)-(k-2)
=
k.
\]
Since \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\),
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]

Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup t_i)=T_i.
\]
Moreover, \(A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}\) is independent and spans \(T_m\vee T_n\). Therefore
\[
p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}
=
\bar p(T_m\vee T_n).
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

---

### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\)

Consider the rank-three interval
\[
[Z,Y].
\]
Let \(N\) be its simplification: the elements of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
This is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid.

Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P)
\]
for each point \(P\) of \(N\).

We first prove that
\[
w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). Since \(D\) is a circuit in the simplification of the contraction by \(Z\), the chosen representatives form a circuit in the contraction, hence lift to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) and the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), because \(A\cup z\) is \(M\)-dependent for \(z\in Z\). For \(e_P\), we have
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus
\[
\min_{P\in D} w(P)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\).

Now the images
\[
L_i=H_i/Z
\]
are hyperplanes of \(N\), and
\[
H_i\wedge H_j
\]
corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) of \(N\). Therefore the desired relation follows from the following rank-three edge lemma.

---

## 3.2 Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\neq L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If two \(L_i\)’s coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies. So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

We use the standard superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans: for a vector \(w\), the condition \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) is equivalent to saying that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\{x:w_x\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global constant so that \(\min_x w_x=0\). Since \(N\) has rank three, the nonempty proper superlevel flats form a flag
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane, possibly with one of the two levels absent. Thus there exist \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}.
\]
Here \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\) covers the cases where one level is absent.

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes a nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\).

For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique. The color multiplicities are one of
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4.
\]
In each case either all matchings tie, or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum. This remains true when some costs are \(\infty\).

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), set
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\neq a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of \(c(y_2),c(y_3),c(y_4)\) equal \(\beta\), so the minimum is attained at least twice. If exactly two of the \(y_j\)’s are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Therefore the minimum is never uniquely finite.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, hence the local four-hyperplane lemma, hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(n\) valuated matroid.

---

# 4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, we must show:

For every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\tag{\(\dagger\)-CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent.

Set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

For \(c\notin X\), the flat \(X\vee c\) is a point of the contraction \(M/X\), and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent and the term is \(\infty\).

Thus we need the following codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.1 Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N).
\]

### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We first show that \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
P_1^\perp\wedge P_2^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(K)=k-1\). Choose a basis \(B\) of \(K\). For \(i=1,2,3\), choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three distinct atoms \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit after simplification. Hence, by circuit lifting for contractions, there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply (CI) for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\) and the circuit \(D\). Terms coming from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), while
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces
\[
u(P_3)\ge\lambda.
\]
Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

It remains to recall that in a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat.

Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed. We prove that every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(S)\) lies in \(S\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S\) be an independent set spanning some flat \(F\subseteq\operatorname{cl}(S)\). We prove by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(F\) lies in \(S\).

For \(m\le 2\), this is exactly line-closedness. Assume \(m\ge 3\). Let
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of
\[
F=G\vee b_m.
\]
If \(p\le G\), we are done. If \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise, consider the line
\[
\ell=p\vee b_m.
\]
By modularity inside \(F\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(\ell\wedge G)
=
\operatorname{rk}(\ell)+\operatorname{rk}(G)-\operatorname{rk}(F)
=
2+(m-1)-m
=
1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\). By induction, \(q\in S\), and \(b_m\in S\). Since \(S\) is line-closed, every point on the line \(q\vee b_m=\ell\), including \(p\), lies in \(S\). Thus \(S\) is a flat.

Hence each \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat of \(N\), so \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(N)\). This proves the codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

## 4.2 Applying the codimension-one lemma to circuits of \(M\)

Return to the \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and the independent \((n-1)\)-set \(A\), with \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\).

Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). On \(C\setminus X\), the terms are exactly the lift of the tropical vector \(u\) from the contraction \(M/X\):
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
u(X\vee c).
\]

The vector \(u\) is in the Bergman fan of the simplification of \(M/X\), and its lift is in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\): parallel elements have equal values, and loop elements have value \(\infty\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Indeed, for every \(e\in C\setminus X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus e),
\]
and after contracting \(X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus e\bigr).
\]
Hence \(e\) lies in some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus X\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then choosing a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would give a circuit on which \(u\) has a unique finite minimum, contradicting \(u\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\).

Therefore
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

---

# 5. Involutivity

Since \(p^\dagger\) is now known to be a valuated quotient of \(M\), the flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\) as well.

Let \(I\) be a \(k\)-subset.

If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty
\]
and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((F^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.}
\]

---

# 6. Compatibility with ordinary flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}(F))\) flat \(G\) satisfies
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\), namely
\[
G\wedge F=0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]

Let \(H\) be a rank-\(\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger
\]
is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when
\[
H^\perp
\]
is a complement of \(F\). By polarity,
\[
H^\perp\wedge F=0,\quad H^\perp\vee F=E
\]
is equivalent to
\[
H\vee F^\perp=E,\quad H\wedge F^\perp=0.
\]
Thus \(H\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
\boxed{(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}}
\]
with the same normalization of finite coordinates.

---

Hence, in the finite simple modular case, the coordinate construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is fully rigorous: it sends valuated quotients of \(M\) to valuated quotients of \(M\), satisfies the tropical Plücker relations, satisfies the quotient incidence relations with \(M\), is involutive, and sends ordinary flat quotients indexed by \(F\) to those indexed by \(F^\perp\).

\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

## Simplification reduction

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let

\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]

be its loop set, let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let

\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]

be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Work first with affine tropical cones \(\widetilde{\Trop}(-)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\), \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Projectivization is obtained by quotienting by \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\). If one deletes the all-\(\infty\) point projectively, the rank-zero spaces below become empty on both sides; the statements remain unchanged.

Define

\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]

by

\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

This commutes with tropical scaling:

\[
\Delta(x+\lambda\mathbf 1)=\Delta(x)+\lambda\mathbf 1,
\]

so it descends to tropical projective space. It is injective, except for the harmless all-loop degenerate case where both sides have only the rank-zero object.

---

### 1. Zero-parallel extension of valuated matroids

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta \bar q\) on \(E\) by

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]

for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\).

This is exactly the valuated matroid obtained from \(\bar q\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each class \(a\in \bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\). Indeed, in a Plücker relation indexed by \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\) and \(B\in \binom{E}{k+1}\), a term can be finite only if both \(A\cup b\) and \(B-b\) avoid loops and meet every original parallel class at most once. If \(B\) has no repeated class, the relation is the projected relation for \(\bar q\). If \(B\) has exactly one repeated class, the only possibly finite terms are the two terms obtained by deleting one or the other copy, and those two terms are equal. All other obstructed cases give only \(\infty\)-terms. Thus \(q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

The loop equations force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\). The two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) whenever \(e,f\) lie in the same original non-loop parallel class. After imposing these equations, the remaining valuated circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(\bar q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

---

### 2. Coordinate cocircuits

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(k>0\) and \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define

\[
C_A^q(e)=
\begin{cases}
q_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\). This is just the tropical Plücker relation for \(q\): for every \(T\in \binom{E}{k+1}\),

\[
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+C_A^q(e)\bigr)
=
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+q_{A\cup e}\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer. If \(C_A^q\) is all \(\infty\), it is harmless; projectively it is simply ignored.

For \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(q\), so the loop-forcing statements below are automatic.

---

### 3. Descent of a quotient of \(M\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

#### Loops remain loops

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(\ell\in L_0\). Every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate \(\infty\), because \(\ell\) is a loop of \(M\).

If \(\ell\) were not a loop of \(q\), there would be a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni \ell\). Put \(A=B-\ell\). Then

\[
C_A^q(\ell)=q_B<\infty.
\]

But \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), contradiction. Hence every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

#### Original parallel classes are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies

\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

Therefore, for every \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\),

\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]

with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

Consequences:

1. No finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\).  
   Indeed, if \(B\) contained both, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
   \]

   while

   \[
   q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
   \]

   contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation.  
   If \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original class, then \(f\notin A\), and

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
   \]

Thus each original non-loop parallel class is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

#### Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set

\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]

This is independent of the choice of representatives by the equality \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\) inside each original parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved classes. Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\):

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Therefore

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Since also

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]

and \(\Delta\) is injective on the non-loop coordinates, the containment

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]

implies

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). The construction is unique because the values \(\bar q_{\bar I}\) are forced by any representative set \(I\).

The rank-zero case is included: a rank-zero valuated matroid has only the empty basis and all elements are loops, so it descends to the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

---

### 4. Converse: zero-parallel extension preserves quotients

Conversely, let \(\bar q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\), so

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Let \(q=\Delta \bar q\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\). Then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

Hence \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Poset isomorphism of relative Dressians

Define

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(M)
\]

by

\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Trop(\Delta \bar q).
\]

The previous two steps show that \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined and surjective.

It is injective and order-reflecting because \(\Delta\) is injective:

\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq \Delta(X_2).
\]

Therefore

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 6. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Hence

\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]

is a canonical lattice isomorphism, with inverse

\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Put

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

on ground set \(E\), and

\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]

on ground set \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

we get

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]

Thus \(N_F\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

So the diagram

\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]

is carried by \(\Delta_*\) to

\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(M).
\]

---

### 7. Transfer of anti-involutions

Suppose

\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M)
\]

is an order-reversing involution. Since \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S)\) is a lattice isomorphism, \(\alpha\) descends to

\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}
:
\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]

This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now suppose one has constructed an order-reversing involution

\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(S)
\]

extending \(\bar\alpha\). Then define

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). By the flat-compatibility proved above, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),

\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/\alpha(F)\oplus U_{0,\alpha(F)}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original anti-involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\).

So the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loops and parallel classes removed.

## Verdict

I find **no fatal gap and no counterexample** in the claimed theorem. The proof strategy is sound: reduce to the simplification, observe the simplification is modular, define the polar/dagger valuated matroid in the simple modular case, prove it is an involutive quotient operation, and use the point-annihilator theorem to get order reversal.

However, `writeup_r5_dde284` is **not quite submission-ready**. The main fixes are expository but mathematically important: use affine cones before projectivizing, replace the simplification-descent paragraph with the coordinate-cocircuit argument, state the standard valuated-matroid theorems explicitly, and add the rank-zero/full-rank boundary cases in the annihilator argument.

Below is the compact checklist of exact items I would require before submission.

---

## 1. Standard facts that must be stated or cited

Work with affine cones  
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E,\qquad \mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Use the convention that “minimum attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimizer.”

The proof needs the following facts explicitly.

1. **Containment = valuated quotient.**  
   For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
   \[
   \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(q)
   \]
   iff \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). For \(q=M\) constant, this is equivalent to the circuit-incidence condition
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   having no unique finite minimizer for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((\operatorname{rk}p-1)\)-set \(A\).

2. **Coordinate cocircuits lie in the tropical linear space.**  
   For rank \(k\) \(p\), the vector
   \[
   D_A(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A,
   \end{cases}
   \qquad |A|=k-1,
   \]
   lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

3. **Cocircuit span / ordinary annihilator duality.**  
   For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on \(N\),
   \[
   u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
   \iff
   \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq
   \{v:\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
   \]
   This includes the rank \(0\) and full-rank cases.

4. **Plücker uniqueness, if using representatives.**  
   If the final proof defines
   \[
   \Phi(\Trop(p)):=\Trop(p^\dagger),
   \]
   then it must cite that \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines \(p\) up to global tropical scalar.  
   Alternatively, avoid this by defining \(\Phi\) intrinsically through the annihilator formula
   \[
   \Phi(X)=\{x\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq H_x\}.
   \]

---

## 2. Affine/projective replacement paragraph

Use this convention near the start:

> We first work affinely. For rank \(k\) \(p\), \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) includes \(\infty^E\). If \(k=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\), so its projectivization is empty. Projective tropical spaces are obtained by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. All containment and annihilator statements are proved affinely and then projectivized. Thus rank-zero spaces become empty projectively, while the affine all-\(\infty\) vector causes no exceptional behavior.

This removes the boundary ambiguity in the point-annihilator theorem and in \(\Dr(M)\).

---

## 3. Simplification descent: replace the current proof

The current write-up says too vaguely that \(\Trop(M)\) is “cut out” by loop/parallel equations. That is not literally sufficient. Replace with the coordinate-cocircuit proof:

> Let \(q\) be rank \(k\) with \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). If \(k=0\), descent is immediate. Assume \(k>0\). For every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q(e)=q_{A\cup e}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\), hence in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).
>
> If \(I\) is a finite \(q\)-basis containing an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(D_A^q\) has a unique finite minimum on \(C\), namely at \(c\), contradicting \(D_A^q\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Hence every finite \(q\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.
>
> If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Therefore \(D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f)\) for every \(A\). It follows that no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\), and replacing \(e\) by \(f\) in a finite basis preserves both finiteness and valuation. Thus each original parallel class is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class.
>
> Therefore \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and
> \[
> \widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
> \]
> Since \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta(\widetilde{\Trop}(\operatorname{si}(M)))\) and \(\Delta\) is injective on the simplified coordinates, \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\).

Also state the all-loop matroid separately: then the projective \(\Trop(M)\) is empty and \(\Dr(M)\) has only the empty object.

---

## 4. Simple modular dagger construction

The proof in `t12` is essentially sound. The final write-up should explicitly isolate these lemmas.

- **Flat constancy.**  
  For a quotient \(p\) of a simple modular \(M\), every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-set has \(p_I=\infty\), and \(p_I\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat.

- **Definition.**
  \[
  p^\dagger_J=
  \begin{cases}
  \bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\\
  \infty,&J\text{ \(M\)-dependent},
  \end{cases}
  \qquad |J|=r-k.
  \]

- **Local Plücker lemma.**  
  When reducing to four hyperplanes \(H_1,\dots,H_4\) in a rank \(k+2\) flat \(Y\), define
  \[
  [ij]=
  \begin{cases}
  \bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
  \infty,&H_i=H_j.
  \end{cases}
  \]
  This repeated-hyperplane convention is necessary.

- **Rank-three edge lemma.**  
  State the superlevel-flat criterion with the affine convention: \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\) iff all real superlevel sets \(\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}\) are flats. The weighted point-line flag argument is valid, including infinite weights.

- **Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\).**  
  In the codimension-one polarity lemma, explicitly say that the lifted vector on \(M/X\) has value \(\infty\) on loops and equal values on parallel classes, hence lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\); otherwise \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\), ruling out a unique finite minimum.

- **Flat compatibility.**  
  Keep the complement argument:
  \[
  (p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
  \]
  Here \(p^F\) is the constant matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), and finite coordinates occur exactly on lattice complements of \(F\).

---

## 5. Point-annihilator theorem: required final form

The Boolean-apartment argument is not circular, but it must be stated affinely and with boundary ranks.

Use the following theorem:

> Let \(M\) be finite, simple, loopless, modular, with polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). For a quotient \(p\) of rank \(k\) and \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
> \[
> x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
> \iff
> \widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
> \]
> where \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) iff for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
> \[
> \min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
> \]
> has no unique finite minimizer, with \(b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp\).

Then include the Boolean-apartment calculation:
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\notin S\},
\]
so \(B^*\) is an \(M\)-basis and
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

The converse direction must use **global coordinate cocircuits of \(p\)**, not an extension property for arbitrary points of \(\Trop(p|_B)\). That is the key non-circular point.

Finally, add the rank boundaries:

- If \(k=0\), then projectively \(\Trop(p)=\varnothing\) and \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\Trop(M)\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(\Trop(p)=\Trop(M)\) and \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\varnothing\). Affinely, the only \(x\) with \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) is \(x=\infty^E\), which disappears projectively.

---

## 6. Final definition of the involution

The cleanest final definition is intrinsic:

\[
\Phi(X)=\{[x]\in \Trop(M): X\subseteq H_x\}.
\]

If \(X=\Trop(p)\), the point-annihilator theorem gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is well-defined as a map on tropical linear spaces, not merely on Plücker representatives. Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
gives \(\Phi^2=\mathrm{id}\), and the annihilator theorem gives
\[
X\subseteq Y\implies \Phi(Y)\subseteq \Phi(X).
\]

For a nonsimple \(M\), define
\[
\Phi_M=\Delta_*\circ \Phi_{\operatorname{si}(M)}\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
The flat-quotient compatibility under simplification then gives
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

With these edits, the theorem is ready.

writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt:
Write a fully self-contained proof of the following theorem.

Theorem. Let M be a finite matroid on a finite ground set E. Let Trop(M) be the projective tropical linear space obtained as the closure of the Bergman fan of M. Let Dr(M) be the poset of projective tropical linear spaces X contained in Trop(M), ordered by inclusion; equivalently, Dr(M) is represented by valuated matroid quotients p of the ordinary matroid M, with X = Trop(p). Suppose the flat lattice L(M) has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Then there is an inclusion-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) such that, for every flat F of M, under the standard embedding F |-> Trop(M/F plus loops on F), one has Phi(Trop(M/F plus loops on F)) = Trop(M/F^perp plus loops on F^perp).

The proof must be self-contained for a reader who has not seen any prior solver output. Define every convention and symbol inline. Do not refer to 'the previous argument', 'the problem above', or any attached text. You may cite standard valuated-matroid facts, but state them precisely before using them.

Required structure and details:

1. Affine conventions. Work first with affine tropical cones in T^E, where T = R union {infinity}, including the all-infinity vector. Use the convention that a tropical minimum condition means there is no unique finite minimizer. Explain how projective tropical spaces are obtained by deleting the all-infinity vector and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. Explicitly handle rank-zero and full-rank boundary cases.

2. Standard facts. State the following as standard facts: containment Trop(p) subset Trop(q) is equivalent to p being a valuated matroid quotient of q; for rank k p, each coordinate cocircuit D_A(e)=p_{A union e} for |A|=k-1 lies in the affine Trop(p); ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality says u in Trop(mu^*) iff Trop(mu) is contained in the tropical hyperplane with coefficients u; a tropical linear space determines its valuated Pluecker vector up to global tropical scalar.

3. Simplification. Let S = si(M). Define the diagonal map Delta from T^S to T^E by repeating coordinates on original non-loop parallel classes and putting infinity on original loops. Prove that Delta induces a poset isomorphism Dr(S) -> Dr(M). The proof must use coordinate cocircuits: loops remain loops; finite quotient bases are M-independent; original parallel classes cannot be split and have zero internal valuated offset. Prove compatibility with the embedded flat subposet: for a flat F of M corresponding to a flat Fbar of S, M/F plus loops on F is the zero-parallel extension of S/Fbar plus loops on Fbar. Mention the all-loop case separately if needed.

4. Modularity from the flat-lattice involution. Show that the induced anti-involution on L(S) makes L(S) anti-isomorphic to its opposite, hence L(S) is both semimodular and lower semimodular, so S is modular. Do not use component classification.

5. Simple modular case. Assume now M is simple, loopless, rank r, modular, and has a flat-lattice polarity F -> F^perp. Let p be a rank k valuated quotient of M. Prove flat constancy: p_I is infinity on M-dependent k-subsets, and for independent k-subsets I,J with cl_M(I)=cl_M(J), one has p_I=p_J. Define pbar(X) for rank-k flats X. Then define p^dagger on (r-k)-subsets J by p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) if J is M-independent, and infinity otherwise.

6. Prove p^dagger is a valuated matroid quotient of M. Include explicit lemmas:
   a. Local dual Pluecker lemma in a modular interval. For four hyperplanes H1,H2,H3,H4 in a rank k+2 flat Y, with [ij]=pbar(Hi meet Hj) for distinct hyperplanes and [ij]=infinity when the hyperplanes repeat or the meet has wrong rank, the three opposite sums [12]+[34], [13]+[24], [14]+[23] have no unique finite minimum. Prove this by considering the rank of the total meet; in the codimension-three case, reduce to a rank-three edge lemma.
   b. Rank-three edge lemma. State and prove it using the superlevel-flat characterization of the Bergman fan of a simple rank-three modular matroid. A concise proof by decomposing a tropical point into indicators of a nested point-line flag is acceptable, but it must cover infinite weights and repeated/degenerate configurations.
   c. Quotient-incidence for p^dagger. For every circuit C of M and every set A of size r-k-1, prove the minimum of p^dagger_{A union c} over c in C\A has no unique finite minimizer. Use the codimension-one polarity lemma: in the contraction M/cl(A), the vector induced by H |-> pbar(H^perp) has line-closed superlevel sets, hence lies in the affine tropical linear space of the contraction. Explain loops and parallel classes in the contraction.

7. Prove involutivity and flat compatibility in the simple modular case. Show (p^dagger)^dagger=p. For the flat quotient p^F corresponding to M/F plus loops on F, prove (p^F)^dagger=p^{F^perp}. Use modular complements: finite bases of M/F correspond to lattice complements of F, and polarity sends complements of F to complements of F^perp.

8. Point-annihilator theorem and order reversal. For x in the affine Trop(M), define its polar hyperplane H_x inside affine Trop(M) by the basis equations: for every M-basis B={b1,...,br}, set b_i^* = cl_M(B\{b_i})^perp, and require min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}) to have no unique finite minimizer. Prove the theorem: x in Trop(p^dagger) iff Trop(p) is contained in H_x. Use Boolean apartments: prove cl({b_i : i in S})^perp = cl({b_i^* : i notin S}), so B^* is a basis and p^dagger restricted to B^* is the ordinary valuated dual of p restricted to B. In the converse direction, do not assume arbitrary points of Trop(p restricted to B) extend globally; instead use global coordinate cocircuits of p, which lie in Trop(p), to verify the ordinary dual equations on every apartment.

9. Define Phi in the simple modular case intrinsically by Phi(X) = { projective x in Trop(M) : X is contained in H_x }. Then, if X=Trop(p), the point-annihilator theorem gives Phi(X)=Trop(p^dagger). Deduce Phi is well-defined, inclusion-reversing, involutive, and extends F -> F^perp on flat quotients.

10. Return to the original nonsimple matroid. Define Phi_M = Delta_* o Phi_S o Delta_*^{-1}. Use the simplification compatibility from step 3 to prove it extends the original flat-lattice involution.

Write the proof in a polished mathematical style. It is acceptable to label the technical local lemmas and prove them succinctly, but do not omit them entirely. Avoid relying on Desarguesian projective geometry or component classification.

task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are the final red-team referee before the proof is written up. Read the attached outputs carefully, especially t16, t14, t12, t11, and t15. Your job is to attack the claimed theorem: for every finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, the relative Dressian Dr(M), ordered by inclusion, admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending F -> F^perp on the embedded flat subposet.

Focus on possible hidden gaps in: (1) the simplification isomorphism Dr(si(M)) -> Dr(M), including loops, parallel classes, zero valuated offsets, and compatibility with flat quotients; (2) the assertion that the simplification is modular; (3) the flat-coordinate dagger construction p -> p^dagger in the simple modular case, including dependent subsets, repeated hyperplanes, rank 0 and full-rank cases; (4) the proof that p^dagger is a valuated quotient, especially the local four-hyperplane Plucker lemma and codimension-one polarity lemma; (5) the affine point-annihilator theorem and its converse using global coordinate cocircuits; and (6) projectivization from affine cones.

If you find a fatal flaw or counterexample, state it precisely and explain how it breaks the theorem or which weaker theorem survives. If you find only fixable gaps, give exact patches. If you find the proof sound, produce a concise certification checklist with the precise lemmas that must appear in the final write-up. Do not merely summarize; test edge cases aggressively.

## Verdict

**No remaining gap found**, after inserting one explicit lattice-theoretic patch: a finite geometric lattice with an order-reversing involution is modular. With that lemma stated, the prior chain proves the desired theorem for finite matroids. Below is the exact lemma chain I would certify for the final write-up.

---

# 0. Conventions

Work affinely first. For a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\),

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E,\qquad \mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]

includes the all-\(\infty\) vector. Circuit equations use:

> “the minimum is attained at least twice” = “there is no unique finite minimizer.”

Thus an all-\(\infty\) relation is satisfied. For \(d=0\),

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]

Projectivization is recovered at the end by

\[
\Trop(p)=\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]

So rank-zero affine spaces become empty projectively.

---

# 1. Standard facts the final proof may cite

The following are the exact outside facts needed.

### SF1. Containment equals valuated quotient

For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\]

if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\).

In particular, if \(q=M\) is the constant valuated matroid of a matroid \(M\), then \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) iff for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((\operatorname{rk} p-1)\)-set \(A\),

\[
\min_{c\in C} D_A^p(c)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer, where

\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

For rank \(0\), this condition is vacuous and \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\).

---

### SF2. Coordinate cocircuits lie in the tropical linear space

If \(p\) has rank \(k>0\) and \(A\in\binom{E}{k-1}\), then the vector

\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A
\end{cases}
\]

lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

This is just the tropical Plücker relation for \(p\).

---

### SF3. Ordinary affine tropical annihilator duality

For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on a finite set \(N\), define

\[
H_u^N=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]

Then

\[
u\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u^N.
\]

This includes the boundary ranks:

- if \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=0\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\{\infty^N\}\), so the right side holds for every \(u\);
- if \(\operatorname{rk}\mu=|N|\), then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)=\mathbb T^N\), and the right side holds iff \(u=\infty^N\), matching \(\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)\).

---

### SF4. Uniqueness of valuated Plücker coordinates

The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated matroid \(p\) up to addition of a global tropical scalar.

Equivalently, if

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(q),
\]

then \(p=q+\lambda\) for some \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\). Thus defining

\[
\Phi(\Trop(p)):=\Trop(p^\dagger)
\]

is independent of the Plücker representative. Alternatively, the final write-up can avoid this issue by defining \(\Phi\) intrinsically via the annihilator formula.

---

### SF5. Bergman superlevel-flat criterion

For an ordinary matroid \(N\),

\[
w\in \widetilde{\Trop}(N)
\]

iff for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set

\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]

is a flat of \(N\). This is used in the rank-three edge lemma and the codimension-one polarity lemma.

---

# 2. Necessary lattice patch: polarity forces modularity

This should be inserted explicitly.

Let \(L=\mathcal L(M)\) be the finite geometric lattice of flats of \(M\), with rank function \(\rho\). A geometric lattice is semimodular, so

\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]

If \(L\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then

\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]

Apply submodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\). Since

\[
X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp=(X\vee Y)^\perp,\qquad
X^\perp\vee Y^\perp=(X\wedge Y)^\perp,
\]

one obtains the reverse inequality

\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]

Hence equality holds for all \(X,Y\). Therefore \(\mathcal L(M)\), and equivalently \(\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M))\), is modular.

This is the bridge from an arbitrary finite matroid with flat-lattice polarity to the simple modular case.

---

# 3. Simplification isomorphism audit

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set, let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes, and let

\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]

be the simplification. Define

\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]

by

\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

Then:

### 3.1 Zero-parallel extension

If \(\bar q\) is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define \(q=\Delta\bar q\) by

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is a valuated matroid: tropical Plücker relations either project to those for \(\bar q\), have only \(\infty\)-terms, or, when a parallel class is repeated, have exactly two finite equal terms.

Moreover,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Loops force \(\infty\)-coordinates; zero-parallel pairs force equal coordinates; the remaining equations are exactly those of \(\bar q\).

In particular,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

---

### 3.2 Descent of quotients

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid with

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

If \(k=0\), descent is immediate.

Assume \(k>0\). For every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\), hence in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Consequences:

1. **Finite \(q\)-bases are \(M\)-independent.**  
   If a finite \(q\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(D_A^q\) has a unique finite minimum on \(C\), contradiction.

2. **Loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\).**  
   Every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has loop coordinates \(\infty\).

3. **Original parallel classes are not split and have zero valuated offsets.**  
   If \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops in \(M\), then every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Hence

   \[
   D_A^q(e)=D_A^q(f)
   \]

   for all \(A\). Therefore no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\), and replacing \(e\) by \(f\) in a finite basis preserves valuation exactly, not merely up to an offset.

Thus \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(S\), and

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Since \(\Delta\) is injective on simplified coordinates,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Thus \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, zero-parallel extension of any quotient of \(S\) is a quotient of \(M\). Hence

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 3.3 Rank-zero and all-loop cases

Rank-zero quotients descend to rank-zero quotients. Affinely they are the singleton \(\{\infty\}\); projectively they are empty.

If \(M\) is all loops, then \(\Trop(M)\) is empty projectively and \(\Dr(M)\) has only the empty object. The theorem is then trivial.

---

### 3.4 Compatibility with flat quotients

Flats of \(M\) correspond to flats of \(S\) by

\[
F\longmapsto \bar F=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]

For

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]

one has

\[
r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

one gets

\[
N_F=\Delta\bigl(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\bigr).
\]

Therefore the embedded flat subposets are respected by \(\Delta_*\).

So the nonsimple case is fully reduced to the simple modular case.

---

# 4. Simple modular dagger theorem audit

Now assume \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and put \(n=r-k\).

---

### 4.1 Flat constancy

The quotient incidence relations imply:

1. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(|I|=k\), then

   \[
   p_I=\infty.
   \]

2. If \(I,I'\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank-\(k\) flat, then

   \[
   p_I=p_{I'}.
   \]

Thus \(\bar p(F)\) is well-defined for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\):

\[
\bar p(F)=p_I
\]

for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\).

Also, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. This is proved by transporting one finite \(p\)-basis along the \(M\)-basis graph and using quotient incidence at each exchange step.

---

### 4.2 Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

For \(|J|=n=r-k\), define

\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,&J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]

This is nontrivial because a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) spans a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), and any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) gives \(p^\dagger_J<\infty\).

Boundary ranks are immediate:

- if \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid \(M\), up to scalar;
- if \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is rank zero.

---

### 4.3 Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

For \(0<k<r\), the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) reduces to the local four-hyperplane lemma.

Given \(|S|=n-2\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\):

- If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If \(S\) is independent but some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every Plücker matching has an \(\infty\)-term.
- Otherwise set \(Y=X^\perp\), and

  \[
  H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp\le Y.
  \]

Then the Plücker relation becomes the following.

For hyperplanes \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) in a rank \(k+2\) flat \(Y\), define

\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34], [13]+[24], [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer.

The repeated-hyperplane convention is essential and correct: if, say, \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while the other two matching sums are equal.

For distinct \(H_i\), let

\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]

The three possible ranks of \(Z\) give:

1. \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\): all pairwise meets equal \(Z\), so all sums are equal.
2. \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\): choose \(t_i\) in the triple intersections and reduce to an ordinary Plücker relation for \(p\).
3. \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\): pass to the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). The induced weight \(w(P)=\bar p(P)\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\). The rank-three edge lemma, proved by the superlevel-flat criterion and a point-line flag analysis, gives the desired matching relation.

Thus \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

---

### 4.4 Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

One must show that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-set \(A\),

\[
\min_{c\in C}D_A^{p^\dagger}(c)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, this is automatic.

Assume \(A\) is independent, let

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]

For each point \(P\) of the simplification of \(M/X\), define

\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]

The codimension-one polarity lemma proves

\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\operatorname{si}(M/X)).
\]

Its proof is sound:

- superlevel sets \(\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}\) are line-closed;
- line-closed point sets are flats in finite simple modular matroids;
- hence the superlevel-flat criterion applies.

Lifting \(u\) to \(M/X\) gives a vector with value \(\infty\) on loops and equal values on parallel elements, hence an element of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

Finally, \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Therefore a unique finite minimum on \(C\setminus X\) would give a unique finite minimum on some circuit of \(M/X\), contradiction.

So \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 4.5 Involutivity

For \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\):

- if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) and \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\);
- if \(I\) is independent, with \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then

\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]

Thus

\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

---

### 4.6 Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of

\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]

Finite coordinates of \(p^F\) occur exactly on rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}F\) flats complementary to \(F\). Polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore

\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]

with the same normalization.

---

# 5. Affine point-annihilator theorem audit

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) quotient of \(M\). For \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), define \(\widetilde H_x\) by:

\[
y\in\widetilde H_x
\iff
\forall M\text{-bases }B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\quad
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer},
\]

where

\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]

The theorem is:

\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### 5.1 Boolean apartment calculation

For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),

\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i).
\]

Applying polarity gives

\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]

Hence \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

If

\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\]

then

\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

This is the key apartment-level duality.

---

### 5.2 Forward direction

Assume

\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

For any \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) and any \(M\)-basis \(B\),

\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]

By ordinary affine annihilator duality,

\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]

has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\),

\[
y\in\widetilde H_x.
\]

Thus

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

---

### 5.3 Converse direction: global cocircuits are essential

Assume

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). To show

\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\]

check the circuit equations of \(\mu_B^*\).

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let

\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]

The global coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), hence in \(\widetilde H_x\). Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives

\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]

with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is exactly the circuit equation for

\[
x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

This avoids the invalid extension claim “every point of \(\Trop(\mu_B)\) extends to \(\Trop(p)\).” The proof uses only global coordinate cocircuits of \(p\).

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts correctly to every \(M\)-basis.

Now check an arbitrary \(p^\dagger\)-circuit equation indexed by \(J\subseteq E\), \(|J|=r-k+1\):

- If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis and use the restriction already proved.
- If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\).
- If \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and their \(p^\dagger\)-coefficients are constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Therefore

\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

### 5.4 Boundary ranks

If \(k=0\), then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\qquad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

The theorem is immediate.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), while \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Affinely,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]

The only \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfying

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x
\]

is \(x=\infty^E\). This follows by testing coordinate cocircuits of \(M\) on each basis. Projectively, this becomes the assertion that the annihilator is empty.

---

# 6. Final theorem skeleton

For simple modular \(M\), define affinely

\[
\widetilde\Phi(\widetilde{\Trop}(p))
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

Equivalently, intrinsically,

\[
\widetilde\Phi(X)
=
\{x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M): X\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]

The point-annihilator theorem gives equality with \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\).

Then:

- involutivity follows from \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\);
- order reversal follows because if \(X\subseteq Y\), then

  \[
  Y\subseteq \widetilde H_x \implies X\subseteq \widetilde H_x;
  \]

- flat compatibility follows from

  \[
  (p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
  \]

For nonsimple \(M\), use

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_{\operatorname{si}(M)}\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

This is order-reversing, involutive, and sends

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
\]

to

\[
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Projectivization is recovered by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and quotienting by tropical scaling. All constructions commute with scaling, and rank-zero affine objects become empty projective objects.

Thus the original theorem is proved.

---

## Simplification reduction

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let

\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]

be its loop set, let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let

\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]

be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Work first with affine tropical cones \(\widetilde{\Trop}(-)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\), \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Projectivization is obtained by quotienting by \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\). If one deletes the all-\(\infty\) point projectively, the rank-zero spaces below become empty on both sides; the statements remain unchanged.

Define

\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]

by

\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

This commutes with tropical scaling:

\[
\Delta(x+\lambda\mathbf 1)=\Delta(x)+\lambda\mathbf 1,
\]

so it descends to tropical projective space. It is injective, except for the harmless all-loop degenerate case where both sides have only the rank-zero object.

---

### 1. Zero-parallel extension of valuated matroids

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta \bar q\) on \(E\) by

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]

for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\).

This is exactly the valuated matroid obtained from \(\bar q\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each class \(a\in \bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\). Indeed, in a Plücker relation indexed by \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\) and \(B\in \binom{E}{k+1}\), a term can be finite only if both \(A\cup b\) and \(B-b\) avoid loops and meet every original parallel class at most once. If \(B\) has no repeated class, the relation is the projected relation for \(\bar q\). If \(B\) has exactly one repeated class, the only possibly finite terms are the two terms obtained by deleting one or the other copy, and those two terms are equal. All other obstructed cases give only \(\infty\)-terms. Thus \(q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

The loop equations force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\). The two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) whenever \(e,f\) lie in the same original non-loop parallel class. After imposing these equations, the remaining valuated circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(\bar q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

---

### 2. Coordinate cocircuits

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(k>0\) and \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define

\[
C_A^q(e)=
\begin{cases}
q_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\). This is just the tropical Plücker relation for \(q\): for every \(T\in \binom{E}{k+1}\),

\[
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+C_A^q(e)\bigr)
=
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+q_{A\cup e}\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer. If \(C_A^q\) is all \(\infty\), it is harmless; projectively it is simply ignored.

For \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(q\), so the loop-forcing statements below are automatic.

---

### 3. Descent of a quotient of \(M\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

#### Loops remain loops

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(\ell\in L_0\). Every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate \(\infty\), because \(\ell\) is a loop of \(M\).

If \(\ell\) were not a loop of \(q\), there would be a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni \ell\). Put \(A=B-\ell\). Then

\[
C_A^q(\ell)=q_B<\infty.
\]

But \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), contradiction. Hence every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

#### Original parallel classes are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies

\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

Therefore, for every \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\),

\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]

with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

Consequences:

1. No finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\).  
   Indeed, if \(B\) contained both, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
   \]

   while

   \[
   q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
   \]

   contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation.  
   If \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original class, then \(f\notin A\), and

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
   \]

Thus each original non-loop parallel class is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

#### Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set

\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]

This is independent of the choice of representatives by the equality \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\) inside each original parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved classes. Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\):

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Therefore

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Since also

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]

and \(\Delta\) is injective on the non-loop coordinates, the containment

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]

implies

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). The construction is unique because the values \(\bar q_{\bar I}\) are forced by any representative set \(I\).

The rank-zero case is included: a rank-zero valuated matroid has only the empty basis and all elements are loops, so it descends to the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

---

### 4. Converse: zero-parallel extension preserves quotients

Conversely, let \(\bar q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\), so

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Let \(q=\Delta \bar q\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\). Then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

Hence \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Poset isomorphism of relative Dressians

Define

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(M)
\]

by

\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Trop(\Delta \bar q).
\]

The previous two steps show that \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined and surjective.

It is injective and order-reflecting because \(\Delta\) is injective:

\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq \Delta(X_2).
\]

Therefore

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 6. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Hence

\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]

is a canonical lattice isomorphism, with inverse

\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Put

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

on ground set \(E\), and

\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]

on ground set \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

we get

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]

Thus \(N_F\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

So the diagram

\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]

is carried by \(\Delta_*\) to

\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(M).
\]

---

### 7. Transfer of anti-involutions

Suppose

\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M)
\]

is an order-reversing involution. Since \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S)\) is a lattice isomorphism, \(\alpha\) descends to

\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}
:
\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]

This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now suppose one has constructed an order-reversing involution

\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(S)
\]

extending \(\bar\alpha\). Then define

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). By the flat-compatibility proved above, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),

\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/\alpha(F)\oplus U_{0,\alpha(F)}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original anti-involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\).

So the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loops and parallel classes removed.
